name: FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS IN NAVAL ARCHITECTURE

topics.title: Scope of Finite Element Method in Solving Engineering Problems
General Steps in Finite Element Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST753 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS IN NAVAL ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** General Steps in Finite Element Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Chandrupatla & Belegundu / Reddy):**
> The Finite Element Method (FEM) is a numerical procedure for obtaining approximate solutions to the governing differential equations of engineering problems by discretizing the domain into a finite number of simple subdomains (elements), deriving element equations using variational methods or weighted residuals, assembling them under compatibility and equilibrium, and solving the resulting algebraic system for unknown field variables.

In the context of **Naval Architecture**, FEA is the workhorse behind analyzing a ship's hull girder, plate panels, stiffened shells, propeller blades, and offshore structural components under wave, hydrostatic, and impact loads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FEA is NOT an analysis of a single object — it is a systematic procedure (workflow) that converts a physical problem into a solvable linear/non-linear algebraic system.**

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Jigsaw Puzzle of a Ship"

Imagine you have a giant photograph of a ship (the actual complex structure). You cannot solve the stress on every square millimeter of that photograph in one go. So you:

1. **Cut the photograph into a jigsaw puzzle** (discretize the domain into finite elements).
2. **Solve a tiny corner piece** (element-level equations where shape functions are simple).
3. **Glue all pieces back together**, ensuring the picture lines up at the edges (assembly using compatibility).
4. **Look at the whole picture** to understand the ship (solve global system + post-process).

Each jigsaw piece is an **element**, the picture edges that must match are **compatibility / boundary conditions**, and the rules for how the picture is glued together are the **governing equations (equilibrium)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea in One Line:**
> *Divide → Approximate locally → Assemble globally → Solve → Interpret.*

### Why FEA for Naval Architecture?
- **Ship hulls** are complex double-curved shells with cut-outs, brackets, and openings — no closed-form solution exists.
- **Stiffened plate panels** in deck and bottom plating have repeating structural features (ideal for mesh generation).
- **Sloshing, slamming, and hydrostatic pressure** loading can be applied as distributed loads on elements.
- **Material and geometric non-linearities** (e.g., buckling, plastic hinge formation during collision) are tractable with FEA.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Course Outcome (CO) Mapping (KTU 2024):**
> CO1 → *Apply the fundamental concepts of FEA to discretize simple marine structural problems.*
> This topic lays the procedural foundation for CO1, CO2, and CO3 across all subsequent modules.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The FEA workflow is universally described as a **seven-step procedure** (with sub-steps in industry-grade packages like ANSYS, NASTRAN, ABAQUS, and Sesam). Every step is mandatory — skipping or short-cutting any step leads to **erroneous results**.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Problem Definition (Preprocessing — conceptual stage)**
   - State the physics: linear static, modal, buckling, dynamic, thermal, fluid-structure interaction?
   - Identify geometry, material properties ($E$, $\nu$, $\rho$, $\sigma_y$), boundary conditions, loads.
   - *Why?* Because every assumption you commit to here propagates to the answer.

2. **Domain Discretization (Meshing)**
   - Divide the structure (e.g., a ship hull plate) into a finite number of elements.
   - 1-D: bar elements (trusses, stiffeners); 2-D: triangular (T3), quadrilateral (Q4, Q8); 3-D: tetrahedra (Tet4, Tet10), hexahedra (Hex8, Hex20); shells for ship plating.
   - *How?* Nodes (nodal points) define element geometry. Mesh density controls accuracy vs. cost.

3. **Selection of Displacement (Interpolation) Model**
   - Assume the field variable $u(x)$ inside each element is a polynomial of a chosen order:

$$
u(x) \approx \hat{u}(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(x)\, u_i
$$

   - $N_i(x)$ = shape (interpolation) functions; $u_i$ = nodal values (unknowns).
   - Order must satisfy **completeness** (rigid-body modes + constant strain) and **compatibility** ($C^0$ or $C^1$ continuity between elements).

4. **Derivation of Element Stiffness Matrix & Load Vector**
   - From the principle of virtual work or weighted residual (Galerkin) method:

$$
[K^{(e)}] = \int_{V^{(e)}} [B]^T [D] [B]\, dV
$$

   - Element load vector (body force $b$, traction $t$):

$$
\{F^{(e)}\} = \int_{V^{(e)}} [N]^T \{b\}\, dV + \int_{S^{(e)}} [N]^T \{t\}\, dS
$$

   - $[D]$ = material property matrix; $[B]$ = strain-displacement matrix.

5. **Assembly of Global Equations**

$$
[K] \{U\} = \{F\}
$$

   - Built by enforcing **compatibility** (shared nodes have unique DOFs) and **equilibrium** at nodes (sum of element forces = applied load).

6. **Application of Boundary Conditions (Essential / Dirichlet)**
   - Prescribed displacements $U = \bar{U}$ (e.g., fixed support at keel block).
   - Methods: penalty, elimination, or Lagrange multipliers.
   - *Why?* Without BCs, $[K]$ is singular — infinite solutions.

7. **Solution for Unknown Nodal DOFs + Post-Processing**
   - Solve $[K]\{U\} = \{F\}$ using direct (Gauss, Cholesky) or iterative (CG, GMRES) solvers.
   - Recover strains $\{\varepsilon\} = [B]\{U^{(e)}\}$ and stresses $\{\sigma\} = [D]\{\varepsilon\}$.
   - Verify convergence (mesh refinement, $h$-/$p$-refinement).

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-exam tested. Memorize symbol meaning + units.**

| Quantity | Symbol / Formula | Units (SI) | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Displacement approximation | $\hat{u} = \sum N_i u_i$ | m | Field variable inside element |
| Strain-displacement | $\{\varepsilon\} = [B]\{u\}$ | — | Relates geometry to deformation |
| Constitutive law | $\{\sigma\} = [D]\{\varepsilon\}$ | Pa | Material response (Hookean) |
| Element stiffness | $[K^{(e)}] = \int [B]^T[D][B]\, dV$ | N/m | Element resistance to nodal load |
| Element load vector | $\{F^{(e)}\} = \int [N]^T\{b\}\, dV + \int [N]^T\{t\}\, dS$ | N | Distributed loads mapped to nodes |
| Global system | $[K]\{U\} = \{F\}$ | — | Assembled equilibrium |
| Stress recovery | $\{\sigma^{(e)}\} = [D][B]\{U^{(e)}\}$ | Pa | Post-processed result |
| Convergence test | $\vert \sigma_{h/2} - \sigma_h \vert / \sigma_h \leq \text{tol}$ | — | $h$-refinement check |

**Real-world utility:** These equations power every shipyard's rule check — from **DNV GL**, **Bureau Veritas**, to **ABS** rule scantlings — implemented inside FEM solvers to certify hull girder, plate, and stiffener strength against wave and cargo loads.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a derivation here?** Module 1 of KTU frequently asks: *"Show how the global stiffness matrix is assembled from element matrices"* or *"Derive the stiffness matrix for a 2-noded bar/truss element"*. The derivation below is the standard template.

### Analytical Derivation: 1-D Bar Element (Spring Stiffness)

Consider a prismatic bar of cross-section $A$, modulus $E$, length $L$, with nodes $1$ (left) and $2$ (right). Axial displacement $u(x)$.

**Step 1 — Displacement approximation (linear shape functions):**

$$
u(x) = N_1 u_1 + N_2 u_2
$$

$$
N_1(x) = 1 - \frac{x}{L}, \qquad N_2(x) = \frac{x}{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Strain-displacement (axial strain):**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{du}{dx} = \frac{dN_1}{dx} u_1 + \frac{dN_2}{dx} u_2 = -\frac{1}{L}u_1 + \frac{1}{L}u_2
$$

$$
\Rightarrow [B] = \frac{1}{L}\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Constitutive relation (uniaxial Hooke's law):**

$$
\sigma = E\,\varepsilon
$$

$$
\Rightarrow [D] = [E]
$$

**Step 4 — Element stiffness by virtual work:**

$$
[K^{(e)}] = \int_0^L [B]^T [D] [B]\, A\, dx
$$

Substituting $[B]$, $[D]$, and noting they are constant:

$$
[K^{(e)}] = \int_0^L \frac{1}{L}\begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} [E]\, \frac{1}{L}\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} A\, dx
$$

$$
= \frac{AE}{L^2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\int_0^L dx
$$

$$
\Rightarrow [K^{(e)}] = \frac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Element load vector (distributed axial body load $b$ per unit length):**

$$
\{F^{(e)}\} = \int_0^L [N]^T b\, A\, dx = \int_0^L \begin{bmatrix} N_1 \\ N_2 \end{bmatrix} bA\, dx
$$

For constant $b$:

$$
\{F^{(e)}\} = \frac{bAL}{2}\begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $1/2$ arises because the load is split equally between the two nodes — a property of linear shape functions (lumped equivalent).

### Analytical Matrix — Why Each Step Matters

| Step | Mathematical Output | Physical Meaning | Naval Arch. Analogue |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shape functions $N_i$ | Polynomial map $x \to u$ | Assumed deformation field | Linear bending of a stiffener segment |
| Strain-displacement $[B]$ | $[B] = \partial [N]/\partial x$ | Geometric operator | Curvature $\kappa$ of hull plate |
| Material matrix $[D]$ | Hooke's law matrix | Material stiffness | Mild steel $E = 2.06 \times 10^{11}$ Pa |
| Stiffness $[K^{(e)}]$ | Energy-equivalent resistance | Element resisting nodal forces | Spring constant of a frame member |
| Assembly $[K]$ | Sum over shared nodes | Global equilibrium of hull | Whole ship equilibrium under wave bending |
| Boundary conditions | Fix prescribed DOFs | Constraint (e.g., supported hull) | Build-up block / dry-dock support |
| Solve + post-process | $\{U\}, \{\sigma\}$ | Nodal response | Hull girder bending stress distribution |

### Convergence of FEA — Mathematical Statement

A properly posed FEA solution converges to the exact solution as the element size $h \to 0$:

$$
\lim_{h \to 0} \vert u^h - u^{exact} \vert_{E} = 0
$$

where $\vert \cdot \vert_E$ is the **energy norm**. The **a-priori error estimate** for linear elements in 1-D Poisson problems:

$$
\vert u^h - u^{exact} \vert_{E} \leq C \cdot h \cdot \vert u'' \vert
$$

with $C$ = constant independent of mesh. This is why engineers use **$h$-refinement** (smaller elements) or **$p$-refinement** (higher-order polynomials) — a topic revisited in Module 4.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram note:** A pure physical drawing of meshing a ship hull is impractical in Mermaid. Instead, the architecture-level block diagram below maps every FEA stage to its data, software counterpart, and naval-architecture deliverable — exactly as practiced in shipyards using **Sesam (DNV)**, **Maestro / Maxsurf**, and **ANSYS Aqwa**.

### Mermaid Block Diagram — FEA Workflow for Naval Structures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Step 0 Problem Definition] --> A1[Step 1 Geometry and Material]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2 Domain Discretization Meshing]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3 Displacement Model Shape Functions]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4 Derive Element K and F]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5 Global Assembly]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6 Apply Boundary Conditions]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7 Solve and Post Process]
    A7 --> A8{Convergence Achieved}
    A8 -- No --> A9[Refine Mesh or Increase Polynomial Order]
    A9 --> A2
    A8 -- Yes --> A10[Validate against Rules DNV GL ABS LR]
    A10 --> A11[Final Hull Scantlings and Reports]

    classDef stepStyle fill:#E6F2FF,stroke:#003366,color:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decisionStyle fill:#FFE6E6,stroke:#990000,color:#990000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef outputStyle fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#006600,color:#006600,stroke-width:2px
    class A0,A1,A2,A3,A4,A5,A6,A7,A11 stepStyle
    class A8,A9 decisionStyle
    class A10,A11 outputStyle
```

### Mermaid Diagram — Data and Software Layer Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G[Geometry from Hull Lines IGS or STEP] --> M[Meshing Q4 Q8 T3 Tet4 Hex8]
    M --> P[Pre Processor ANSYS APDL]
    P --> S[Solver Sparse Direct or Iterative]
    S --> R[Post Processor Stress Contour Plot]
    R --> V[Verification against DNV or ABS Rules]
    V --> O[Class Approval and Construction]

    classDef softStyle fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#CC6600,color:#663300,stroke-width:2px
    classDef dataStyle fill:#E6F2FF,stroke:#003366,color:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ruleStyle fill:#FFE6F2,stroke:#660033,color:#660033,stroke-width:2px
    class G,M dataStyle
    class P,S,R softStyle
    class V,O ruleStyle
```

### Element Hierarchy Reference (KTU-Relevant)

| Element Family | Topology | Typical Naval Use |
|---|---|---|
| Bar / Truss (L2) | 1-D, 2 nodes | Stiffener web modeling, bracing |
| Beam (Euler-Bernoulli) | 1-D, 2 nodes | Frames, girders, pillars |
| Plane stress CST (T3) | 2-D, 3 nodes | Initial plate analysis, transition zones |
| Plane stress Q4 / Q8 | 2-D, 4 / 8 nodes | Hull plate panels, bulkheads |
| Shell (S4R / S8R) | 3-D curved | Ship hull plating, decks, superstructure |
| Solid (Hex8 / Tet4) | 3-D | Local details — bracket, hatch corner, foundation |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
> **List any three general steps in the finite element analysis procedure.**

**Model Answer:**
The general steps of FEA are:

1. **Discretization of the domain** — dividing the structure (e.g., a hull plate) into a finite number of elements connected at nodes.
2. **Selection of interpolation (displacement) model** — assuming the field variable varies inside each element as a polynomial expressed through shape functions.
3. **Derivation of element stiffness matrix and load vector** — using the principle of virtual work or Galerkin method to obtain $[K^{(e)}]$ and $\{F^{(e)}\}$.

> Other acceptable steps: assembly of global equations, application of boundary conditions, solution for nodal unknowns, and post-processing for stresses/strains.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
> **Why is it mandatory to apply boundary conditions before solving $[K]\{U\} = \{F\}$? What happens if they are not applied?**

**Model Answer:**
Boundary conditions (essential / Dirichlet conditions) constrain prescribed nodal degrees of freedom to known values (e.g., zero displacement at a fixed support). Without applying them:

- The global stiffness matrix $[K]$ remains **singular** (determinant $= 0$).
- The system $[K]\{U\} = \{F\}$ has **either no solution or infinitely many solutions** because the structure is unconstrained — pure rigid-body modes are not removed.
- The physical interpretation is that the body can translate or rotate freely without resistance.

In naval architecture, neglecting boundary conditions would correspond to analyzing a hull without a build-up block — the structure would float freely in the analysis space, producing zero meaningful stress.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Module 1 Internal Choice, KTU 2024 Pattern]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — Derivation & Assembly

**(a)** *Derive the stiffness matrix for a 1-D linear bar element of length $L$, cross-section area $A$, and Young's modulus $E$ using the displacement method. Show all intermediate steps. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *For the two-bar assembly shown (bar 1: $A_1 = 600\,\text{mm}^2$, $L_1 = 1\,\text{m}$, bar 2: $A_2 = 400\,\text{mm}^2$, $L_2 = 1.5\,\text{m}$, $E = 2 \times 10^5\,\text{N/mm}^2$, axial load $P = 10\,\text{kN}$ at the free end), assemble the global stiffness matrix and find the nodal displacements. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q3(a) — Bar Stiffness Derivation

**Step 1 — Displacement field** (linear interpolation between nodes 1 and 2):

$$
u(x) = \left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right) u_1 + \frac{x}{L}\, u_2
$$

$$
\Rightarrow [N(x)] = \begin{bmatrix} 1 - x/L & x/L \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Strain**:

$$
\varepsilon(x) = \frac{du}{dx} = -\frac{u_1}{L} + \frac{u_2}{L}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow [B] = \frac{1}{L}\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Constitutive**: $[D] = [E]$

**Step 4 — Element stiffness via principle of minimum potential energy**:

$$
[K^{(e)}] = \int_0^L [B]^T [D] [B]\, A\, dx = \frac{AE}{L^2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\int_0^L dx
$$

$$
\boxed{[K^{(e)}] = \frac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** $AE/L$ is the axial stiffness of the bar — directly analogous to a spring constant $k$.

---

#### Model Solution to Q3(b) — Assembly & Solution

**Step 1 — Individual stiffness matrices**

For bar 1:

$$
k_1 = \frac{A_1 E}{L_1} = \frac{600 \times 2 \times 10^5}{1000} = 1.2 \times 10^5\,\text{N/mm}
$$

$$
[K^{(1)}] = \begin{bmatrix} k_1 & -k_1 \\ -k_1 & k_1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

For bar 2:

$$
k_2 = \frac{A_2 E}{L_2} = \frac{400 \times 2 \times 10^5}{1500} = 5.33 \times 10^4\,\text{N/mm}
$$

$$
[K^{(2)}] = \begin{bmatrix} k_2 & -k_2 \\ -k_2 & k_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Global assembly** (assuming both bars share node 2):

$$
[K] = \begin{bmatrix} k_1 & -k_1 & 0 \\ -k_1 & k_1 + k_2 & -k_2 \\ 0 & -k_2 & k_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Numerically:

$$
[K] = \begin{bmatrix} 120{,}000 & -120{,}000 & 0 \\ -120{,}000 & 173{,}333 & -53{,}333 \\ 0 & -53{,}333 & 53{,}333 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{N/mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Boundary condition** $u_1 = 0$ (fixed at node 1):

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 173{,}333 & -53{,}333 \\ -53{,}333 & 53{,}333 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_2 \\ u_3 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 10{,}000 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve** (e.g., Gaussian elimination or matrix inversion):

$$
u_2 = 0.115\,\text{mm},\quad u_3 = 0.302\,\text{mm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marks split for KTU valuation:** Derivation (a) → 4 marks for $[B]$, 2 marks for $[K^{(e)}]$, 1 mark for physical meaning. Assembly (b) → 2 marks individual $k$, 2 marks assembly, 2 marks BC application, 1 mark final answer.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternate Choice (Conceptual + Numerical)

**(a)** *Explain with a neat sketch the seven general steps of the finite element analysis procedure as applied to a ship hull plate analysis. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A simply supported beam of length $L = 4\,\text{m}$, flexural rigidity $EI = 2 \times 10^4\,\text{N·m}^2$, is loaded with a uniformly distributed load $w = 5\,\text{kN/m}$. Model it with two beam elements of equal length and obtain the nodal displacements using FEA. Compare with the exact tip deflection. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution Outline to Q3B

**(a)** Steps: (1) Problem definition, (2) Domain discretization (mesh of plate into Q4 elements), (3) Displacement model (Hermitian shape functions for beam / bilinear for plate), (4) Derive $[K^{(e)}]$ and $\{F^{(e)}\}$ using energy method, (5) Assemble global $[K]$ and $\{F\}$, (6) Apply boundary conditions (simply supported → $v=0$ at both ends), (7) Solve and post-process stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, $\tau_{xy}$ — sketch the mesh, deformed shape, and stress contour.

**(b)** For a 2-element beam model with 3 nodes:

$$
[K^{(e)}] = \frac{EI}{L_e^3}\begin{bmatrix} 12 & 6L_e & -12 & 6L_e \\ 6L_e & 4L_e^2 & -6L_e & 2L_e^2 \\ -12 & -6L_e & 12 & -6L_e \\ 6L_e & 2L_e^2 & -6L_e & 4L_e^2 \end{bmatrix},\quad L_e = 2\,\text{m}
$$

Equivalent nodal load vector for UDL on each element:

$$
\{F^{(e)}\} = \frac{wL_e}{2}\begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ L_e/6 \\ 1 \\ -L_e/6 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

Solving with $v_1 = v_3 = 0$ (simple supports) yields the **mid-span deflection** $v_2$, which is then compared to the exact analytical solution:

$$
v_{exact} = \frac{5 w L^4}{384\,EI}
$$

**Typical numerical answer:** $v_{FEA} \approx 0.0208\,\text{m}$, $v_{exact} = 0.0208\,\text{m}$ (close agreement; minor error due to coarse mesh).

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in FEA derivation problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the cross-section area $A$** in the stiffness integration — the formula $[K^{(e)}] = \int [B]^T [D][B]\, dV$ requires $dV$, which for a bar is $A\,dx$. Writing $dx$ alone is the most common error.
> 2. **Sign errors in $[B]$** — axial strain is $\partial u/\partial x$, and signs of $[B]$ must be consistent across all elements in the assembly.
> 3. **Singular matrix after assembly** — means boundary conditions are missing. Always reduce the system using $u_{\text{prescribed}}$ BEFORE solving.
> 4. **Wrong units** — KTU expects SI throughout ($N$, $m$, $Pa$). Mixing mm and N is a guaranteed 0.5-mark deduction.
> 5. **Skipping the physical interpretation** — the closing statement *"This represents the bar's resistance to axial deformation, analogous to a spring constant $AE/L$"* earns 1 bonus mark per KTU scheme.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **FEA is a 7-step procedure:** Define → Discretize → Interpolate → Derive $[K^{(e)}, F^{(e)}]$ → Assemble → Apply BCs → Solve & post-process.
- [x] **The displacement method** is the standard approach taught in KTU Module 1.
- [x] **Shape functions** must be **complete** (polynomial up to linear terms minimum) and **compatible** ($C^0$ continuity).
- [x] **Element stiffness** $[K^{(e)}] = \int_{V^{(e)}} [B]^T [D][B]\, dV$ is derived via **minimum potential energy**.
- [x] **Element load vector** = body force contribution + traction contribution.
- [x] **Assembly** uses **direct stiffness method**: sum contributions of all elements sharing a node.
- [x] **$[K]$ is singular without BCs** — always apply essential conditions before solving.
- [x] **Convergence** is achieved by mesh refinement ($h \to 0$) or polynomial enrichment ($p$-refinement).
- [x] **Naval applications:** Hull plating (shell elements), stiffeners (beam elements), brackets/fillets (solid elements).
- [x] **Key results to memorize:**
  - 1-D bar: $k = AE/L$
  - 1-D beam: $k_{ij}$ entries involve $12, 6L, 4L^2, 2L^2$ factors
  - 2-D CST: $[K^{(e)}] = A_e [B]^T[D][B]$ with thickness $t$
- [x] **Common FEA software in naval architecture:** ANSYS, ABAQUS, Sesam (DNV), NASTRAN, LS-DYNA (impact), Maxsurf (initial sizing).
- [x] **CO Mapping:** This topic builds the procedural foundation for CO1 (apply FEM to marine problems) and is revisited in Modules 2 (1-D elements), 3 (2-D elements), and 5 (applications to ship structures).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variational Formulations and Weighted Residual Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST753 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS IN NAVAL ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Variational Formulations and Weighted Residual Methods # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Variational Formulation A **Variational Formulation** is a mathematical restatement of a boundary value problem (differential equation + boundary conditions) in the form of finding a function that **extremizes (minimizes or maximizes) a functional** — an integral expression that depends on the unknown function and its derivatives. In Naval Architecture and structural mechanics, the most common variational principle is the **Principle of Minimum Potential Energy (PMPE)**, which states that a structure in stable equilibrium attains a configuration that minimizes the total potential energy functional. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Variational methods form the mathematical backbone of the **Finite Element Method (FEM)**. Without the variational (weak) form, the finite element discretization cannot be derived in a systematic, energy-consistent manner. ## 1.2 Weighted Residual Method (WRM) The **Weighted Residual Method** is a systematic technique used to obtain approximate solutions to differential equations. It does **not** require the existence of a variational principle. The method forces the residual (the error obtained when an approximate solution is substituted into the governing differential equation) to be orthogonal to a set of chosen **weighting (or test) functions**. The general WRM statement is: $$\int_{\Omega} W_i \cdot R \, d\Omega = 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$ where $R$ is the residual and $W_i$ are the weight functions. > [!NOTE] > The **Galerkin Method** is the most important special case of WRM, and is the primary WRM used in finite element analysis. When the operator is self-adjoint and positive-definite, the Galerkin method is **equivalent to the Rayleigh-Ritz variational approach**. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — A Ball on a Hilly Terrain:** > > Imagine rolling a ball on a hilly landscape. The ball **always settles at the lowest possible point** in any valley. To find this equilibrium point, you could either: > 1. **(Strong Form / Differential Form):** Solve the differential equation of motion $F = m\ddot{x}$ subject to equilibrium conditions (the **forces** balance). > 2. **(Weak Form / Variational Form):** Find the point where the **total potential energy is minimum** — i.e., find the shape of the hill as a function of position, and locate the bottom of the valley. > > Both methods give the same answer, but the variational approach is **computationally more convenient** because it requires only first derivatives (weaker continuity) and naturally accommodates approximate trial solutions — exactly what FEM exploits. ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Engineering Metrics in Naval Architecture - **Young's Modulus** for marine steel: **E ≈ 200 GPa** - **Poisson's ratio** for marine steel: **ν ≈ 0.3** - **Density of seawater**: **ρ ≈ 1025 kg/m³** - **Yield strength** of mild steel (ship hull): **σ_y ≈ 235 MPa** (Grade A) > [!IMPORTANT] > In the context of **Naval Architecture**, variational and weighted residual methods are routinely used to analyze **hull plate bending**, **stiffened panel strength**, **ship structural vibration**, and **wave-induced loading on ship structures**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Strong Form vs. The Weak Form A boundary value problem is typically given in the **strong form**: $$L[u] = f \quad \text{in } \Omega, \quad B[u] = g \quad \text{on } \Gamma$$ where $L[\cdot]$ is a differential operator, $B[\cdot]$ is a boundary operator, $\Omega$ is the domain, and $\Gamma$ is the boundary. The **weak (variational) form** is obtained by: 1. Multiplying the strong form by a test function $v$ (which vanishes on essential boundaries). 2. Integrating over the domain $\Omega$. 3. Applying **Green's theorem (integration by parts)** to reduce the order of derivatives on $u$. This relaxation of differentiability requirements is the origin of the term "weak form." ## 2.2 The Rayleigh-Ritz Method (Variational Approach) The Rayleigh-Ritz method is the classical variational technique: - **Step 1:** Assume an approximate solution as a linear combination of trial functions: $$u_h(x) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j \, \phi_j(x)$$ - **Step 2:** Substitute into a functional $\Pi[u]$ (e.g., total potential energy). - **Step 3:** Set $\dfrac{\partial \Pi}{\partial c_j} = 0$ for all $j$ to find the coefficients $c_j$. > [!NOTE] > The Rayleigh-Ritz method **requires** a variational functional to exist. It cannot be applied to non-self-adjoint problems directly. ## 2.3 Weighted Residual Methods — The Five Major Variants The Weighted Residual Method (WRM) expresses the residual as: $$R(x) = L[u_h] - f$$ and enforces: $$\int_{\Omega} W_i(x) \, R(x) \, d\Omega = 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$ | **Method** | **Weight Function Choice** | **Best Suited For** | **Remarks** | |---|---|---|---| | **Galerkin** | $W_i = \phi_i$ (same as trial functions) | Self-adjoint & non-self-adjoint | Most widely used in FEM | | **Least Squares** | $W_i = \dfrac{\partial R}{\partial c_i}$ | Any linear/non-linear PDE | Produces symmetric matrix | | **Collocation** | $W_i = \delta(x - x_i)$ (Dirac delta) | Boundary-layer problems | Reduces to point-satisfaction | | **Subdomain** | $W_i = 1$ on sub-domain $\Omega_i$ | Piecewise analysis | Crude, rarely used in FEM | | **Method of Moments** | $W_i$ = orthogonal polynomials | Engineering applications | Used in electromagnetics | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Description** | **Application in Naval Arch.** | |---|---|---|---| | Strong Form | $L[u] = f$ in $\Omega$ | Governing PDE | Hull plate bending equation | | Residual | $R = L[u_h] - f$ | Error from approximate solution | Measure of approximation quality | | WRM Statement | $\int_{\Omega} W_i R \, d\Omega = 0$ | Orthogonality condition | Basis of Galerkin FEM | | Galerkin Weights | $W_i = \phi_i$ | Same as trial functions | Standard FEM choice | | Weak Form (1D rod) | $\int_0^L EA \dfrac{du}{dx}\dfrac{dv}{dx}dx = \int_0^L qv \, dx$ | After integration by parts | Axial stiffness of stiffeners | | Plate Bending (Kirchhoff) | $\int D \nabla^2 w \nabla^2 v \, dA = \int q v \, dA$ | Bilinear form for ship plates | Hull plate analysis | | Total Potential Energy | $\Pi = U - W$ | $U$ = strain energy, $W$ = external work | Minimum gives equilibrium | | Strain Energy (1D bar) | $U = \dfrac{1}{2}\int_0^L EA \left(\dfrac{du}{dx}\right)^2 dx$ | Stored elastic energy | Bar/rod stiffness analysis | | External Work (1D) | $W = \int_0^L qu \, dx + P \cdot u(L)$ | Work done by loads | Distributed + point loads | | Green's Theorem (1D) | $\int u' v \, dx = \left[uv\right]_0^L - \int u v' \, dx$ | Integration by parts | Derives weak from strong form | | Self-Adjoint Operator | $\langle Lu, v \rangle = \langle u, Lv \rangle$ | Equivalence Galerkin = Ritz | Confirms FEM consistency | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on Pipe Symbols:** Absolute value bars in the formulas above have been written using the standard math notation $\vert \cdot \vert$ to preserve the integrity of the markdown table. Do not confuse with table syntax. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility In **Naval Architecture**, variational and weighted residual methods are the cornerstone of: - **Hull-girder vibration analysis** (natural frequencies and mode shapes). - **Stiffened panel ultimate strength** assessment under combined bending + hydrostatic pressure. - **Plate buckling analysis** of ship bottom and side shells. - **Wave diffraction and radiation** problems using boundary element methods (BEM) — closely related to WRM. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Strong Form to Weak Form (1D Bar Problem) > **Problem Statement (Strong Form):** > > A uniform bar of cross-sectional area $A$, Young's modulus $E$, length $L$, subjected to a distributed axial load $q(x)$, fixed at $x=0$ and free at $x=L$: $$\frac{d}{dx}\left(EA\frac{du}{dx}\right) + q(x) = 0, \quad 0 \le x \le L$$ with boundary conditions: - **Essential (Dirichlet):** $u(0) = 0$ - **Natural (Neumann):** $EA\dfrac{du}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = 0$ --- ### Step 1 — Multiply by a Test Function Choose a test function $v(x)$ that satisfies $v(0) = 0$ (zero on the essential boundary). Multiply the strong form by $v(x)$: $$v(x) \cdot \left[\frac{d}{dx}\left(EA\frac{du}{dx}\right) + q(x)\right] = 0$$ ### Step 2 — Integrate Over the Domain $$\int_0^L v(x) \left[\frac{d}{dx}\left(EA\frac{du}{dx}\right) + q(x)\right] dx = 0$$ ### Step 3 — Split the Integral $$\int_0^L v \frac{d}{dx}\left(EA\frac{du}{dx}\right) dx + \int_0^L v \, q(x) \, dx = 0$$ ### Step 4 — Apply Integration by Parts (Green's Theorem in 1D) Recall: $\int_a^b u' v \, dx = \left[uv\right]_a^b - \int_a^b u v' \, dx$ Applying to the first term with $u' \to EA\dfrac{du}{dx}$ and $v \to v$: $$\int_0^L v \frac{d}{dx}\left(EA\frac{du}{dx}\right) dx = \left[v \cdot EA \frac{du}{dx}\right]_0^L - \int_0^L EA \frac{du}{dx} \frac{dv}{dx} \, dx$$ ### Step 5 — Apply Boundary Conditions At $x = 0$: $v(0) = 0$ by choice, so the lower bound vanishes. At $x = L$: $EA\dfrac{du}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = 0$ (natural BC), so the upper bound vanishes too. Therefore: $$\int_0^L v \frac{d}{dx}\left(EA\frac{du}{dx}\right) dx = -\int_0^L EA \frac{du}{dx} \frac{dv}{dx} \, dx$$ ### Step 6 — Write the Weak (Variational) Form Substituting back: $$-\int_0^L EA \frac{du}{dx} \frac{dv}{dx} \, dx + \int_0^L v \, q(x) \, dx = 0$$ Or, equivalently: $$\boxed{\int_0^L EA \frac{du}{dx} \frac{dv}{dx} \, dx = \int_0^L v \, q(x) \, dx}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The weak form is **weaker** in differentiability: $u$ now only needs to be $C^0$ continuous (one derivative is moved to $v$), whereas the strong form required $C^1$ continuity. This is precisely why FEM works with simple shape functions (linear, quadratic). --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Galerkin Method Applied to 1D Bar ### Step 1 — Assume an Approximate (Trial) Solution $$u_h(x) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j \phi_j(x)$$ where $\phi_j(x)$ are chosen trial (shape) functions satisfying essential BCs, and $c_j$ are unknown coefficients. ### Step 2 — Form the Residual Substitute $u_h$ into the strong form. For the bar problem: $$R(x) = \frac{d}{dx}\left(EA\frac{du_h}{dx}\right) + q(x)$$ ### Step 3 — Enforce Weighted Orthogonality (Galerkin Choice) In Galerkin's method, the weight functions are the trial functions themselves: $W_i = \phi_i$. The WRM condition is: $$\int_0^L \phi_i(x) \cdot R(x) \, dx = 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$ ### Step 4 — Expand and Simplify $$\int_0^L \phi_i \frac{d}{dx}\left(EA\frac{du_h}{dx}\right) dx + \int_0^L \phi_i \, q(x) \, dx = 0$$ ### Step 5 — Apply Integration by Parts (as derived above) $$-\int_0^L EA \frac{du_h}{dx} \frac{d\phi_i}{dx} \, dx + \int_0^L \phi_i \, q(x) \, dx = 0$$ Substitute $u_h$: $$-\int_0^L EA \left(\sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j \frac{d\phi_j}{dx}\right) \frac{d\phi_i}{dx} \, dx + \int_0^L \phi_i \, q(x) \, dx = 0$$ ### Step 6 — Rearrange into the Canonical Matrix Form $$\sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j \underbrace{\int_0^L EA \frac{d\phi_i}{dx} \frac{d\phi_j}{dx} \, dx}_{K_{ij}} = \underbrace{\int_0^L \phi_i \, q(x) \, dx}_{F_i}$$ In matrix notation: $$\boxed{[K]\{c\} = \{F\}}$$ where: - $K_{ij} = \int_0^L EA \dfrac{d\phi_i}{dx} \dfrac{d\phi_j}{dx} \, dx$ — the **stiffness matrix** entry. - $F_i = \int_0^L \phi_i q(x) \, dx$ — the **load vector** entry. > [!NOTE] > This is the **fundamental equation of FEM**. Every finite element code — whether used for ship hulls, offshore platforms, or aerospace structures — solves, at its core, an equation of this exact form. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | **Aspect** | **Rayleigh-Ritz (Variational)** | **Galerkin (WRM)** | **Collocation (WRM)** | |---|---|---|---| | Mathematical basis | Variational principle | Orthogonal residual | Residual = 0 at selected points | | Requires functional? | **Yes** | No | No | | Operator requirement | Self-adjoint, positive definite | Works for non-self-adjoint too | Works for any PDE | | Matrix symmetry | Symmetric (if self-adjoint) | Symmetric if operator is self-adjoint | Generally non-symmetric | | Numerical effort | Low–Moderate | Moderate | Low | | Accuracy | High | High | Moderate (depends on point choice) | | Connection to FEM | Direct (energy minimization) | Direct (weak form) | Indirect (used in BEM) | | Use in Naval Arch. | Plate & shell energy analysis | Standard FEM in ship structural codes | Wave diffraction BEM | # 4.
