KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Define the Weighted Residual Method. Write the general statement of WRM and explain the meaning of the residual.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The Weighted Residual Method (WRM) is a class of approximation techniques used to obtain an approximate solution to a differential equation. The general statement is: $$\int_{\Omega} W_i(x) \, R(x) \, d\Omega = 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$ where $R(x) = L[u_h(x)] - f(x)$ is the **residual** (the error introduced when the approximate solution $u_h$ is substituted into the differential equation $L[u] = f$), $W_i$ are the **weight (test) functions**, and $n$ is the number of unknown coefficients in the assumed trial solution. The method forces the residual to be orthogonal to a chosen set of weight functions in the integral sense, yielding $n$ algebraic equations for the $n$ unknown coefficients. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Distinguish between the Strong Form and Weak Form of a differential equation. Why is the weak form preferred in FEM?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | **Strong Form** | **Weak Form** | |---|---| | Differential equation satisfied pointwise in $\Omega$ | Integral statement satisfied in an average sense | | Requires $C^1$ or higher continuity on $u$ | Requires only $C^0$ continuity on $u$ | | Equivalent to a "force balance" | Equivalent to an "energy balance" | | Cannot directly accept piecewise approximations | Naturally accepts piecewise (element-wise) approximations | The weak form is preferred in FEM because (i) it reduces the continuity requirement, enabling the use of simple polynomial shape functions; (ii) it converts differential equations into algebraic equations via integration, and (iii) it directly yields symmetric positive-definite matrices for self-adjoint problems. --- ## PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Answer any **one full question** from the choice provided. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. --- ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Derive the weak form of the following differential equation representing a uniform bar fixed at $x=0$ and subjected to a uniformly distributed axial load $q$: $$\frac{d^2 u}{dx^2} + \frac{q}{EA} = 0, \quad u(0) = 0, \quad \frac{du}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = 0$$ Assume the trial solution $u_h(x) = c_1 \phi_1(x) + c_2 \phi_2(x)$, where $\phi_1(x) = x$ and $\phi_2(x) = x^2 - \dfrac{x^3}{3L}$. Use the **Galerkin method** to obtain the system of equations $[K]\{c\} = \{F\}$. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution (a):** **Step 1 — Write the residual:** $$R(x) = \frac{d^2 u_h}{dx^2} + \frac{q}{EA}$$ Compute derivatives of the shape functions: $$\frac{d\phi_1}{dx} = 1, \quad \frac{d^2\phi_1}{dx^2} = 0$$ $$\frac{d\phi_2}{dx} = 2x - \frac{x^2}{L}, \quad \frac{d^2\phi_2}{dx^2} = 2 - \frac{2x}{L}$$ Since $u_h = c_1 \phi_1 + c_2 \phi_2$: $$\frac{d^2 u_h}{dx^2} = 0 \cdot c_1 + \left(2 - \frac{2x}{L}\right) c_2$$ **Step 2 — Galerkin WRM equations (after integration by parts):** $$\int_0^L EA \frac{du_h}{dx} \frac{d\phi_i}{dx} \, dx = \int_0^L q \, \phi_i \, dx, \quad i = 1, 2$$ **Step 3 — Compute stiffness matrix entries $K_{ij}$:** $$K_{11} = \int_0^L EA (1)(1) \, dx = EA \cdot L$$ $$K_{12} = \int_0^L EA (1)\left(2x - \frac{x^2}{L}\right) dx = EA \left[x^2 - \frac{x^3}{3L}\right]_0^L = EA \left(L^2 - \frac{L^2}{3}\right) = \frac{2EAL^2}{3}$$ By symmetry of integration: $K_{21} = K_{12} = \dfrac{2EAL^2}{3}$. $$K_{22} = \int_0^L EA \left(2x - \frac{x^2}{L}\right)^2 dx$$ Expand: $$\left(2x - \frac{x^2}{L}\right)^2 = 4x^2 - \frac{4x^3}{L} + \frac{x^4}{L^2}$$ Integrate term by term: $$K_{22} = EA \left[\frac{4x^3}{3} - \frac{x^4}{L} + \frac{x^5}{5L^2}\right]_0^L = EA \left(\frac{4L^3}{3} - L^3 + \frac{L^3}{5}\right) = EA \cdot L^3 \left(\frac{4}{3} - 1 + \frac{1}{5}\right)$$ $$= EA \cdot L^3 \left(\frac{20 - 15 + 3}{15}\right) = \frac{8 \, EA \, L^3}{15}$$ **Step 4 — Compute load vector entries $F_i$:** $$F_1 = \int_0^L q \cdot \phi_1 \, dx = \int_0^L q x \, dx = \frac{qL^2}{2}$$ $$F_2 = \int_0^L q \cdot \phi_2 \, dx = q \int_0^L \left(x^2 - \frac{x^3}{3L}\right) dx = q \left[\frac{L^3}{3} - \frac{L^3}{12}\right] = q L^3 \left(\frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{12}\right) = \frac{qL^3}{4}$$ **Step 5 — Final Galerkin System:** $$\boxed{\begin{bmatrix} EA \, L & \dfrac{2EAL^2}{3} \\[6pt] \dfrac{2EAL^2}{3} & \dfrac{8EAL^3}{15} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} c_1 \\ c_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} \dfrac{qL^2}{2} \\[6pt] \dfrac{qL^3}{4} \end{Bmatrix}}$$ **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **Principle of Minimum Potential Energy (PMPE)**. Show that for a 1D linear elastic bar, the PMPE leads to the weak form derived in part (a). **Model Solution (b):** **The PMPE Statement:** A linear elastic body in equilibrium under prescribed external loads attains a configuration in which the **total potential energy** is stationary (and a minimum for stable equilibrium). The total potential energy is: $$\Pi[u] = U[u] - W[u]$$ where: - $U[u]$ = **Strain energy** stored in the body - $W[u]$ = **Work done by external forces** **For the 1D bar problem:** **Strain energy:** $$U = \frac{1}{2} \int_0^L EA \left(\frac{du}{dx}\right)^2 dx$$ **External work** (distributed load $q$ only, no point loads in this case): $$W = \int_0^L q \, u \, dx$$ **Total potential energy:** $$\Pi[u] = \frac{1}{2} \int_0^L EA \left(\frac{du}{dx}\right)^2 dx - \int_0^L q \, u \, dx$$ **Stationarity condition:** Take a variation $u \to u + \epsilon v$ (where $v(0) = 0$ to preserve essential BC) and set $\dfrac{d\Pi}{d\epsilon}\bigg\vert_{\epsilon=0} = 0$: $$\delta \Pi = \int_0^L EA \frac{du}{dx} \frac{dv}{dx} \, dx - \int_0^L q \, v \, dx = 0$$ Rearranging: $$\int_0^L EA \frac{du}{dx} \frac{dv}{dx} \, dx = \int_0^L q \, v \, dx$$ This is **exactly the weak form** obtained in part (a) using the Galerkin WRM. Hence, for self-adjoint, conservative problems, **PMPE ≡ Galerkin WRM ≡ Rayleigh-Ritz**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget to apply the boundary condition $v(0) = 0$ for the test function when deriving the weak form. This step is **mandatory** — without it, the integration by parts yields a non-vanishing boundary term that is mathematically incorrect. Marks are routinely lost here. --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a)** State the **Galerkin Method**. With a neat sketch, explain the procedure to obtain an approximate solution to a boundary value problem using the Galerkin Method. List **any three** other Weighted Residual Methods and state how they differ from Galerkin's method in the choice of weight functions. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution (a):** **Galerkin Method Statement:** The Galerkin Method is a special case of the Weighted Residual Method in which the weight functions are chosen to be **identical to the trial (shape) functions** used in the approximate solution. Mathematically: $$\int_{\Omega} \phi_i(x) \, R(x) \, d\Omega = 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$ where $R(x) = L[u_h] - f$ is the residual and $u_h(x) = \sum_{j=1}^n c_j \phi_j(x)$. **Procedure (Step-by-Step):** 1. Identify the governing differential equation (strong form) and BCs. 2. Choose a set of trial functions $\{\phi_j\}$ that satisfy the essential (geometric) BCs. 3. Construct the approximate solution: $u_h = \sum c_j \phi_j$. 4. Compute the residual $R(x)$ by substituting $u_h$ into the strong form. 5. Form the Galerkin equations: $\int_{\Omega} \phi_i R \, d\Omega = 0$. 6. Solve the resulting algebraic system for $\{c_j\}$. **Three other WRMs:** | **Method** | **Weight Function** | **Difference from Galerkin** | |---|---|---| | **Collocation Method** | $W_i = \delta(x - x_i)$ | Forces residual to vanish at $n$ discrete collocation points; integral becomes a point-satisfaction | | **Least Squares Method** | $W_i = \dfrac{\partial R}{\partial c_i}$ | Minimizes the $L^2$ norm of residual squared; yields a symmetric positive-definite matrix | | **Subdomain Method** | $W_i = 1$ on $\Omega_i$, $0$ elsewhere | Residual is forced to zero on average over each sub-domain $\Omega_i$ | **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Consider the boundary value problem: $$\frac{d^2 u}{dx^2} + u + x = 0, \quad 0 \le x \le 1, \quad u(0) = u(1) = 0$$ Use the **Collocation Method** with trial solution $u_h(x) = c_1 \sin(\pi x) + c_2 \sin(2\pi x)$ and collocation points $x_1 = 0.25$ and $x_2 = 0.75$. Obtain the system of algebraic equations. **Model Solution (b):** **Step 1 — Compute the residual:** $$R(x) = \frac{d^2 u_h}{dx^2} + u_h + x$$ Compute derivatives: $$\frac{du_h}{dx} = c_1 \pi \cos(\pi x) + c_2 (2\pi) \cos(2\pi x)$$ $$\frac{d^2 u_h}{dx^2} = -c_1 \pi^2 \sin(\pi x) - c_2 (2\pi)^2 \sin(2\pi x) = -c_1 \pi^2 \sin(\pi x) - 4 c_2 \pi^2 \sin(2\pi x)$$ Substitute: $$R(x) = -c_1 \pi^2 \sin(\pi x) - 4c_2 \pi^2 \sin(2\pi x) + c_1 \sin(\pi x) + c_2 \sin(2\pi x) + x$$ Group: $$R(x) = c_1 (1 - \pi^2)\sin(\pi x) + c_2 (1 - 4\pi^2)\sin(2\pi x) + x$$ **Step 2 — Collocation at $x_1 = 0.25$:** $R(0.25) = 0$: $$c_1 (1 - \pi^2)\sin(\pi/4) + c_2 (1 - 4\pi^2)\sin(\pi/2) + 0.25 = 0$$ Using $\sin(\pi/4) = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$ and $\sin(\pi/2) = 1$: $$c_1 (1 - \pi^2)\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} + c_2 (1 - 4\pi^2) + 0.25 = 0 \quad \cdots (1)$$ **Step 3 — Collocation at $x_2 = 0.75$:** $R(0.75) = 0$: $$c_1 (1 - \pi^2)\sin(3\pi/4) + c_2 (1 - 4\pi^2)\sin(3\pi/2) + 0.75 = 0$$ Using $\sin(3\pi/4) = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$ and $\sin(3\pi/2) = -1$: $$c_1 (1 - \pi^2)\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} - c_2 (1 - 4\pi^2) + 0.75 = 0 \quad \cdots (2)$$ **Step 4 — Final Collocation System:** $$\boxed{\begin{bmatrix} (1 - \pi^2)\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} & (1 - 4\pi^2) \\[6pt] (1 - \pi^2)\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} & -(1 - 4\pi^2) \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} c_1 \\ c_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} -0.25 \\ -0.75 \end{Bmatrix}}$$ Numerical solution: $\pi^2 \approx 9.87$, so $(1 - \pi^2) \approx -8.87$ and $(1 - 4\pi^2) \approx -38.48$. $$\begin{bmatrix} -6.27 & -38.48 \\ -6.27 & 38.48 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} c_1 \\ c_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} -0.25 \\ -0.75 \end{Bmatrix}$$ Solving: $c_1 \approx 0.040$ and $c_2 \approx -0.004$. The approximate solution is $u_h(x) \approx 0.040 \sin(\pi x) - 0.004 \sin(2\pi x)$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Collocation Method):** Students frequently mis-evaluate $\sin(n\pi x)$ at non-integer values of $n\pi x$ and confuse $\sin(3\pi/2)$ with $\sin(\pi/2)$. Always use the unit circle to confirm signs. Marks are commonly lost due to sign errors. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Variational Formulations & Weighted Residual Methods** - ✅ **Variational Formulation** = restating a PDE as a minimization of a functional (energy functional). - ✅ **Weighted Residual Method (WRM)** = a broader class of methods that enforces orthogonality of the residual to weight functions. - ✅ **Rayleigh-Ritz Method** = classical variational technique; requires a functional; equivalent to Galerkin for self-adjoint operators. - ✅ **Galerkin Method** = most popular WRM; weight functions = trial functions; works for self-adjoint & non-self-adjoint problems. - ✅ **Strong Form** → pointwise differential equation ($C^1$ continuity required). **Weak Form** → integral statement ($C^0$ continuity required). - ✅ **Integration by parts (Green's Theorem)** is the key step that converts strong form → weak form. - ✅ **Essential (Dirichlet) BCs** are imposed on the trial functions; **Natural (Neumann) BCs** emerge automatically from the weak form. - ✅ The canonical FEM equation: $\boxed{[K]\{c\} = \{F\}}$ where $K_{ij} = \int_{\Omega} EA \dfrac{d\phi_i}{dx} \dfrac{d\phi_j}{dx} dx$ and $F_i = \int_{\Omega} \phi_i q \, dx$. - ✅ **Collocation Method**: weights = Dirac deltas; simple but accuracy depends on point selection. - ✅ **Least Squares WRM**: weights = $\partial R / \partial c_i$; yields symmetric matrix; high accuracy. - ✅ **Subdomain WRM**: weights = 1 on sub-region; rarely used in modern FEM. - ✅ In **Naval Architecture**, these methods underpin hull plate analysis, stiffened panel design, ship vibration, and wave-structure interaction. - ✅ **PMPE ≡ Galerkin ≡ Rayleigh-Ritz** for self-adjoint, positive-definite, conservative systems.

topics.title: Analysis of Trusses, Beams, and Frames
Analysis of Plates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST753 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS IN NAVAL ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Plates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the framework of **Finite Element Analysis (FEA)** as applied to **Naval Architecture**, a **plate** is a *flat structural element bounded by two parallel surfaces (the top and bottom faces) whose mid-surface geometry is planar, having a thickness $h$ that is small compared to its in-plane dimensions $a$ and $b$ (typically $h/a \le 1/10$).* A plate resists applied **transverse loads** (loads normal to its mid-surface, e.g., hydrostatic water pressure, cargo loads, sea impact) primarily through **flexural (bending) action**, supplemented by membrane action when in-plane loads coexist.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plate vs. Shell:** A **plate** has a *flat* mid-surface; a **shell** has a *curved* mid-surface. Ship hull plating, while physically curved in a 3D sense, is locally analyzed as a flat plate element in FEA discretizations of stiffened panels, deck plates, and bulkhead plating.

## 1.2 Classical Plate Theories — Hierarchy

Two principal kinematic theories govern plate bending formulations used in naval FEA:

| Theory | Kinematic Assumption | Valid Range (h/a) | Use in Naval FEA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Kirchhoff–Love Plate Theory (KLPT)** — also called *Classical Plate Theory (CPT)* | Normals to the mid-surface remain straight, normal, and inextensible after deformation; transverse shear strain $\gamma_{xz} = \gamma_{yz} = 0$ | Thin plates $\le 1/20$ | Ship deck plating, superstructure panels |
| **Mindlin–Reissner Plate Theory (MRPT)** — also called *First-Order Shear Deformation Theory (FSDT)* | Normals remain straight but *not necessarily normal*; constant transverse shear strain through thickness | Moderately thick $1/100$ to $1/10$ | Bottom shell plating, bulkheads in way of high local loads |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — the cafeteria tray:**
> Imagine holding a rigid cafeteria tray horizontally with both hands and pressing down on its center with your thumb. The tray bends slightly, and you see "ripples" of deflection across its surface.
> - If the tray is made of **thin aluminium** (h/a is small), the deflection is smooth and dominated by **bending** — this is *Kirchhoff–Love* behavior.
> - If the tray is made of **thick rubbery plastic**, it sags and you can see the cross-section *rotate* independently of bending — this *shear deformation* is captured by *Mindlin–Reissner* theory.
> In naval architecture, **the hull plate under slamming or hydrostatic pressure behaves exactly like the tray** — and the FEA "mesh" is the discrete model of how engineers calculate that sag.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Flexural Rigidity of Plate:** $D = \dfrac{Eh^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$ — measured in **N·m**.
- **Poisson's ratio** $\nu$ for shipbuilding steel: $\nu \approx 0.3$ (dimensionless).
- **Young's Modulus** $E$ for shipbuilding steel (Grade AH36): $E = 2.06 \times 10^{5}$ **MPa**.
- **Plate slenderness ratio:** $\beta = b/t$ (breadth-to-thickness); for Class societies, $\beta \le 60$ typically required.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Differential Equation of Plate Bending

For a plate bent by a transverse distributed load $q(x,y)$ per unit area (e.g., **N/m²**), the governing differential equation in classical thin-plate theory is:

$$D \left( \frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial x^{4}} + 2 \frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial x^{2} \partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial y^{4}} \right) = q(x,y)$$

Compactly written using the biharmonic operator $\nabla^{4}$:

$$D \nabla^{4} w(x,y) = q(x,y)$$

where $w(x,y)$ is the **transverse deflection** of the mid-surface (measured downward positive, conventional in naval hydrostatics).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The biharmonic operator $\nabla^{4}$ is the 2-D analog of the beam equation $EI\,w''''(x) = q(x)$.** This is the single most important equation in plate analysis — it must be memorized.

## 2.2 Internal Resultants (Moment & Shear Field)

The plate develops the following stress-resultants per unit length:

| Resultant | Symbol | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bending moment about $y$-axis | $M_{x}$ | $-D\left( \dfrac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial x^{2}} + \nu \dfrac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial y^{2}}\right)$ | **N·m/m** |
| Bending moment about $x$-axis | $M_{y}$ | $-D\left( \dfrac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial y^{2}} + \nu \dfrac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial x^{2}}\right)$ | **N·m/m** |
| Twisting moment | $M_{xy}$ | $-D(1-\nu)\dfrac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial x \partial y}$ | **N·m/m** |
| Kirchhoff effective transverse shear | $Q_{x}$ | $-D\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\nabla^{2}w$ | **N/m** |
| Kirchhoff effective transverse shear | $Q_{y}$ | $-D\dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}\nabla^{2}w$ | **N/m** |

## 2.3 Boundary Conditions — The Four Classical Types

For a plate edge with outward normal $\hat{n}$ and tangent $\hat{t}$:

| Type | Geometric (Essential) | Force (Natural) | Naval Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Simply Supported (SS)** | $w = 0$ | $M_{n} = 0$ | Plate supported on a stiffener with no rotational restraint |
| **Clamped (C) / Fixed** | $w = 0,\ \partial w/\partial n = 0$ | — | Plate welded into a rigid web frame or bulkhead |
| **Free (F)** | — | $M_{n} = 0,\ V_{n} = 0$ | Plate edge free (e.g., hatch corner) |
| **Guided / Symmetry** | $\partial w/\partial n = 0$ | $M_{n} = 0$ | Symmetry line of an analysis model |

The **Kirchhoff corner force** $R = 2M_{xy}$ appears at re-entrant (convex) corners of free plates and is critical for accurate hull-corner stress prediction.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = \dfrac{Eh^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$ | Plate flexural rigidity |
| 2 | $D\nabla^{4}w = q$ | Governing PDE (thin plates) |
| 3 | $w_{\max} = \dfrac{\alpha\, q\, a^{4}}{D}$ | Center deflection of rectangular plate |
| 4 | $M_{\max} = \beta\, q\, a^{2}$ | Design bending moment |
| 5 | $\sigma_{x,\max} = \dfrac{6M_{x}}{h^{2}}$ | Maximum bending stress at outer fibre |
| 6 | $\tau_{xy,\max} = \dfrac{6M_{xy}}{h^{2}}$ | Maximum twisting stress |
| 7 | $K = \dfrac{\sigma \sqrt{a}}{\sigma_{0}}$ — *form follows panel geometry* | Stress concentration / buckling coefficient |
| 8 | $[\mathbf{K}]^{e} = \int_{A^{e}} [\mathbf{B}]^{T} [\mathbf{D}] [\mathbf{B}]\, dA$ | Plate element stiffness in FEA |
| 9 | $[\mathbf{M}]^{e} = \rho h \int_{A^{e}} [\mathbf{N}]^{T} [\mathbf{N}]\, dA$ | Consistent mass matrix of plate element |
| 10 | $\beta = b/t$ | Plate slenderness (Class rule check) |

**Navier's simply-supported rectangular plate coefficient:**

$$\alpha = \frac{4}{\pi^{4}} \sum_{m=1,3,5,\ldots} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots} \frac{(-1)^{\frac{m+n}{2}-1}}{mn\left(\frac{m^{2}}{a^{2}} + \frac{n^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)^{2}}$$

For square plate ($a=b$) under UDL: $\alpha \approx 0.00406$; $\beta_{\text{moment}} \approx 0.0479$.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in Naval Architecture

Plate analysis underpins the **direct strength assessment** (DSA) of:
- **Hull girder plating** under sagging/hogging (longitudinal bending).
- **Bottom shell plates** under slamming loads (transient pressure impulses).
- **Bulkhead plating** under hydrostatic head from adjacent compartments.
- **Deck plates** under container loads (linear or patch loads).

The FEA discretization converts the continuous $\nabla^{4}w = q/D$ problem into a discrete algebraic system $\mathbf{K}\mathbf{U} = \mathbf{F}$ solvable on a computer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Governing Equation from Energy Principles

We derive the governing plate equation using the principle of minimum total potential energy — the foundation stone of FEM.

### Step 1: Strain Energy of Bending

The bending strain energy stored in a plate of mid-surface area $A$ under small deflections $w$ is:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} \int_{A} \left[ M_{x}\kappa_{x} + M_{y}\kappa_{y} + 2M_{xy}\kappa_{xy} \right] dA$$

Substituting the curvature-displacement relations $\kappa_{x} = -\partial^{2}w/\partial x^{2}$, $\kappa_{y} = -\partial^{2}w/\partial y^{2}$, $\kappa_{xy} = -\partial^{2}w/\partial x \partial y$:

$$U = \frac{D}{2} \int_{A} \left[ \left(\frac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial x^{2}}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial y^{2}}\right)^{2} + 2\nu \frac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial x^{2}}\frac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial y^{2}} + 2(1-\nu)\left(\frac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial x \partial y}\right)^{2} \right] dA$$

### Step 2: Potential Energy of Transverse Load

$$\Pi_{\text{load}} = -\int_{A} q\, w\, dA$$

### Step 3: Total Potential Energy

$$\Pi = U - \int_{A} q\, w\, dA$$

### Step 4: First Variation (Stationarity)

Setting $\delta\Pi = 0$ and applying integration by parts **twice** to transfer derivatives from $\delta w$ onto the field $w$ (and generating natural boundary terms on the edges):

$$\delta\Pi = \int_{A} \left[ D\nabla^{4}w - q \right] \delta w\, dA + \text{boundary terms} = 0$$

Since $\delta w$ is arbitrary inside the domain, the integrand must vanish:

$$\boxed{D\nabla^{4}w - q = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad D\nabla^{4}w = q}$$

The boundary terms yield the natural boundary conditions:
- $M_{n} = 0$ (vanishing bending moment), and
- $V_{n} = Q_{n} + \partial M_{nt}/\partial t = 0$ (vanishing effective shear, the Kirchhoff condition).

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Euler–Lagrange equation** of the plate-bending functional. The same derivation in FEM is replaced by element-wise integration of $[B]^{T}[D][B]$ over each element.

## 3.2 Derivation II: Navier Solution for Simply-Supported Rectangular Plate

This is the **canonical analytical benchmark** against which most KTU examiners test plate theory understanding.

### Step 1: Double Fourier Series Expansion

For a rectangular plate $0 \le x \le a,\ 0 \le y \le b$, simply supported on all four edges, with UDL $q_{0}$:

$$w(x,y) = \sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} w_{mn} \sin\!\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)$$

### Step 2: Expand the Load Function

The uniform load $q_{0}$ is expanded in the same basis:

$$q_{0} = \sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} q_{mn} \sin\!\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)$$

Using orthogonality, $q_{mn}$ for $q(x,y) = q_{0}$:

$$q_{mn} = \frac{4}{ab}\int_{0}^{a}\!\int_{0}^{b} q_{0} \sin\!\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right) dx\, dy = \frac{16 q_{0}}{\pi^{2} mn}\quad (m,n \text{ odd})$$

and $q_{mn} = 0$ when either $m$ or $n$ is even.

### Step 3: Substitute into the PDE

Apply $\nabla^{4}$ on the assumed series. Since $\nabla^{2}\sin(\cdot) = -\pi^{2}(m^{2}/a^{2}+n^{2}/b^{2})\sin(\cdot)$:

$$D\nabla^{4}w = D\sum_{m,n} w_{mn} \left[\pi^{2}\!\left(\frac{m^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{n^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)\right]^{2} \sin\!\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)$$

Equating term by term to the load series:

$$D\, w_{mn}\, \pi^{4}\!\left(\frac{m^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{n^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)^{2} = q_{mn}$$

### Step 4: Solve for Each Coefficient

$$w_{mn} = \frac{q_{mn}}{\pi^{4} D \left(\frac{m^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{n^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)^{2}} = \frac{16 q_{0}}{\pi^{6} D\, mn\left(\frac{m^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{n^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)^{2}}\quad (m,n \text{ odd})$$

### Step 5: Final Closed-Form Series

$$\boxed{w(x,y) = \frac{16 q_{0}}{\pi^{6} D}\sum_{m=1,3,5,\ldots}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots} \frac{\sin\!\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)}{mn\left(\frac{m^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{n^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)^{2}}}$$

Center deflection ($x=a/2,\ y=b/2$) gives $\sin$ terms equal to unity:

$$w_{\max} = \frac{16 q_{0}}{\pi^{6} D}\sum_{m=1,3,5,\ldots}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots} \frac{(-1)^{(m+n)/2-1}}{mn\left(\frac{m^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{n^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)^{2}}$$

For a **square plate** ($a=b$): $w_{\max} \approx 0.00406\, q_{0}a^{4}/D$.

## 3.3 Derivation III: FEM Discretization — Element Stiffness Matrix

### Step 1: Displacement Field Assumption

For a 4-noded rectangular plate element (ACM element, Adini–Clough–Melosh), the deflection field uses 12 DOFs (3 per node: $w,\ \theta_{x} = \partial w/\partial y,\ \theta_{y} = -\partial w/\partial x$):

$$w(x,y) = [\mathbf{N}(x,y)]\{\mathbf{u}\}^{e}$$

where $\{\mathbf{u}\}^{e} = [w_{1},\theta_{x1},\theta_{y1},\ldots,w_{4},\theta_{x4},\theta_{y4}]^{T}$.

### Step 2: Curvature-Displacement (B-matrix) Construction

$$\{\boldsymbol{\kappa}\} = \begin{Bmatrix} -\partial^{2}w/\partial x^{2} \\ -\partial^{2}w/\partial y^{2} \\ -2\partial^{2}w/\partial x\partial y \end{Bmatrix} = [\mathbf{B}(x,y)]\{\mathbf{u}\}^{e}$$

### Step 3: Constitutive (D-matrix) for Plate Bending

$$\{\mathbf{M}\} = \begin{Bmatrix} M_{x} \\ M_{y} \\ M_{xy} \end{Bmatrix} = [\mathbf{D}]\{\boldsymbol{\kappa}\},\qquad [\mathbf{D}] = \frac{Eh^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & (1-\nu)/2 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4: Element Stiffness via Strain Energy

$$\boxed{[\mathbf{K}]^{e} = \int_{A^{e}} [\mathbf{B}]^{T}[\mathbf{D}][\mathbf{B}]\, dA = \int_{-1}^{1}\!\int_{-1}^{1} [\mathbf{B}]^{T}[\mathbf{D}][\mathbf{B}]\, \vert\mathbf{J}\vert\, d\xi\, d\eta}$$

The Jacobian $\mathbf{J} = \partial(x,y)/\partial(\xi,\eta)$ maps the parent element (in natural coordinates $\xi,\eta$) to the physical element (in $x,y$). For a rectangular element of sides $a_{e},b_{e}$: $\vert\mathbf{J}\vert = a_{e}b_{e}/4$.

### Step 5: Global Assembly

The structure's equation of motion (or equilibrium under static load) is assembled:

$$[\mathbf{K}]_{\text{global}}\{\mathbf{U}\} = \{\mathbf{F}\}_{\text{global}}$$

Boundary conditions (clamped / simply supported) are imposed by deleting rows/columns or via penalty methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the BFS element is preferred over ACM for thin plates:** The BFS (Bergan–Felippa–Slatin) element uses 16 DOFs (4 nodes × 4 DOFs: $w,\ \theta_{x},\ \theta_{y},\, f$ — the *drilling* DOF). It passes the **patch test** for thin plates, while the ACM element fails it.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Plate Analysis Workflow in Naval FEA

The following block-level functional architecture illustrates the end-to-end plate analysis pipeline as implemented in ship structural design bureaus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1 Geometric Idealization] --> B[Step 2 Element Selection]
    B --> C[Step 3 Mesh Discretization]
    C --> D[Step 4 Material and Thickness Assignment]
    D --> E[Step 5 Boundary Condition Application]
    E --> F[Step 6 Load Application]
    F --> G[Step 7 Assembly of Global Stiffness K and Mass M]
    G --> H[Step 8 Solver Run Linear or Nonlinear]
    H --> I[Step 9 Post Processing Deflections and Stresses]
    I --> J[Step 10 Code Check IACS CSR or Class Rules]
    J --> K[Step 11 Design Iteration or Acceptance]
```

## 4.2 Plate Element Family Hierarchy

The naval FEA practitioner must select an element family appropriate to the plate slenderness regime. The hierarchy below organizes the most common elements encountered in ship structural analysis (e.g., in Nastran, Ansys, or Sesam/GeniE).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Plate Element Family] --> Q[Thin Plate Regime h to a less than 1 over 20]
    P --> R[Thick Plate Regime h to a between 1 over 100 and 1 over 10]
    Q --> S[ACM 12 DOF Rectangular]
    Q --> T[BFS 16 DOF Rectangular Conforming]
    Q --> U[DKT Discrete Kirchhoff Triangle]
    Q --> V[DKQ Discrete Kirchhoff Quadrilateral]
    R --> W[Mindlin Q4 24 DOF Quadrilateral]
    R --> X[Mindlin Q8 48 DOF Serendipity Quad]
    R --> Y[Mindlin Q9 54 DOF Lagrangian Quad]
    R --> Z[HCT 9 DOF Triangular]
```

## 4.3 Boundary Condition Schematic for a Rectangular Plate Panel

The four classical edge conditions are visualized for a simply-supported–clamped–free–guided plate (SCFG), a benchmark problem frequently used to validate FEA codes against the classical Levy solution.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RectangularPlatePanel[Rectangular Plate Panel]
        direction LR
        EdgeTop[Top Edge]:::sim
        EdgeLeft[Left Edge]:::clamp
        EdgeRight[Right Edge]:::free
        EdgeBottom[Bottom Edge]:::guide
    end
    classDef sim fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef clamp fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef free fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef guide fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

**Legend of the four BC colors used in the diagram:**
- **Simply supported (gray)** — edge roller, $w=0$, $M_{n}=0$.
- **Clamped (orange)** — edge fully fixed, $w=0$, $\partial w/\partial n=0$.
- **Free (yellow)** — edge unloaded, $M_{n}=0$, $V_{n}=0$.
- **Guided (green)** — symmetry-like, $\partial w/\partial n=0$, $M_{n}=0$.

## 4.4 Deflection Mode Shape Schematic

The fundamental mode of a simply-supported plate under uniform load is a smooth single-lobe bowl. Higher modes exhibit additional nodal lines — critical for vibration and buckling analysis of hull plates.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ModeFamily[Deflection Mode Family of SS Plate]
        direction LR
        Mode1["Mode 1 1 1 bowl shape symmetric"]:::m1
        Mode2["Mode 2 2 1 one nodal line along x"]:::m2
        Mode3["Mode 3 1 2 one nodal line along y"]:::m3
        Mode4["Mode 4 2 2 two nodal lines cross"]:::m4
    end
    classDef m1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef m2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef m3 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef m4 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The bracketed pairs $(m,n)$ in the labels above denote the *number of half-waves* in the $x$- and $y$-directions respectively — the same integers that appear in the Navier series of §3.2.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define "flexural rigidity" of a plate and state its SI units.
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Flexural rigidity of a plate is the resistance offered by the plate to bending deformation and is given by:

$$D = \frac{E h^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$$

where $E$ is Young's modulus, $h$ is the plate thickness, and $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio. The factor $1/(1-\nu^{2})$ accounts for the *plane-strain* restraint in the through-thickness direction (distinguishing plates from beams, which use $EI$ with no such factor). The SI unit of $D$ is **N·m** (Newton-meter).

For shipbuilding steel with $E = 2.06 \times 10^{11}$ Pa, $\nu = 0.3$, and $h = 0.02$ m:

$$D = \frac{(2.06\times 10^{11})(0.02)^{3}}{12(1-0.3^{2})} \approx 1.59\times 10^{5}\ \text{N·m}$$

### Q2. Differentiate between Kirchhoff–Love and Mindlin–Reissner plate theories.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Kirchhoff–Love (CPT) | Mindlin–Reissner (FSDT) |
|---|---|---|
| Kinematic assumption | Normals remain straight, *normal*, and inextensible | Normals remain straight but *not necessarily normal* |
| Transverse shear deformation | **Neglected** ($\gamma_{xz}=\gamma_{yz}=0$) | **Included** (constant through thickness) |
| Independent rotations | None — derived from $w$ | Independent nodal rotations $\theta_{x},\theta_{y}$ |
| Valid range | Thin plates, $h/a \le 1/20$ | Moderately thick, $1/100 \le h/a \le 1/10$ |
| DOFs per node | 3 ($w,\ \partial w/\partial x,\ \partial w/\partial y$) | 3 ($w,\ \theta_{x},\ \theta_{y}$) |

**Memory hook:** Kirchhoff–Love = *bending only*; Mindlin–Reissner = *bending + shear*.

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Plate Element Formulation:**
> The most common mark-loss area in plate-FEM questions is the **constitutive [D]-matrix**. Students frequently write the plane-stress [D] matrix (used for membrane elements) instead of the plate-bending [D] which has $h^{3}/12$ factored out and $(1-\nu)/2$ in the shear entry. A second common error is forgetting the *Jacobian determinant* $\vert\mathbf{J}\vert$ in the stiffness integral — leading to wrong units and losing 2 marks. Always verify the symmetry of $[\mathbf{K}]^{e}$ before declaring your answer correct.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 (Model)]

**(a)** Derive the governing differential equation of a thin rectangular plate subjected to a uniform transverse load using the principle of minimum total potential energy. Clearly state the strain-energy functional and identify all boundary terms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a simply-supported rectangular plate of dimensions $a \times b$ and thickness $h$, under uniform load $q_{0}$, develop the **Navier double Fourier series solution** and obtain the expression for the centre deflection $w_{\max}$. Compute the numerical coefficient for a square plate. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The total potential energy functional for a thin plate is:

$$\Pi[w] = \frac{D}{2}\int_{A}\!\int\! \left[ \left(\frac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial x^{2}}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial y^{2}}\right)^{2} + 2\nu\frac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial x^{2}}\frac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial y^{2}} + 2(1-\nu)\!\left(\frac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial x\partial y}\right)^{2} \right] dA - \int_{A}\!\int\! q\,w\, dA$$

Taking the first variation $\delta\Pi = 0$, integrating by parts twice in each of the $x$- and $y$-directions, and using the Gauss divergence theorem to convert area integrals of derivatives to boundary line integrals:

$$\delta\Pi = \int_{A}\!\int\! \left[ D\nabla^{4}w - q \right]\delta w\, dA + \oint_{\partial A}\!\Big[ V_{n}\,\delta w - M_{n}\,\delta(\partial w/\partial n) \Big] ds = 0$$

By the fundamental lemma of calculus of variations (since $\delta w$ is arbitrary inside the domain and on the geometric part of the boundary), the area integrand must vanish identically:

$$\boxed{D\nabla^{4}w = q(x,y)}$$

The line-integral terms yield the natural boundary conditions:
- $M_{n} = 0$ along free/loaded edges.
- $V_{n} = Q_{n} + \partial M_{nt}/\partial t = 0$ along free edges.

Geometric (essential) boundary conditions are imposed separately:
- $w=0$ (simply supported or clamped).
- $\partial w/\partial n = 0$ (clamped or guided).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Assume a double Fourier sine series compatible with the simply-supported boundary conditions $w(0,y)=w(a,y)=w(x,0)=w(x,b)=0$:

$$w(x,y) = \sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} w_{mn} \sin\!\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)$$

Apply $\nabla^{4}$ using $\nabla^{2}\sin(m\pi x/a)\sin(n\pi y/b) = -\pi^{2}(m^{2}/a^{2}+n^{2}/b^{2})\sin(\cdot)$:

$$D\nabla^{4}w = D\sum_{m,n} w_{mn}\pi^{4}\!\left(\frac{m^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{n^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)^{2}\sin\!\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right) = q_{0}$$

Expand $q_{0}$ similarly. By Fourier orthogonality:

$$q_{0} = \sum_{m,n} q_{mn}\sin\!\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right),\quad q_{mn} = \frac{16 q_{0}}{\pi^{2} mn}\ \ (m,n\ \text{odd}),\quad 0\ \text{otherwise}$$

Equating coefficients term-by-term:

$$w_{mn} = \frac{16 q_{0}}{\pi^{6} D\, mn\,(m^{2}/a^{2}+n^{2}/b^{2})^{2}}\ \ (m,n\ \text{odd})$$

The center deflection ($x=a/2,\ y=b/2$, $\sin$ terms $=1$, and $(-1)^{(m+n)/2-1}$ factor from series evaluation at center):

$$w_{\max} = \frac{16 q_{0}}{\pi^{6} D}\sum_{m=1,3,5,\ldots}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots} \frac{(-1)^{(m+n)/2-1}}{mn\,(m^{2}/a^{2}+n^{2}/b^{2})^{2}}$$

For **square plate** ($a=b$), taking $m=n=1$ as leading term:

$$w_{11} = \frac{16 q_{0}}{\pi^{6} D \cdot 1\cdot 1 \cdot (2/a^{2})^{2}} = \frac{16 q_{0} a^{4}}{4\pi^{6} D} = \frac{4 q_{0} a^{4}}{\pi^{6} D} \approx \frac{4 q_{0}a^{4}}{961.4\,D}$$

Including the full series gives the well-known coefficient:

$$\boxed{w_{\max} \approx 0.00406\,\frac{q_{0} a^{4}}{D}}$$

and the maximum bending moment coefficient (also at centre): $M_{\max} \approx 0.0479\,q_{0}a^{2}$.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]

**(a)** With reference to naval structural applications, classify plate-bending finite elements. Compare the ACM (non-conforming) and BFS (conforming) rectangular elements in terms of degrees of freedom, patch test compliance, and applicability to thin hull plating. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the FEA of a ship deck plate modelled as a rectangular Mindlin plate element, derive the element stiffness matrix expression. Explain the role of the Jacobian $\mathbf{J}$ and the shear-locking phenomenon. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Classification of plate-bending elements (by kinematic theory):**

1. **Kirchhoff-family (thin plates, $h/a \le 1/20$):**
   - **ACM** element — Adini–Clough–Melosh rectangular, 12 DOFs (3 per node).
   - **BFS** element — Bergan–Felippa–Slatin rectangular, 16 DOFs (4 per node, includes *drilling* DOF $f$).
   - **DKT** — Discrete Kirchhoff Triangle.
   - **DKQ** — Discrete Kirchhoff Quadrilateral (BFS-like on quad mesh).

2. **Mindlin-family (FSDT, moderately thick plates):**
   - **Q4** quadrilateral — 24 DOFs (3 per node × 4 + no drilling).
   - **Q8** serendipity — 48 DOFs.
   - **Q9** Lagrangian — 54 DOFs.
   - **HCT** — Hsieh–Clough–Tocher triangular, 9 DOFs.
   - **LST** — Linear Strain Triangle.

**Comparison ACM vs BFS for hull plating:**

| Feature | ACM | BFS |
|---|---|---|
| DOFs per node | 3 | 4 (adds drilling $f$) |
| Total DOFs per element | 12 | 16 |
| Patch test | **Fails** (non-conforming) | **Passes** |
| Field completeness | Incomplete cubic | Complete cubic in deflection + drill |
| Convergence rate | Slow (extra spurious energy modes) | Optimal |
| Use in ship design | Teaching/codes only | **Standard in Class-rule FE analyses** |

**Naval recommendation:** For deck plating (typically thin relative to stiffener spacing), use **BFS** or **DKQ** elements. ACM is acceptable only for academic illustration and *not* for submission to Class societies (DNV, LR, ABS, BV).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Mindlin plate element kinematics:** For a 4-noded quadrilateral, each node carries DOFs $\{w,\ \theta_{x},\ \theta_{y}\}^{T}$. The displacement and rotation fields are interpolated independently:

$$w(x,y) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} N_{i}(\xi,\eta)\, w_{i},\quad \theta_{x}(x,y) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} N_{i}(\xi,\eta)\, \theta_{xi},\quad \theta_{y}(x,y) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} N_{i}(\xi,\eta)\, \theta_{yi}$$

The Mindlin curvature and shear strain vectors are:

$$\{\boldsymbol{\kappa}\} = \begin{Bmatrix} \partial\theta_{y}/\partial x \\ -\partial\theta_{x}/\partial y \\ \partial\theta_{y}/\partial y - \partial\theta_{x}/\partial x \end{Bmatrix},\qquad \{\boldsymbol{\gamma}\} = \begin{Bmatrix} \partial w/\partial x + \theta_{y} \\ \partial w/\partial y - \theta_{x} \end{Bmatrix}$$

Constitutive matrices:

$$[\mathbf{D}_{b}] = \frac{Eh^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}\!\begin{bmatrix}1&\nu&0\\\nu&1&0\\0&0&(1-\nu)/2\end{bmatrix},\quad [\mathbf{D}_{s}] = \kappa_{s}\frac{Eh}{2(1+\nu)}\!\begin{bmatrix}1&0\\0&1\end{bmatrix}$$

where $\kappa_{s}=5/6$ is the shear-correction factor for rectangular cross-sections.

Element stiffness matrices (bending + shear):

$$[\mathbf{K}_{b}]^{e} = \int_{A^{e}} [\mathbf{B}_{b}]^{T}[\mathbf{D}_{b}][\mathbf{B}_{b}]\, dA,\qquad [\mathbf{K}_{s}]^{e} = \int_{A^{e}} [\mathbf{B}_{s}]^{T}[\mathbf{D}_{s}][\mathbf{B}_{s}]\, dA$$

$$[\mathbf{K}]^{e} = [\mathbf{K}_{b}]^{e} + [\mathbf{K}_{s}]^{e}$$

**Role of Jacobian $\mathbf{J}$:** The bilinear isoparametric mapping $x(\xi,\eta),\ y(\xi,\eta)$ between parent (square $\xi,\eta\in[-1,1]$) and physical element introduces the Jacobian:

$$\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} \partial x/\partial\xi & \partial y/\partial\xi \\ \partial x/\partial\eta & \partial y/\partial\eta \end{bmatrix}$$

Derivatives in physical coordinates are obtained by $\partial(\cdot)/\partial x = (1/\vert\mathbf{J}\vert)(\partial(\cdot)/\partial\eta\,\partial y/\partial\xi - \partial(\cdot)/\partial\xi\,\partial y/\partial\eta)$, with $\vert\mathbf{J}\vert$ appearing as the area-scaling factor in the integral. The integral is evaluated in natural coordinates:

$$[\mathbf{K}]^{e} = \int_{-1}^{1}\!\int_{-1}^{1} [\mathbf{B}(\xi,\eta)]^{T}[\mathbf{D}][\mathbf{B}(\xi,\eta)]\,\vert\mathbf{J}\vert\, d\xi\, d\eta$$

This is evaluated by **Gauss quadrature** — typically $2\times 2$ points for Q4.

**Shear locking:** For very thin Mindlin plates ($h/a \to 0$), the shear energy $[\mathbf{K}_{s}]^{e}$ becomes energetically dominant and over-stiffens the element, producing *spurious* parasitic shear stresses that force the element to behave as if $\gamma_{xz}=\gamma_{yz}=0$. The deflection collapses to zero as $h\to 0$ — an unphysical result. **Remedies** include:
- **Reduced integration** of the shear part (1-point Gauss instead of 2×2) — assumed-strain methods.
- **Discrete Kirchhoff** constraint (DKT, DKQ) to enforce $\gamma=0$ kinematically.
- **B-bar** or **EAS** enhanced-assumed-strain formulations.

In ship FEA, **selective/reduced integration** is the most common practical remedy for hull plating analysed with Mindlin Q4 elements.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Governing equation:** $D\nabla^{4}w = q$ — memorize verbatim. $D = Eh^{3}/[12(1-\nu^{2})]$.
- **Plate vs shell:** plate = flat mid-surface; shell = curved mid-surface.
- **Plate vs beam:** $EI\,w'''' = q$ (1-D, beam) becomes $D\nabla^{4}w = q$ (2-D, plate). Plane-strain factor $1/(1-\nu^{2})$ is the hallmark of plate rigidity.
- **Four boundary condition types:** simply supported ($w=0,\ M_{n}=0$), clamped ($w=0,\ \partial w/\partial n=0$), free ($M_{n}=0,\ V_{n}=0$), guided ($\partial w/\partial n=0,\ M_{n}=0$).
- **Navier solution** = double sine series for *simply supported* rectangular plates under sinusoidal/U DL loads.
- **Levy solution** = single sine series + series solution of an ODE in the other coordinate, for plates with two opposite simply-supported edges and arbitrary BCs on the other two.
- **Square SS plate under UDL coefficients:** $w_{\max} \approx 0.00406\,q_{0}a^{4}/D$ and $M_{\max} \approx 0.0479\,q_{0}a^{2}$.
- **Element stiffness integral:** $[\mathbf{K}]^{e} = \int_{A^{e}} [\mathbf{B}]^{T}[\mathbf{D}][\mathbf{B}]\, dA$, evaluated via Gauss quadrature with $\vert\mathbf{J}\vert$ factor.
- **Plate-bending [D]-matrix** has $h^{3}/12$ factored out — never confuse with plane-stress [D].
- **ACM vs BFS:** ACM fails the patch test, BFS passes; for class-rule submissions always use BFS/DKQ.
- **Shear locking** affects Mindlin elements in the thin-plate limit — remedies: reduced integration, DKT/DKQ, EAS.
- **Naval applications to remember:** hull girder plating, deck plates under patch loads, bulkhead plating under hydrostatic pressure, bottom shell under slamming.
- **Stress resultants are per unit length** (N·m/m for moments, N/m for shears) — distinct from beam internal forces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-mile revision tip (KTU 2024 Scheme):** When asked to "derive the plate equation" in the exam, always include (i) the strain-energy functional, (ii) the variation $\delta\Pi = 0$, (iii) integration by parts showing the boundary terms, and (iv) the natural BCs. Examiners award full marks only when **all four** steps are explicit. A derivation that jumps directly to $D\nabla^{4}w = q$ loses 3 of 7 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Three-Dimensional Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST753 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS IN NAVAL ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Three-Dimensional Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Finite Element Analysis (FEA) in Naval Architecture**, the *Analysis of Three-Dimensional Problems* refers to the numerical discretization and solution of elastostatic and elastodynamic field problems in which the state variables (displacement, stress, strain) vary independently along all three principal Cartesian coordinates (x, y, z). 

For KTU 2024 Scheme purposes, this topic specifically covers:
- 3D elasticity formulation using the **principle of minimum potential energy**.
- Discretization of a 3D continuum using **solid (brick and tetrahedral) isoparametric elements**.
- Derivation of the **element stiffness matrix** $[\mathbf{k}]^e$ for 3D solid elements.
- Evaluation of $[k]^e$ through **numerical (Gauss-Legendre) integration** over the element volume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic is foundational for advanced modules (Module 2 onward) that deal with plate/shell elements and ship-hull structural analysis. Mastery of the 3D stiffness formulation is mandatory.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **ship's rudder horn casting** or a **stern frame bracket** — a chunky, three-dimensional component where stresses genuinely flow in *all three directions* simultaneously. A 2D plane-stress or plane-strain analysis would be dangerously misleading here because the out-of-plane stress components (e.g., $\sigma_z$, $\tau_{yz}$) are non-negligible.

The Finite Element Method handles this by:
1. **Carving** the solid into tiny **3D "bricks"** (hexahedra) or **"pyramids"** (tetrahedra) — think of a ship modeler building a digital hull from thousands of small LEGO-like volumetric blocks.
2. **Assuming** a simple polynomial variation of displacement inside each block.
3. **Stitching** the blocks together at shared nodes so equilibrium is enforced globally.

The result: a giant linear system $[\mathbf{K}]\{\mathbf{U}\} = \{\mathbf{F}\}$ that, when solved, gives the complete 3D displacement field — and from it, the full 3D stress tensor at every point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants of Interest:** For marine-grade steel typical in shipbuilding, $E \approx 2.0 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/mm}^2$ and $\nu \approx 0.30$. These values populate the constitutive matrix $[\mathbf{D}]$.

## 1.3 Why 3D Analysis is Critical in Naval Architecture

| Ship Component | Reason 3D Analysis is Mandatory |
|----------------|----------------------------------|
| Stern frame / rudder horn | High triaxial stress concentration at the propeller aperture |
| Bulbous bow transition | Geometric curvature produces full 3D stress state |
| Bracket knees (triple junctions) | All six stress components ($\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z, \tau_{xy}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{zx}$) are significant |
| Shaft bracket / boss | Stress concentration cannot be captured by 2D models |
| Anchor pockets, mooring brackets | Local 3D stress field governs fatigue life |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 3D Elasticity Problem — Mathematical Setup

A 3D elastic continuum in equilibrium is governed by:

**Equilibrium Equations** (3 equations, one per direction):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \sigma_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xz}}{\partial z} + f_x &= 0 \\
\frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \sigma_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{yz}}{\partial z} + f_y &= 0 \\
\frac{\partial \tau_{xz}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{yz}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \sigma_z}{\partial z} + f_z &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Strain–Displacement Relations** (kinematics, small deformations):

$$
\{\varepsilon\} = \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_x \\ \varepsilon_y \\ \varepsilon_z \\ \gamma_{xy} \\ \gamma_{yz} \\ \gamma_{zx} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \partial u / \partial x \\ \partial v / \partial y \\ \partial w / \partial z \\ \partial u / \partial y + \partial v / \partial x \\ \partial v / \partial z + \partial w / \partial y \\ \partial w / \partial x + \partial u / \partial z \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Constitutive Relation** (3D Hooke's Law for isotropic material):

$$
\{\sigma\} = [\mathbf{D}] \{\varepsilon\}
$$

where the **3D Elasticity Matrix** $[\mathbf{D}]$ is:

$$
[\mathbf{D}] = \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)} \begin{bmatrix} 1-\nu & \nu & \nu & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ \nu & 1-\nu & \nu & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ \nu & \nu & 1-\nu & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \frac{1-2\nu}{2} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \frac{1-2\nu}{2} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \frac{1-2\nu}{2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $\frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}$ is often written in compact form using the **bulk modulus $K$** and **shear modulus $G$**: $K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$, $G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$.

## 2.2 Discretization — From Continuum to Finite Elements

The 3D domain $\Omega$ is divided into $n_e$ **solid elements** (tetrahedra or hexahedra). Inside each element $e$, the displacement field is approximated as:

$$
\{u(x,y,z)\} = \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ w \end{bmatrix} = [\mathbf{N}(x,y,z)] \, \{\mathbf{d}\}^e
$$

where:
- $[\mathbf{N}]$ is the **shape function matrix** (of size $3 \times 3n$, where $n$ is the number of nodes per element).
- $\{\mathbf{d}\}^e$ is the **element nodal displacement vector** (size $3n \times 1$).

The corresponding **strain field** becomes:

$$
\{\varepsilon\} = [\mathbf{B}] \, \{\mathbf{d}\}^e
$$

where $[\mathbf{B}]$ is the **strain–displacement matrix** (size $6 \times 3n$).

## 2.3 Element Stiffness Matrix for 3D Solids

Applying the principle of minimum potential energy, the element stiffness matrix is:

$$
[\mathbf{k}]^e = \int_{V_e} [\mathbf{B}]^T [\mathbf{D}] [\mathbf{B}] \, dV
$$

This is the **central equation** of 3D FEA. The integration is over the element volume $V_e$ in physical (x, y, z) space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In 3D problems, $\dim([\mathbf{k}]^e) = 3n \times 3n$. For an 8-noded hexahedral element, this is a $24 \times 24$ symmetric matrix. The size of $[\mathbf{k}]^e$ is what makes 3D ship structural FE models computationally intensive.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Notes |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------|
| 1 | Displacement field | $\{u\} = [\mathbf{N}]\{\mathbf{d}\}^e$ | Approximation inside element |
| 2 | Strain vector | $\{\varepsilon\} = [\mathbf{B}]\{\mathbf{d}\}^e$ | 6 components |
| 3 | Stress vector | $\{\sigma\} = [\mathbf{D}][\mathbf{B}]\{\mathbf{d}\}^e$ | 6 components |
| 4 | Element stiffness | $[\mathbf{k}]^e = \int_{V_e} [\mathbf{B}]^T[\mathbf{D}][\mathbf{B}] \, dV$ | Symmetric, positive-definite |
| 5 | Element nodal force | $\{\mathbf{f}\}^e = \int_{V_e} [\mathbf{N}]^T \{\mathbf{b}\} dV + \int_{S_e} [\mathbf{N}]^T \{\mathbf{t}\} dS$ | Body force $\mathbf{b}$ + traction $\mathbf{t}$ |
| 6 | 3D Elasticity matrix | $[\mathbf{D}] = \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}[\text{matrix}]$ | Symmetric, $6\times 6$ |
| 7 | Hexahedral element nodes | 8 corner nodes | Trilinear shape functions |
| 8 | Tetrahedral element nodes | 4 corner nodes | Linear shape functions |
| 9 | Gauss quadrature (brick) | $\int_{V_e} F dV = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} w_i w_j w_k \vert J\vert F(\xi_i,\eta_j,\zeta_k)$ | Typically $2\times 2 \times 2 = 8$ points |
| 10 | Tetra Gauss order | 1-point at centroid with weight $V/6$ | Exact for linear shape functions |
| 11 | Jacobian determinant | $\vert J\vert = \det \begin{bmatrix} \partial x/\partial \xi & \partial y/\partial \xi & \partial z/\partial \xi \\ \partial x/\partial \eta & \partial y/\partial \eta & \partial z/\partial \eta \\ \partial x/\partial \zeta & \partial y/\partial \zeta & \partial z/\partial \zeta \end{bmatrix}$ | Maps $(\xi,\eta,\zeta) \to (x,y,z)$ |
| 12 | Penalty parameter | $\lambda = \frac{E\nu}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}$ | Appears in $[\mathbf{D}]$ diagonal |
| 13 | Shear modulus | $G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | Used in off-diagonal blocks |
| 14 | Plane-stress reduction | 3D $[\mathbf{D}]$ collapses to $3\times 3$ matrix | Special case ($\sigma_z = 0$) |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Naval Architecture

- **Stern frame analysis**: Full 3D stress state around the propeller boss — informs fatigue life calculation per IACS UR S18.
- **Hatch corner coaming**: Triaxial stress concentration at the hatch corner — governs scantling design of bulk carriers.
- **Torsion of ship hulls under warping**: 3D FEM captures warping-induced shear stresses that beam theory misses.
- **Crashworthiness / collision analysis**: 3D solid elements model the local crushing of double-bottom structures during grounding.
- **Welding residual stress**: 3D thermo-mechanical coupling uses the same 3D $[\mathbf{B}]$ and $[\mathbf{D}]$ framework.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Potential Energy Functional

The total potential energy $\Pi$ of a 3D elastic body is:

$$
\Pi = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} \int_{V} \{\varepsilon\}^T [\mathbf{D}] \{\varepsilon\} \, dV}_{\text{Strain energy } U} - \underbrace{\int_{V} \{u\}^T \{\mathbf{b}\} \, dV - \int_{S} \{u\}^T \{\mathbf{t}\} \, dS}_{\text{Work done by external loads } W}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Minimizing $\Pi$ ($\delta \Pi = 0$) is the variational statement of equilibrium. This is the **engine** of the FEM.

### Step-by-Step Minimization

**Step 1:** Substitute the discrete displacement field $\{u\} = [\mathbf{N}]\{\mathbf{d}\}^e$ into the strain expression:

$$
\{\varepsilon\} = [\mathbf{B}]\{\mathbf{d}\}^e
$$

where the strain-displacement operator $[\mathbf{L}]$ is:

$$
[\mathbf{L}] = \begin{bmatrix} \partial/\partial x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \partial/\partial y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \partial/\partial z \\ \partial/\partial y & \partial/\partial x & 0 \\ 0 & \partial/\partial z & \partial/\partial y \\ \partial/\partial z & 0 & \partial/\partial x \end{bmatrix}
$$

so that $[\mathbf{B}] = [\mathbf{L}][\mathbf{N}]$.

**Step 2:** Substitute into the strain energy term $U$:

$$
U = \frac{1}{2} (\{\mathbf{d}\}^e)^T \left( \int_V [\mathbf{B}]^T [\mathbf{D}] [\mathbf{B}] \, dV \right) \{\mathbf{d}\}^e
$$

Recognize that the bracketed term is precisely $[\mathbf{k}]^e$:

$$
U = \frac{1}{2} (\{\mathbf{d}\}^e)^T [\mathbf{k}]^e \{\mathbf{d}\}^e
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the work term $W$:

$$
W = (\{\mathbf{d}\}^e)^T \left( \int_V [\mathbf{N}]^T \{\mathbf{b}\} \, dV + \int_S [\mathbf{N}]^T \{\mathbf{t}\} \, dS \right) = (\{\mathbf{d}\}^e)^T \{\mathbf{f}\}^e
$$

**Step 4:** Form the total potential energy:

$$
\Pi = \frac{1}{2} (\{\mathbf{d}\}^e)^T [\mathbf{k}]^e \{\mathbf{d}\}^e - (\{\mathbf{d}\}^e)^T \{\mathbf{f}\}^e
$$

**Step 5:** Take the first variation and set to zero ($\delta \Pi = 0$):

$$
\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial \{\mathbf{d}\}^e} = [\mathbf{k}]^e \{\mathbf{d}\}^e - \{\mathbf{f}\}^e = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{[\mathbf{k}]^e \{\mathbf{d}\}^e = \{\mathbf{f}\}^e}
$$

This is the **element equilibrium equation**. Assembling over all elements gives the global system $[\mathbf{K}]\{\mathbf{U}\} = \{\mathbf{F}\}$.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Shape Functions for a 4-Noded Tetrahedral Element

Consider a tetrahedron with nodes $i, j, k, m$ at positions $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$, etc. Using **natural (volume) coordinates** $(L_i, L_j, L_k, L_m)$ such that $L_i + L_j + L_k + L_m = 1$, the linear shape function at node $i$ is:

$$
N_i = L_i = \frac{V_i}{V_{\text{total}}}
$$

where $V_i$ is the volume of the sub-tetrahedron formed by node $i$ and the opposite face.

Explicitly, the shape function in Cartesian coordinates is:

$$
N_i(x,y,z) = \frac{1}{6V} \begin{vmatrix} 1 & x & y & z \\ 1 & x_j & y_j & z_j \\ 1 & x_k & y_k & z_k \\ 1 & x_m & y_m & z_m \end{vmatrix}
$$

where the total volume is:

$$
V = \frac{1}{6} \begin{vmatrix} 1 & x_i & y_i & z_i \\ 1 & x_j & y_j & z_j \\ 1 & x_k & y_k & z_k \\ 1 & x_m & y_m & z_m \end{vmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Linear shape functions over a tetrahedron give **constant strain** within the element. This means $[\mathbf{B}]$ is **constant** (does not depend on x, y, z) — and the volume integral in $[\mathbf{k}]^e = [\mathbf{B}]^T[\mathbf{D}][\mathbf{B}] V$ reduces to a simple matrix product multiplied by the element volume.

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Shape Functions for an 8-Noded Hexahedral (Brick) Element

The 8-noded brick uses **isoparametric formulation** in the parent (natural) coordinate system $(\xi, \eta, \zeta)$ with $\xi, \eta, \zeta \in [-1, +1]$.

The coordinates and displacement of any point inside the element are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(\xi,\eta,\zeta) &= \sum_{i=1}^{8} N_i(\xi,\eta,\zeta) \, x_i \\
y(\xi,\eta,\zeta) &= \sum_{i=1}^{8} N_i(\xi,\eta,\zeta) \, y_i \\
z(\xi,\eta,\zeta) &= \sum_{i=1}^{8} N_i(\xi,\eta,\zeta) \, z_i
\end{aligned}
$$

The **trilinear shape function** for corner node $i$ (with local coordinates $\xi_i, \eta_i, \zeta_i = \pm 1$) is:

$$
N_i(\xi,\eta,\zeta) = \frac{1}{8} (1 + \xi \xi_i)(1 + \eta \eta_i)(1 + \zeta \zeta_i)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Verify: at node $i$, $(\xi, \eta, \zeta) = (\xi_i, \eta_i, \zeta_i)$, so $N_i = \frac{1}{8}(1+1)(1+1)(1+1) = 1$. At any other node $j \neq i$, one of the factors vanishes, giving $N_i = 0$. ✓

## 3.4 Derivation of the Jacobian Matrix

To transform derivatives from natural to physical coordinates, we use the **chain rule**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial N_i}{\partial x} &= \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi} \frac{\partial \xi}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta} \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \zeta} \frac{\partial \zeta}{\partial x} \\
\frac{\partial N_i}{\partial y} &= \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi} \frac{\partial \xi}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta} \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \zeta} \frac{\partial \zeta}{\partial y} \\
\frac{\partial N_i}{\partial z} &= \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi} \frac{\partial \xi}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta} \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \zeta} \frac{\partial \zeta}{\partial z}
\end{aligned}
$$

In compact matrix form:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \partial N_i/\partial x \\ \partial N_i/\partial y \\ \partial N_i/\partial z \end{bmatrix} = [\mathbf{J}]^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} \partial N_i/\partial \xi \\ \partial N_i/\partial \eta \\ \partial N_i/\partial \zeta \end{bmatrix}
$$

where the **Jacobian matrix** is:

$$
[\mathbf{J}] = \begin{bmatrix} \partial x/\partial \xi & \partial y/\partial \xi & \partial z/\partial \xi \\ \partial x/\partial \eta & \partial y/\partial \eta & \partial z/\partial \eta \\ \partial x/\partial \zeta & \partial y/\partial \zeta & \partial z/\partial \zeta \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sum \partial N_i/\partial \xi \cdot x_i & \sum \partial N_i/\partial \xi \cdot y_i & \sum \partial N_i/\partial \xi \cdot z_i \\ \sum \partial N_i/\partial \eta \cdot x_i & \sum \partial N_i/\partial \eta \cdot y_i & \sum \partial N_i/\partial \eta \cdot z_i \\ \sum \partial N_i/\partial \zeta \cdot x_i & \sum \partial N_i/\partial \zeta \cdot y_i & \sum \partial N_i/\partial \zeta \cdot z_i \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The determinant of the Jacobian $\det(\mathbf{J})$ (often written $\vert J\vert$) appears in the volume element transformation: $dV = \vert J\vert \, d\xi \, d\eta \, d\zeta$. This is the key quantity for numerical integration.

## 3.5 Derivation of the 3D Stiffness Matrix via Numerical Integration

The general 3D element stiffness matrix is:

$$
[\mathbf{k}]^e = \int_{-1}^{1}\int_{-1}^{1}\int_{-1}^{1} [\mathbf{B}(\xi,\eta,\zeta)]^T [\mathbf{D}] [\mathbf{B}(\xi,\eta,\zeta)] \, \vert J(\xi,\eta,\zeta)\vert \, d\xi \, d\eta \, d\zeta
$$

Using **Gauss-Legendre quadrature** with $n_g$ points per direction:

$$
[\mathbf{k}]^e \approx \sum_{p=1}^{n_g} \sum_{q=1}^{n_g} \sum_{r=1}^{n_g} w_p w_q w_r \, [\mathbf{B}(\xi_p, \eta_q, \zeta_r)]^T [\mathbf{D}] [\mathbf{B}(\xi_p, \eta_q, \zeta_r)] \, \vert J(\xi_p, \eta_q, \zeta_r)\vert
$$

where $w_p, w_q, w_r$ are Gauss weights and $(\xi_p, \eta_q, \zeta_r)$ are Gauss points.

**Standard Gauss Points and Weights for 3D Brick Elements:**

| Order $n_g$ | Gauss Points $\xi_p$ | Weights $w_p$ | Total Points in 3D |
|--------------|----------------------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 (under) | 0 | 2 | 1 |
| 2 (standard) | $\pm 1/\sqrt{3} \approx \pm 0.5774$ | 1 | 8 |
| 3 (over) | $0$, $\pm 0.7746$ | 0.8889, 0.5556 | 27 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the 8-noded brick with **linear** strain field, $2 \times 2 \times 2 = 8$ Gauss points are sufficient and exact. This is the most common choice in ship structural FE software.

## 3.6 Worked Example: Stiffness of a Unit Tetrahedral Element

Consider a regular tetrahedron with vertices at:
- Node 1: $(0, 0, 0)$
- Node 2: $(1, 0, 0)$
- Node 3: $(1/2, \sqrt{3}/2, 0)$
- Node 4: $(1/2, \sqrt{3}/6, \sqrt{6}/3)$

The element volume is:

$$
V = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{12}
$$

**Linear shape functions** (4 of them):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_1 &= 1 - x - \frac{y}{\sqrt{3}} - z\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} \\
N_2 &= x - \frac{y}{\sqrt{3}} \\
N_3 &= \frac{2y}{\sqrt{3}} \\
N_4 &= z\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Strain-displacement matrix** $[\mathbf{B}]$ (constant, $6 \times 12$):

$$
[\mathbf{B}] = \begin{bmatrix} \partial N_1/\partial x & 0 & 0 & \partial N_2/\partial x & 0 & 0 & \partial N_3/\partial x & 0 & 0 & \partial N_4/\partial x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \partial N_1/\partial y & 0 & 0 & \partial N_2/\partial y & 0 & 0 & \partial N_3/\partial y & 0 & 0 & \partial N_4/\partial y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \partial N_1/\partial z & 0 & 0 & \partial N_2/\partial z & 0 & 0 & \partial N_3/\partial z & 0 & 0 & \partial N_4/\partial z \\ \partial N_1/\partial y & \partial N_1/\partial x & 0 & \partial N_2/\partial y & \partial N_2/\partial x & 0 & \partial N_3/\partial y & \partial N_3/\partial x & 0 & \partial N_4/\partial y & \partial N_4/\partial x & 0 \\ 0 & \partial N_1/\partial z & \partial N_1/\partial y & 0 & \partial N_2/\partial z & \partial N_2/\partial y & 0 & \partial N_3/\partial z & \partial N_3/\partial y & 0 & \partial N_4/\partial z & \partial N_4/\partial y \\ \partial N_1/\partial z & 0 & \partial N_1/\partial x & \partial N_2/\partial z & 0 & \partial N_2/\partial x & \partial N_3/\partial z & 0 & \partial N_3/\partial x & \partial N_4/\partial z & 0 & \partial N_4/\partial x \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Stiffness matrix:**

$$
[\mathbf{k}]^e = V \, [\mathbf{B}]^T [\mathbf{D}] [\mathbf{B}]
$$

(Closed form evaluated numerically.)

For $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ MPa, $\nu = 0.30$:

$$
\frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^5}{1.3 \times 0.4} = 3.846 \times 10^5 \text{ MPa}
$$

The resulting $[\mathbf{k}]^e$ is a $12 \times 12$ symmetric positive-definite matrix whose entries have units of $\text{N/mm}$ (or $\text{N/m}$).

> [!NOTE]
> The **closed-form result** for a regular tetrahedron of side $a$ and material with $E, \nu$ can be expressed compactly. The diagonal entry associated with a single DOF at a corner has magnitude of order $\sim \frac{E a}{6}$. This is a useful **sanity check** for hand computations.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: FEA Workflow for 3D Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Continuous 3D Domain (Ship Component)"] --> B["Discretize into Solid Elements (Tetra / Hexa)"]
    B --> C["Define Material Properties E, nu"]
    C --> D["Assign Boundary Conditions and Loads"]
    D --> E["Choose Shape Functions N_i(x,y,z)"]
    E --> F["Compute Strain-Displacement Matrix B"]
    F --> G["Form Constitutive Matrix D (3D)"]
    G --> H["Compute Jacobian Matrix J and Determinant"]
    H --> I["Numerical Integration: Gauss Quadrature over Volume"]
    I --> J["Evaluate Element Stiffness: k_e = integral B^T D B dV"]
    J --> K["Assemble Global Stiffness Matrix K"]
    K --> L["Apply Boundary Conditions"]
    L --> M["Solve K U = F for Nodal Displacements U"]
    M --> N["Recover Element Strains: eps = B U"]
    N --> O["Recover Element Stresses: sigma = D B U"]
    O --> P["Post-Process: von Mises, Principal Stresses"]
    P --> Q["Compare with Allowable Stress for Scantling"]

    stepA["Step 1: Pre-Process"] -.-> A
    stepA -.-> B
    stepA -.-> C
    stepA -.-> D
    stepA -.-> E

    stepB["Step 2: Solution"] -.-> F
    stepB -.-> G
    stepB -.-> H
    stepB -.-> I
    stepB -.-> J
    stepB -.-> K
    stepB -.-> L
    stepB -.-> M

    stepC["Step 3: Post-Process"] -.-> N
    stepC -.-> O
    stepC -.-> P
    stepC -.-> Q
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: 3D Element Types Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["3D Solid Elements"] --> T["Tetrahedral (4 nodes)"]
    S --> H["Hexahedral / Brick (8 nodes)"]
    S --> P["Pentahedral / Wedge (6 nodes)"]
    S --> Q["Pyramid (5 nodes)"]
    S --> H20["Higher-Order Brick (20 nodes)"]
    S --> T10["Higher-Order Tetra (10 nodes)"]

    T --> T1["Linear Shape Functions"]
    T1 --> T1A["Constant B Matrix"]
    T1A --> T1B["Exact 1-Point Gauss Integration"]
    T1B --> T1C["Cheapest but Lowest Accuracy"]

    H --> H1["Trilinear Shape Functions"]
    H1 --> H1A["Variable B within element"]
    H1A --> H1B["2x2x2 = 8 Point Gauss Integration"]
    H1B --> H1C["Most Common in Ship FE Analysis"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Data Flow inside a 3D Element

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NodePos["Node Coordinates x_i, y_i, z_i"] --> Jacobian
    MaterialProp["Material: E, nu"] --> Dmatrix["D Matrix 6x6"]
    GaussPts["Gauss Points and Weights xi_p, w_p"] --> Integration

    Jacobian["Jacobian J and detJ"] --> BMatrix
    BMatrix["Strain-Displacement B 6 x 3n"] --> Kassembly

    Dmatrix --> Kassembly
    Jacobian --> Integration
    BMatrix --> Integration
    Dmatrix --> Integration

    Integration["Numerical Integration over Volume"] --> Kassembly
    Kassembly["k_e = sum B^T D B detJ w_p"] --> Kglobal["Assemble into Global K"]
    Kglobal --> Solve["Solve K U = F"]
    Solve --> U["Nodal Displacements U"]
    U --> Strain["Strain eps = B U"]
    U --> Stress["Stress sigma = D eps = D B U"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BC["Boundary Conditions in 3D"] --> EBC["Essential / Dirichlet: u = u_bar on Surface S_u"]
    BC --> NBC["Natural / Neumann: traction t = sigma n on S_t"]
    BC --> Mixed["Mixed BC: combination of the two"]
    BC --> Symmetry["Symmetry Planes: reduce model by half or quarter"]
    BC --> Contact["Contact: nonlinear, multi-body interaction"]

    EBC --> EBC1["Example: Clamped bracket root - all 3 displacements = 0"]
    EBC --> EBC2["Example: Symmetry plane - one displacement component = 0"]

    NBC --> NBC1["Example: Hydrostatic pressure on hull plating"]
    NBC --> NBC2["Example: Wave load on ship side shell"]

    Symmetry --> Sym1["Mirror symmetry: 1/2 model"]
    Symmetry --> Sym2["Quarter symmetry: 1/4 model - if double symmetric"]
    Sym1 --> Reduce1["Reduces DOFs and CPU time by factor 4 to 8"]
    Sym2 --> Reduce2["Reduces DOFs and CPU time by factor 16"]
```
