# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between plane stress and plane strain assumptions. State the modified form of the constitutive matrix $[\mathbf{D}]$ in each case.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Plane Stress:** Assumes the out-of-plane normal stress is zero ($\sigma_z = 0$), applicable to thin plates and shells where the through-thickness direction is traction-free. In plane stress, $\varepsilon_z \neq 0$ (induced by Poisson's effect). The $[\mathbf{D}]$ matrix is $3 \times 3$:

$$
[\mathbf{D}]_{PS} = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & (1-\nu)/2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

- **Plane Strain:** Assumes the out-of-plane normal strain is zero ($\varepsilon_z = 0$), applicable to long structures of uniform cross-section (e.g., thick-walled pipes, dam cross-sections, long ship girders). Here $\sigma_z = \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y) \neq 0$. The $[\mathbf{D}]$ matrix is:

$$
[\mathbf{D}]_{PE} = \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)} \begin{bmatrix} 1-\nu & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1-\nu & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & (1-2\nu)/2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The 3D problem reduces to these 2D cases as special limiting scenarios. In naval architecture, plane stress is more common for hull plating, while plane strain applies to thick longitudinal girders.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List the components of the 3D stress and strain vectors used in FEA. Write the general form of the 3D constitutive matrix $[\mathbf{D}]$ for an isotropic material.**

**Model Answer:**

The 3D stress and strain vectors each contain 6 independent components (Voigt notation):

**Stress vector:** $\{\sigma\} = [\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z, \tau_{xy}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{zx}]^T$

**Strain vector:** $\{\varepsilon\} = [\varepsilon_x, \varepsilon_y, \varepsilon_z, \gamma_{xy}, \gamma_{yz}, \gamma_{zx}]^T$

where $\gamma_{xy} = 2\varepsilon_{xy}$ is the engineering shear strain.

**3D Isotropic Constitutive Matrix** $[\mathbf{D}]$:

$$
[\mathbf{D}] = \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)} \begin{bmatrix} 1-\nu & \nu & \nu & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ \nu & 1-\nu & \nu & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ \nu & \nu & 1-\nu & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & (1-2\nu)/2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & (1-2\nu)/2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & (1-2\nu)/2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The matrix is symmetric ($6 \times 6$) with 21 constants in the most general anisotropic case, reducing to 2 ($E$ and $\nu$) for isotropic materials.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the element stiffness matrix for a 4-noded tetrahedral (CST-extension to 3D) element. Clearly show the steps from shape function definition to the final $[\mathbf{k}]^e$ expression.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a single tetrahedral element of volume $V$ with constant strain matrix $[\mathbf{B}]$ and isotropic material having Young's modulus $E$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$, obtain the closed-form expression for the four corner terms of $[\mathbf{k}]^e$ associated with the $u$-DOF (i.e., the $u$-direction stiffness contributions from each node).

---

#### Model Solution (a) — Derivation:

**Step 1: Define shape functions.** Using natural (volume) coordinates for a tetrahedron with nodes 1, 2, 3, 4, the linear shape functions are:

$$
N_i(x,y,z) = \frac{1}{6V} \begin{vmatrix} 1 & x & y & z \\ 1 & x_j & y_j & z_j \\ 1 & x_k & y_k & z_k \\ 1 & x_m & y_m & z_m \end{vmatrix}, \quad i=1,2,3,4
$$

with $V = \frac{1}{6} \det \begin{bmatrix} x_j - x_i & y_j - y_i & z_j - z_i \\ x_k - x_i & y_k - y_i & z_k - z_i \\ x_m - x_i & y_m - y_i & z_m - z_i \end{bmatrix}$

**Step 2: Form the displacement field:**

$$
\{u\} = [\mathbf{N}]\{\mathbf{d}\}^e, \quad [\mathbf{N}] = \begin{bmatrix} N_1 & 0 & 0 & N_2 & 0 & 0 & N_3 & 0 & 0 & N_4 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & N_1 & 0 & 0 & N_2 & 0 & 0 & N_3 & 0 & 0 & N_4 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & N_1 & 0 & 0 & N_2 & 0 & 0 & N_3 & 0 & 0 & N_4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $[\mathbf{B}]$.** Since shape functions are linear, their derivatives are constants:

$$
[\mathbf{B}] = \begin{bmatrix} \partial N_1/\partial x & 0 & 0 & \partial N_2/\partial x & 0 & 0 & \cdots & \partial N_4/\partial x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \partial N_1/\partial y & 0 & 0 & \partial N_2/\partial y & 0 & \cdots & 0 & \partial N_4/\partial y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \partial N_1/\partial z & 0 & 0 & \partial N_2/\partial z & \cdots & 0 & 0 & \partial N_4/\partial z \\ \partial N_1/\partial y & \partial N_1/\partial x & 0 & \partial N_2/\partial y & \partial N_2/\partial x & 0 & \cdots & \partial N_4/\partial y & \partial N_4/\partial x & 0 \\ 0 & \partial N_1/\partial z & \partial N_1/\partial y & 0 & \partial N_2/\partial z & \partial N_2/\partial y & \cdots & 0 & \partial N_4/\partial z & \partial N_4/\partial y \\ \partial N_1/\partial z & 0 & \partial N_1/\partial x & \partial N_2/\partial z & 0 & \partial N_2/\partial x & \cdots & \partial N_4/\partial z & 0 & \partial N_4/\partial x \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the principle of minimum potential energy:**

$$
[\mathbf{k}]^e = \int_V [\mathbf{B}]^T [\mathbf{D}] [\mathbf{B}] \, dV
$$

**Step 5:** Since $[\mathbf{B}]$ and $[\mathbf{D}]$ are **constant** within the element, the integral collapses to:

$$
\boxed{[\mathbf{k}]^e = V \, [\mathbf{B}]^T [\mathbf{D}] [\mathbf{B}]}
$$

This is a $12 \times 12$ symmetric, positive-definite matrix (4 nodes × 3 DOF per node = 12 DOF per element).

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Closed Form for $u$-DOF Stiffness Terms:

For a tetrahedral element, the $u$-direction DOFs are entries 1, 4, 7, 10 of $\{\mathbf{d}\}^e$. The corresponding **3×3 sub-block** of $[\mathbf{k}]^e$ (mapping $u$-DOFs to $u$-DOFs) is:

$$
[\mathbf{k}]^e_{uu} = V \begin{bmatrix} \partial N_1/\partial x \\ \partial N_2/\partial x \\ \partial N_3/\partial x \\ \partial N_4/\partial x \end{bmatrix} \left( D_{11} \frac{\partial N_1}{\partial x} + D_{14} \frac{\partial N_1}{\partial y} + D_{16} \frac{\partial N_1}{\partial z}, \ldots \right)
$$

For an isotropic material, the off-diagonal terms $D_{14} = D_{16} = 0$ and $D_{11} = \lambda + 2G = \frac{E(1-\nu)}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}$. So:

$$
[\mathbf{k}]^e_{uu} = V (\lambda + 2G) \begin{bmatrix} \partial N_1/\partial x \\ \partial N_2/\partial x \\ \partial N_3/\partial x \\ \partial N_4/\partial x \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \partial N_1/\partial x & \partial N_2/\partial x & \partial N_3/\partial x & \partial N_4/\partial x \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $\lambda = \frac{E\nu}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}$ is the Lamé constant and $G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ is the shear modulus.

**Diagonal entry** (e.g., $k_{11}$ corresponding to $u_1$):

$$
\boxed{k_{11} = V (\lambda + 2G) \left( \frac{\partial N_1}{\partial x} \right)^2 = \frac{E(1-\nu)}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)} \, V \, \left( \frac{\partial N_1}{\partial x} \right)^2}
$$

**Off-diagonal entry** (e.g., $k_{12}$ between $u_1$ and $u_2$):

$$
k_{12} = V (\lambda + 2G) \frac{\partial N_1}{\partial x} \frac{\partial N_2}{\partial x}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the coupling: although the diagonal terms shown above isolate the $u$-$u$ coupling, in general there is **also** coupling with $v$ and $w$ DOFs through the off-diagonal entries of $[\mathbf{D}]$ (i.e., $D_{12}, D_{13}$, etc.) that contribute Poisson's effect.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the isoparametric formulation for an 8-noded hexahedral (brick) element. Show the trilinear shape functions and discuss the role of the Jacobian matrix in the isoparametric transformation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For an 8-noded hexahedral element, the element stiffness matrix is evaluated using $2 \times 2 \times 2$ Gauss quadrature. List the 8 Gauss point coordinates and weights. State the formula used to evaluate $[\mathbf{k}]^e$ in terms of the Gauss points and explain why this integration order is exact for the 8-noded brick.

---

#### Model Solution (a) — Isoparametric Formulation:

**Definition:** An **isoparametric element** uses the **same shape functions** to interpolate both the **geometry (coordinates)** and the **displacement field**.

For an 8-noded hexahedron with nodes $i = 1, 2, \ldots, 8$ at positions $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ and displacements $(u_i, v_i, w_i)$:

**Geometry interpolation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(\xi,\eta,\zeta) &= \sum_{i=1}^{8} N_i(\xi,\eta,\zeta) \, x_i \\
y(\xi,\eta,\zeta) &= \sum_{i=1}^{8} N_i(\xi,\eta,\zeta) \, y_i \\
z(\xi,\eta,\zeta) &= \sum_{i=1}^{8} N_i(\xi,\eta,\zeta) \, z_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Displacement interpolation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u(\xi,\eta,\zeta) &= \sum_{i=1}^{8} N_i(\xi,\eta,\zeta) \, u_i \\
v(\xi,\eta,\zeta) &= \sum_{i=1}^{8} N_i(\xi,\eta,\zeta) \, v_i \\
w(\xi,\eta,\zeta) &= \sum_{i=1}^{8} N_i(\xi,\eta,\zeta) \, w_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trilinear shape function** for corner node $i$ (with natural coordinates $\xi_i, \eta_i, \zeta_i = \pm 1$):

$$
\boxed{N_i(\xi,\eta,\zeta) = \frac{1}{8} (1 + \xi \xi_i)(1 + \eta \eta_i)(1 + \zeta \zeta_i)}
$$

**Role of the Jacobian:** The Jacobian matrix $[\mathbf{J}]$ maps the parent (natural) space $(\xi, \eta, \zeta)$ to the physical space $(x, y, z)$. It is essential because:

1. **Derivative transformation:** $\nabla_x N_i = [\mathbf{J}]^{-1} \nabla_\xi N_i$ — required to compute $[\mathbf{B}]$ in physical coordinates.
2. **Volume transformation:** $dV = \vert \mathbf{J}\vert \, d\xi \, d\eta \, d\zeta$ — required for the volume integral.
3. **Distortion detection:** When the element is severely distorted, $\det(\mathbf{J})$ can become zero or negative at interior points, signaling an invalid element.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a parallelepiped brick element, $[\mathbf{J}]$ is constant (independent of $\xi, \eta, \zeta$). In a general hexahedral element (curved or distorted sides), $[\mathbf{J}]$ varies, and its inverse must be computed at every Gauss point.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Gauss Quadrature for 8-Noded Brick:

**Gauss Points** (for $n_g = 2$ in each direction, on $\xi, \eta, \zeta \in [-1, 1]$):

$$
\xi_p = \eta_q = \zeta_r = \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx \pm 0.5773502692
$$

**Gauss Weights:**

$$
w_p = w_q = w_r = 1
$$

Total: $2 \times 2 \times 2 = 8$ sampling points inside the parent cube.

**Stiffness Matrix Formula:**

$$
[\mathbf{k}]^e = \sum_{p=1}^{2} \sum_{q=1}^{2} \sum_{r=1}^{2} w_p w_q w_r \, [\mathbf{B}(\xi_p, \eta_q, \zeta_r)]^T [\mathbf{D}] [\mathbf{B}(\xi_p, \eta_q, \zeta_r)] \, \vert \mathbf{J}(\xi_p, \eta_q, \zeta_r)\vert
$$

Substituting $w_p = w_q = w_r = 1$:

$$
[\mathbf{k}]^e = \sum_{p,q,r = 1}^{2} [\mathbf{B}(\xi_p, \eta_q, \zeta_r)]^T [\mathbf{D}] [\mathbf{B}(\xi_p, \eta_q, \zeta_r)] \, \vert \mathbf{J}\vert
$$

**Why is $2 \times 2 \times 2$ exact for the 8-noded brick?**

The integrand $[\mathbf{B}]^T[\mathbf{D}][\mathbf{B}] \vert \mathbf{J}\vert$ is a polynomial in $(\xi, \eta, \zeta)$.

- For a **rectangular (parallelepiped) brick**, $\vert \mathbf{J}\vert$ is constant, and the highest-degree term in $[\mathbf{B}]$ comes from the second derivative of trilinear shape functions (which are linear in each variable), giving integrand polynomial degree **2 in each of $\xi, \eta, \zeta$** — i.e., total degree $\leq 6$ in the worst case.
- $n$-point Gauss quadrature is exact for polynomials of degree $\leq 2n - 1$ in one dimension.
- $n = 2$ ⇒ exact for degree $\leq 3$ in each variable, which is **more than sufficient** to integrate the polynomial integrand exactly.

**Therefore:** $2 \times 2 \times 2 = 8$ Gauss points provide **exact integration** for the 8-noded brick element under parallelepiped geometry, and excellent accuracy for mildly distorted bricks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Many students incorrectly believe that $2 \times 2 \times 2$ Gauss integration is "approximate." It is **exact** for the 8-noded brick under rectangular geometry. Marks are deducted for stating otherwise.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** Some students forget to include the **Jacobian determinant** $\vert \mathbf{J}\vert$ in the stiffness integration formula. Always include it — without it, dimensional consistency is lost (the stiffness matrix will not have units of force/length).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — 3D FEA in Naval Architecture**

- ✅ **3D continuum has 6 stress and 6 strain components** (Voigt notation): $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z, \tau_{xy}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{zx}$ and $\varepsilon_x, \varepsilon_y, \varepsilon_z, \gamma_{xy}, \gamma_{yz}, \gamma_{zx}$.
- ✅ **Constitutive matrix $[\mathbf{D}]$ is $6 \times 6$, symmetric, and positive-definite** for isotropic materials; characterized by **two independent constants** $E$ and $\nu$.
- ✅ **Element stiffness formula:** $\boxed{[\mathbf{k}]^e = \int_{V_e} [\mathbf{B}]^T [\mathbf{D}] [\mathbf{B}] \, dV}$.
- ✅ **Tetrahedral (4-node, TET4):** Linear shape functions, **constant** $[\mathbf{B}]$, $12 \times 12$ stiffness, exact 1-point Gauss integration.
- ✅ **Hexahedral (8-node, HEX8 or BRICK8):** Trilinear shape functions, **variable** $[\mathbf{B}]$, $24 \times 24$ stiffness, exact $2 \times 2 \times 2$ Gauss integration.
- ✅ **Isoparametric formulation** uses the same shape functions for geometry and displacement — enables accurate modeling of curved boundaries (e.g., ship hull surfaces).
- ✅ **Jacobian matrix $[\mathbf{J}]$** is the linchpin of isoparametric mapping; $\vert \mathbf{J}\vert$ must be positive throughout the element for a valid mesh.
- ✅ **Gauss quadrature** with $n$ points exactly integrates polynomials of degree $\leq 2n-1$ in one dimension; for 3D, the product of 1D rules extends naturally.
- ✅ **Marine steel typical values:** $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ MPa, $\nu = 0.30$, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³.
- ✅ **Plane stress and plane strain are NOT 3D problems** — they are 2D specializations. Do not confuse them in exams.
- ✅ **For ship structural FE analysis**, the choice between TET4, HEX8, and higher-order elements depends on geometry complexity vs. computational cost; HEX8 is the **industry default** for most ship-class FE packages.
- ✅ **Symmetry boundary conditions** (using $u = 0$ or $v = 0$ on symmetry planes) can reduce the model size by 2×, 4×, or 8× — always exploit symmetry when possible.
- ✅ **Stress recovery** is done via $\sigma = DBU$ at the Gauss points (most accurate), then extrapolated to nodes for visualization.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convergence Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST753 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS IN NAVAL ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Convergence Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# CONVERGENCE CRITERIA IN FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence Criterion (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A finite element solution is said to *converge* to the exact (analytical) solution of the governing differential equation as the mesh is refined (h → 0) or as the polynomial order of the shape functions is increased (p → ∞), provided the chosen element family satisfies the **completeness** and **compatibility** requirements prescribed by the convergence theorems of FEA (Bathe, Zienkiewicz, Irons).

Mathematically, convergence is expressed as the requirement that the **discretization error norm** vanishes:

$$\lim_{h \to 0} \Vert \mathbf{u}_{FE} - \mathbf{u}_{exact} \Vert_{\Omega} = 0$$

where $\mathbf{u}_{FE}$ is the finite element displacement field and $\mathbf{u}_{exact}$ is the analytical solution over the domain $\Omega$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Zoom-In" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Painting a Ship Hull by Pixels:**
> Imagine you are digitally painting the cross-section of a ship's hull. If you use only 4 large pixels (coarse mesh), the curved bow is jagged and inaccurate. As you double the resolution to 16, then 64, then 1024 pixels (mesh refinement, i.e., $h \to 0$), the painted curve progressively *approaches* the true smooth hull line. **Convergence** is the mathematical guarantee that this "approaching" process actually reaches the true curve — and tells you *how fast* it gets there (the **rate of convergence**).

In Naval Architecture, this is critical: predicting the **stress concentration at a hatch corner** of a bulk carrier or the **buckling load of a stiffened panel** requires the analyst to know whether refining the mesh will truly reduce the error, and by how much.

---

## 1.3 Why Convergence Matters in Naval Architecture

In ship structural analysis (per IACS Common Structural Rules and DNV rules), the convergence of FE results is a **mandatory verification step**. A non-converged solution can lead to:

- **Unsafe under-prediction** of peak stresses at structural discontinuities (bracket toes, cut-outs, bilge corners)
- **Over-conservative over-design** that wastes steel tonnage (critical for lightweighting in modern vessel design)
- **Non-conservative fatigue life predictions** (S-N curve evaluations are highly sensitive to peak stress)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The convergence criteria for **membrane elements (CST, LST, Q4, Q8, Q9)** and **plate/shell elements (DKT, MITC)** used in ship hull analysis form the core of this module. The **h-method** and **p-method** are the two principal refinement pathways examined.

---

## 1.4 The Two-Path Refinement Framework

| Refinement Path | Variable | Action | Convergence Behaviour |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **h-Method** | Element size $h$ | Subdivide elements into smaller ones | Algebraic: $\mathcal{O}(h^p)$ |
| **p-Method** | Polynomial order $p$ | Increase shape function degree | Exponential for smooth problems |
| **hp-Method** | Both | Combined adaptive refinement | Exponential even with singularities |

---

## 1.5 Two Foundational Theorems for Convergence

**Theorem 1 — Completeness (Rigid Body + Constant Strain):**
A finite element formulation will converge to the exact solution if its displacement field can reproduce:
1. All **rigid body displacements** (3 translations + 3 rotations in 3-D)
2. All **constant strain states**

**Theorem 2 — Compatibility (Continuity):**
The shape functions must ensure **C⁰ continuity** (displacement continuity) across element boundaries for second-order PDEs (like plate bending and elasticity). C¹ continuity is required for thin-plate (Kirchhoff) elements.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — CONVERGENCE MECHANICS

## 2.1 The Patch Test (Bruce Irons, 1960s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Patch Test** is the *practical engineering standard* used to validate whether an assumed element formulation will converge. If an assemblage of distorted elements can reproduce an exact constant-strain state, the element passes the patch test and is **convergent**.

### Patch Test Variants
1. **Constant Strain Patch Test** — Apply boundary displacements that should produce uniform strain. FE must recover this exactly.
2. **Constant Curvature Patch Test** — For plate/shell elements, applied to verify correct bending recovery.
3. **Higher-Order Patch Tests** — For p-version elements.

For Naval Architecture, the patch test is routinely applied to verify quadrilateral shell elements (e.g., **MITC4, MITC9**) used in hull-girder and orthogrid analyses.

---

## 2.2 Hierarchical Requirements for Convergence

The necessary (and for many cases sufficient) conditions are:

### Condition A — Completeness of Displacement Field
The shape functions $\mathbf{N}$ must span:
$$\mathbf{u}_h = \mathbf{N}\mathbf{q}$$
where $\mathbf{q}$ are the nodal DOFs. The set $\mathbf{N}$ must include:
- **Rigid body modes** (so that an unstressed body remains unstressed under translation/rotation)
- **Constant strain states** (so that the stiffness does not depend on mesh size $h$ in a degenerate way)

### Condition B — Inter-element Continuity
At any shared edge/node $i$ between elements $A$ and $B$:
$$\mathbf{u}_A^{(i)} = \mathbf{u}_B^{(i)} \quad \forall \text{ nodes } i \text{ on common boundary}$$
For thin plates (4th order PDE), the **normal slope** $\partial \mathbf{u}/\partial n$ must also be continuous.

---

## 2.3 Convergence Rate — Algebraic Foundations

### 2.3.1 Error Norm Definitions

The discretization error in the **energy norm** is:
$$e_{energy} = \sqrt{\int_{\Omega} \boldsymbol{\sigma}^T \boldsymbol{\varepsilon} \, d\Omega - \int_{\Omega} \boldsymbol{\sigma}_h^T \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_h \, d\Omega}$$

The error in the **L₂ norm** (Euclidean displacement error):
$$\Vert e \Vert_{L_2} = \sqrt{\int_{\Omega} (\mathbf{u} - \mathbf{u}_h)^T (\mathbf{u} - \mathbf{u}_h) \, d\Omega}$$

### 2.3.2 Asymptotic Convergence Rates (h-Method)

If $u \in H^{p+1}(\Omega)$ (sufficiently smooth exact solution), then:

$$\Vert \mathbf{u} - \mathbf{u}_h \Vert_{L_2} \le C_1 h^{p+1}$$

$$\vert \mathbf{u} - \mathbf{u}_h \vert_{H^1} \le C_2 h^{p}$$

$$\vert a(\mathbf{u},\mathbf{u}) - a(\mathbf{u}_h,\mathbf{u}_h) \vert \le C_3 h^{2p}$$

where $C_1, C_2, C_3$ are constants independent of $h$ (but depending on the exact solution smoothness), and $p$ is the polynomial order of the shape functions.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Convergence Quick Reference

> [!NOTE]
> **The following cheat sheet is the official high-yield formula set expected in KTU 2024 Semester End Examinations.**

| # | Formula / Criterion | Physical Meaning | KTU Tag |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | $\Vert \mathbf{u} - \mathbf{u}_h\Vert_{L_2} = \mathcal{O}(h^{p+1})$ | Displacement error rate (h-method) | Must know |
| 2 | $\vert a(\mathbf{u})-a(\mathbf{u}_h)\vert = \mathcal{O}(h^{2p})$ | Energy norm convergence rate | Must know |
| 3 | $\sigma_{FE} = \sigma_{exact} + \mathcal{O}(h^p)$ | Stress convergence (one order less than displacement) | High yield |
| 4 | $\mathbf{K}_{patch}$ recovers constant strain | Patch test condition | High yield |
| 5 | $N_{min} = 3$ (membrane, 2D) | Minimum DOFs/node for CST | Must know |
| 6 | $N_{min} = 6$ (membrane, 2D) | Minimum DOFs/node for LST | Must know |
| 7 | $C^0$ continuity minimum | For 2nd order PDEs | Must know |
| 8 | $C^1$ continuity | For thin Kirchhoff plates | High yield |
| 9 | $h \to h/2 \Rightarrow$ error $\times 4$ for $p=1$ | Error reduction rule | Numerical problems |
| 10 | $h \to h/2 \Rightarrow$ error $\times 16$ for $p=2$ | Error reduction rule | Numerical problems |
| 11 | $RIC \ge 6$ (3D solid) | Rigid body inclusion check | High yield |
| 12 | $\lambda_{min}(\mathbf{K}) > 0$ | Positive-definiteness for convergence | Important |

---

## 2.5 Convergence Behaviour of Standard Elements (Naval Arch. Context)

| Element Type | DOF/Node | Polyn. Order $p$ | L₂ Error Rate | Application in Ship Structures |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **CST** (Constant Strain Triangle) | 2 (u,v) | 1 | $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$ | Coarse hull plating, preliminary analysis |
| **LST** (Linear Strain Triangle) | 2 (u,v) | 2 | $\mathcal{O}(h^3)$ | Refined hull plate stress |
| **Q4** (4-node quad) | 2 (u,v) | 1 | $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$ | Stiffened panel webs |
| **Q8** (8-node quad) | 2 (u,v) | 2 | $\mathcal{O}(h^3)$ | Bracket toes, transitions |
| **Q9** (9-node quad) | 2 (u,v) | 2 | $\mathcal{O}(h^3)$ | Hatch corner regions |
| **MITC4** shell | 6 | 1 (membrane) | $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$ | Global hull girder |
| **MITC9** shell | 6 | 2 (membrane) | $\mathcal{O}(h^3)$ | Local fine mesh details |

---

## 2.6 Lower and Upper Bound Theorems

These are crucial for naval architects (especially when validating scantling design):

### Lower Bound Theorem (Equilibrium / Stiffness Approach)
> A FE model that uses **only equilibrium-conforming** elements and **kinematically admissible** boundary conditions yields a **stiffness that is greater than or equal to the exact stiffness**.
> $$\mathbf{K}_{FE} \ge \mathbf{K}_{exact} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta_{FE} \le \delta_{exact}$$
> Therefore displacements are **under-predicted** (stiffer structure).

### Upper Bound Theorem (Flexibility / Compliant Approach)
> A FE model using **compatible (but not necessarily equilibrium-conforming)** elements yields displacements **greater than or equal to exact**.
> $$\delta_{FE} \ge \delta_{exact}$$
> Stresses are *not* necessarily bounded.

> [!WARNING]
> **Engineering Practice (DNV-RP-C205):** A converged FE solution should be **bracketed** by mesh refinement studies on both sides — coarse and fine — to ensure the result has stabilized. *Never trust a single-mesh answer for fatigue or ultimate strength assessment.*

---

## 2.7 Convergence at Stress Singularities (Critical for Naval Arch.)

Many ship structural details exhibit **singular stress fields**:
- Hatch corner cut-outs in bulk carriers
- Bracket toes connecting frames to plates
- Bilge knuckle transitions

Near a singularity (re-entrant corner with angle $\beta$), the stress behaves as:
$$\sigma(r) \sim K \cdot r^{\lambda - 1}, \quad \text{where } \lambda = 1 - \omega, \; \omega = \pi/\beta$$

Since $\lambda < 1$, the stress is **unbounded** as $r \to 0$, and the L₂ convergence rate degrades:
$$\Vert \mathbf{u} - \mathbf{u}_h \Vert_{L_2} = \mathcal{O}(h^{2\lambda}) \quad \text{(instead of } h^{p+1}\text{)}$$

**Practical Implication:** For a hatch corner with $\beta = 270°$, $\omega = 2\pi/3$ and $\lambda = 1/3$, so the displacement error rate becomes $\mathcal{O}(h^{2/3})$ — **much slower** than the smooth-region rate. This is why **stress extrapolation** or **p-refinement** is preferred near such singularities.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL WORKED EXAMPLES

## 3.1 Derivation: Convergence Rate from Taylor Expansion

### 3.1.1 Setup
Consider a 1-D bar element with linear shape functions (CST-like in 1-D). Let the exact displacement be $u(x)$ and the FE approximation be $u_h(x)$. For a single element of size $h$, expand $u(x)$ about the centroid $\bar{x}$:

$$u(x) = u(\bar{x}) + u'(\bar{x})(x - \bar{x}) + \frac{u''(\bar{x})}{2}(x - \bar{x})^2 + \frac{u'''(\bar{x})}{6}(x - \bar{x})^3 + \mathcal{O}(h^4)$$

The linear FE approximation captures only the first two terms:
$$u_h(x) = u(\bar{x}) + u'(\bar{x})(x - \bar{x})$$

The local truncation error (pointwise) is:
$$e(x) = u(x) - u_h(x) = \frac{u''(\bar{x})}{2}(x - \bar{x})^2 + \mathcal{O}(h^3)$$

### 3.1.2 Bound on the Pointwise Error
Since $\vert x - \bar{x} \vert \le h/2$:
$$\vert e(x) \vert \le \frac{\vert u''(\bar{x}) \vert}{2} \cdot \frac{h^2}{4} = \frac{\vert u''(\bar{x}) \vert}{8} h^2$$

This is the **pointwise** statement: **error is $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$** for linear elements.

### 3.1.3 Bound on the L₂ Norm
$$\Vert e \Vert^2_{L_2} = \int_0^h e^2(x) \, dx = \int_0^h \left(\frac{u''}{2}\right)^2 (x - \bar{x})^4 dx + \text{higher-order terms}$$

Evaluating the integral:
$$\int_0^h (x - \bar{x})^4 dx = \frac{h^5}{80}$$

So:
$$\Vert e \Vert^2_{L_2} = \frac{u''^2}{4} \cdot \frac{h^5}{80} + \mathcal{O}(h^7) = \frac{u''^2 h^5}{320} + \mathcal{O}(h^7)$$

Taking the square root:
$$\Vert e \Vert_{L_2} = \frac{\vert u'' \vert}{8\sqrt{5}} h^{5/2}$$

Wait — let me recheck the integration bounds. Using centroid $\bar{x} = h/2$, integration from 0 to $h$:

$$\int_0^h (x - h/2)^4 dx = \left[ \frac{(x-h/2)^5}{5} \right]_0^h = \frac{(h/2)^5}{5} - \frac{(-h/2)^5}{5} = \frac{2 h^5}{32 \cdot 5} = \frac{h^5}{80}$$

Thus:
$$\Vert e \Vert_{L_2} = \mathcal{O}(h^{5/2})$$

For an **element-level** error (per-element of size $h$), the L₂ error scales as $h^{5/2}$. When we consider a **global mesh** with $N$ elements on a fixed domain of length $L$ (so $h = L/N$), the global L₂ error scales differently because we sum over all elements:

$$\Vert u - u_h \Vert^2_{L_2, \Omega} = \sum_{e=1}^{N} \int_{\Omega_e} e^2 \, d\Omega$$

For the worst-case mesh (uniform), this gives $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$ per the standard interpolation theory (since the higher-order Taylor terms integrate down). The textbook statement $\Vert u - u_h \Vert_{L_2} = \mathcal{O}(h^{p+1})$ is the **global** rate for the standard interpolation estimate (Céa-like).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Convergence Study for a Plate Under Uniform Load

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Problem:** A simply-supported square plate (1 m × 1 m × 10 mm), material $E = 210$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$, uniform load $q = 1$ kPa. Use Q4 elements and verify convergence rate $p = 2$ in L₂ norm.

### Step 1: Analytical Solution (Timoshenko)
For a simply-supported plate under uniform load:
$$w_{max} = \alpha \cdot \frac{q a^4}{D}, \quad \text{where } D = \frac{E h^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}, \; \alpha = 0.00406$$

Compute $D$:
$$D = \frac{210 \times 10^9 \cdot (0.01)^3}{12 \cdot (1 - 0.09)} = \frac{210 \times 10^9 \cdot 10^{-6}}{10.92} = 19230 \, \text{N·m}$$

Compute $w_{max}$:
$$w_{max} = 0.00406 \cdot \frac{1000 \cdot 1}{19230} = 0.00406 \cdot 5.2 \times 10^{-2} = 2.11 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m} = 0.211 \, \text{mm}$$

### Step 2: FE Convergence Data (4 Mesh Densities)

| Mesh | $h$ (m) | DOFs | $w_{max}$ (mm) | $\vert w_{FE} - w_{exact} \vert$ (mm) | $h^2$ |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| 2×2 | 0.500 | 9 | 0.169 | 0.0420 | 0.250 |
| 4×4 | 0.250 | 25 | 0.198 | 0.0130 | 0.0625 |
| 8×8 | 0.125 | 81 | 0.208 | 0.0034 | 0.0156 |
| 16×16 | 0.0625 | 289 | 0.2105 | 0.00085 | 0.00391 |

### Step 3: Verify the Rate

Compute the **convergence rate** from two successive meshes using:
$$r = \frac{\log(e_{2}/e_{1})}{\log(h_{2}/h_{1})}$$

For 4×4 → 8×8:
$$r = \frac{\log(0.0034/0.0130)}{\log(0.125/0.250)} = \frac{\log(0.2615)}{\log(0.5)} = \frac{-0.5825}{-0.3010} = 1.935$$

For 8×8 → 16×16:
$$r = \frac{\log(0.00085/0.0034)}{\log(0.0625/0.125)} = \frac{\log(0.25)}{\log(0.5)} = \frac{-0.602}{-0.301} = 2.000$$

> **Conclusion:** Asymptotic rate converges to **2.0**, confirming $\Vert e \Vert_{L_2} = \mathcal{O}(h^2)$ for Q4 elements, as expected for $p = 1$ shape functions.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Error Reduction by Element Subdivision

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Problem:** A ship deck plate model currently uses 4×4 Q4 elements. The maximum stress error is found to be 8.4% (vs. fine-mesh reference). To reduce error below 2%, how many elements per side are required?

### Step 1: Apply the Convergence Rate Formula

For Q4 elements, the stress error reduces as:
$$e_{stress} = C \cdot h^{p} = C \cdot h^{1} \quad (p = 1)$$

When the mesh is refined from $h_1$ to $h_2$ (with element counts $N_1 \to N_2$):
$$\frac{e_2}{e_1} = \left(\frac{h_2}{h_1}\right)^p = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^p$$

### Step 2: Insert Known Values

$$e_1 = 8.4\% \text{ at } N_1 = 4, \quad e_2 = 2\% \text{ at } N_2 = ?, \quad p = 1$$

$$\frac{2.0}{8.4} = \left(\frac{4}{N_2}\right)^1$$

$$0.2381 = \frac{4}{N_2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_2 = \frac{4}{0.2381} = 16.8$$

### Step 3: Round Up Conservatively

Choose $N_2 = 20$ elements per side to provide a safety margin. **Verification:**
$$e_{new} = 8.4\% \cdot \frac{4}{20} = 8.4\% \cdot 0.2 = 1.68\% < 2\% \;\; \checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Why h-Refinement Reduces Error by Factor of $4^p$

Suppose the mesh is uniformly refined: $h \to h/2$. Element count becomes $4\times$ (in 2D). The error in energy norm:
$$e_{energy}(h/2) = \left(\frac{h/2}{h}\right)^p e_{energy}(h) = \frac{e_{energy}(h)}{2^p}$$

For $p = 1$ (linear): error reduces by factor **2** (i.e., 50%)
For $p = 2$ (quadratic): error reduces by factor **4** (i.e., 75% reduction)

For displacement (L₂ norm):
$$e_{L_2}(h/2) = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{p+1} e_{L_2}(h)$$

For $p = 1$: factor **4** reduction in displacement error
For $p = 2$: factor **8** reduction in displacement error

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Stiffness Approach (Lower Bound) Verification

> **Problem:** A frame element beam (Young's modulus $E$, length $L$, moment of inertia $I$) is modeled using (a) Euler-Bernoulli beam theory element, (b) Timoshenko beam element with shear locking, (c) Timoshenko beam element with selective reduced integration. Show which converges to the exact stiffness.

### Exact Stiffness (Cantilever, tip-loaded $P$):
$$\delta_{exact} = \frac{P L^3}{3 EI}$$

### (a) Euler-Bernoulli FE:
$$\delta_{EB} = \frac{P L^3}{3 EI} \cdot \text{(no error in displacement for linear }P\text{)}$$
For higher-order loadings, error is $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$. **Convergent, lower bound** if used with consistent mass and proper integration.

### (b) Timoshenko with Full Integration (Shear Locking):
$$\delta_{T} = \frac{P L^3}{3 EI + (something\ large)} \ll \delta_{exact}$$
**Convergent** but **excessively stiff** for thin beams ($L/h \to \infty$). Not useful in practice. **This demonstrates that convergence alone is not sufficient — practical accuracy matters.**

### (c) Timoshenko with Selective Reduced Integration:
$$\delta_{SR} = \frac{P L^3}{3 EI} \cdot (1 + \mathcal{O}(h^2))$$
**Optimal convergence**, no locking. This is the standard choice in commercial codes (ANSYS BEAM188, NASTRan CBAR).

> [!NOTE]
> **Lesson for Naval Architects:** Always check that the chosen element type converges *and* is not locked. Locked elements give **non-conservative** stiffness and **inaccurate** stress fields.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS — CONVERGENCE FRAMEWORK

## 4.1 Convergence Verification Flow (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the complete convergence verification workflow used in ship structural analysis per KTU/Class guidelines:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Structural Problem] --> B[Choose Element Family]
    B --> C{Are Shape Functions Complete?}
    C -- No --> D[Reject Element]
    C -- Yes --> E{Are Rigid Body Modes Spanned?}
    E -- No --> D
    E -- Yes --> F{Compatible Inter-element?}
    F -- No --> D
    F -- Yes --> G[Element Passes Patch Test]
    G --> H[Build Coarse Mesh]
    H --> I[Run Analysis]
    I --> J[Compute Reference Solution]
    J --> K[Refine Mesh h/2]
    K --> L[Run Analysis 2]
    L --> M{Error Reduction Matches h to Power p?}
    M -- No --> N[Check Boundary Conditions / Mesh Topology]
    N --> K
    M -- Yes --> O[Plot Log-Log Convergence Curve]
    O --> P{Slope = p or p+1 in L2?}
    P -- No --> Q[Investigate Singularities / Distortion]
    P -- Yes --> R[Convergence VERIFIED]
    R --> S[Report Element h_max to Class]
    S --> T[End]
```

**Block-Level Interpretation:**

| Block | Function |
|:--|:--|
| A → B | Problem definition; e.g., hatch corner stress |
| C → E → F | Theoretical gatekeeping (completeness, RBM, compatibility) |
| G | Patch test passes for the chosen family (e.g., MITC4) |
| H → L | Mesh refinement study (3–5 levels typically) |
| M → O | Compute and verify theoretical convergence rate |
| P | Log-log slope confirms theoretical rate |
| R → S | Documentation for class approval (DNV/ABS/LR) |

---

## 4.2 Element Hierarchy — Convergence Quality

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CST - Order 1 - Linear] -->|O to h squared| B[LST - Order 2 - Parabolic]
    B -->|O to h cubed| C[Q9 - Order 2 - Serendipity+]
    C -->|O to h cubed| D[Q8 - Order 2 - Serendipity]
    A -->|Locking prone| E[Beam with full integration]
    E -->|SRI fix| F[Beam with selective reduced integration]
    F -->|Optimal convergence| G[Verified Convergent Element]
    B --> G
    C --> G
    D --> G
```

---

## 4.3 Convergence Plot — Schematic (Rate Illustration)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Log-Log Convergence Plot (Schematic)"
    x-axis "log h" [0.5, 0.25, 0.125, 0.0625, 0.03125]
    y-axis "log Error" [-5, -4, -3, -2, -1, 0]
    line [0.0, -1.5, -3.0, -4.5, -6.0]
```

> **Reading the Plot:** A straight line on log-log axes indicates power-law convergence. The slope of the line equals the convergence rate $p$ (for energy norm) or $p+1$ (for L₂ norm).

---

## 4.4 Failure Mode Diagram — Why Convergence May Fail

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Convergence Failure] --> B{Symptom?}
    B -->|Error grows on refinement| C[Element Incompatibility]
    B -->|Error oscillates| D[Hourglassing / Locking]
    B -->|No error reduction at all| E[Incomplete Shape Functions]
    B -->|Slow convergence| F[Stress Singularity]
    C --> G[Use compatible formulation]
    D --> H[Use reduced integration or EAS]
    E --> I[Increase DOFs per element]
    F --> J[Apply p-refinement near singularity]
    G --> K[Re-run Convergence Study]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Convergence Achieved]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
> **State the necessary conditions for a finite element formulation to converge to the exact solution. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A finite element formulation must satisfy the following two necessary (and sufficient for most standard problems) convergence conditions:
1. **Completeness:** The shape functions must be able to represent all **rigid body displacements** (zero strain states) and all **constant strain states** within the element. This guarantees that as $h \to 0$, the element stiffness approaches the correct value.
2. **Compatibility:** The displacement field must be continuous across element boundaries. For 2nd-order PDEs (plane stress, plane strain, plate bending with Mindlin/Reissner theory), **C⁰ continuity** (continuity of displacements) is sufficient. For 4th-order PDEs (Kirchhoff thin plates), **C¹ continuity** (continuity of displacement and its normal derivative) is required.

Additionally, **coordinate system invariance** and **isotropic material representation** are required for elements used in 3D elasticity.

---

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
> **Explain the Patch Test in finite element analysis. Why is it important in naval structural analysis? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The **Patch Test** (Bruce Irons, late 1960s) is a numerical consistency check applied to finite element formulations. A patch of arbitrarily distorted elements is subjected to prescribed boundary displacements that correspond to a constant strain (or constant curvature) state. If the FE solution recovers this exact strain/curvature state throughout the patch, the element is said to **pass the patch test** and is *guaranteed to converge*.

In **naval structural analysis**, the patch test is critically important because:
- Ship hull meshes contain **highly distorted elements** due to the curved hull form (especially at the bow and bilge)
- Standard ship scantling rules (IACS CSR, DNV) require **mesh quality and convergence verification**
- Non-patch-test-passing elements can give **erroneous stress predictions** that compromise safety assessments

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module 2 Internal Choice: 14 Marks each)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Derive the asymptotic convergence rate of the displacement error for a 1-D linear finite element. State the rate of convergence in **L₂ norm** and **energy norm** for an element of polynomial order $p$. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** A ship deck plate (1 m × 1 m, simply-supported, uniform pressure $q = 5$ kPa) is analyzed using Q4 elements. Convergence studies yield the following data:
>
> | Mesh | $h$ (mm) | $w_{center}$ (mm) |
> |:--|:--|:--|
> | 4×4 | 250 | 0.0821 |
> | 8×8 | 125 | 0.0954 |
> | 16×16 | 62.5 | 0.0991 |
> | 32×32 | 31.25 | 0.10005 |
>
> The exact (analytical) deflection at the center is $w_{exact} = 0.1003$ mm.
> Compute the error at each mesh level. Verify the asymptotic convergence rate and discuss what this implies about the choice of element size for engineering use. (7 Marks)

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Convergence Rate (7 Marks)

Consider a 1-D domain $[0,L]$ with exact solution $u(x) \in H^2([0,L])$ (at least twice-differentiable in the Sobolev sense). The FE solution $u_h(x)$ uses $N$ linear elements of size $h = L/N$.

**Step 1:** Express the local error using Taylor's theorem about the midpoint $\bar{x}_e$ of element $e$:
$$u(x) = u(\bar{x}_e) + u'(\bar{x}_e)(x - \bar{x}_e) + \frac{u''(\bar{x}_e)}{2}(x - \bar{x}_e)^2 + \mathcal{O}(h^3)$$

The linear FE approximation on element $e$ is:
$$u_h(x) = u(\bar{x}_e) + u'(\bar{x}_e)(x - \bar{x}_e)$$

The local error is:
$$e(x) = u(x) - u_h(x) = \frac{u''(\bar{x}_e)}{2}(x - \bar{x}_e)^2 + \mathcal{O}(h^3)$$

**Step 2:** Bounding the pointwise error. Since $\vert x - \bar{x}_e \vert \le h/2$:
$$\vert e(x) \vert \le \frac{\Vert u'' \Vert_{\infty}}{8} h^2$$

This establishes **pointwise convergence** as $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$.

**Step 3:** Global L₂ norm bound. Summing the squared error over all $N = L/h$ elements:
$$\Vert u - u_h \Vert^2_{L_2} = \sum_{e=1}^{N} \int_{\Omega_e} e^2 \, d\Omega \le \sum_e \left(\frac{\Vert u'' \Vert^2_{\infty}}{64}\right) h^4 \cdot h = \frac{L \Vert u'' \Vert^2_{\infty}}{64} h^3$$

Taking the square root:
$$\Vert u - u_h \Vert_{L_2} \le C_1 h^{3/2} \quad \text{(per-element sense)}$$

**Generalisation (Aubin–Nitsche + Standard Interpolation Estimate):** For polynomial order $p$ shape functions on a smooth domain, the standard FEM theory (Babuška, Zlamal) gives the **global** error estimates:

$$\boxed{\Vert u - u_h \Vert_{L_2(\Omega)} \le C_1 h^{p+1}}$$

$$\boxed{\vert u - u_h \vert_{H^1(\Omega)} \le C_2 h^{p}}$$

$$\boxed{\vert a(u,u) - a(u_h,u_h) \vert^{1/2} \le C_3 h^{p} \quad \text{(energy norm)}}$$

The energy norm error is one order lower than the L₂ norm. This is the **standard textbook result** (Bathe, Zienkiewicz & Taylor).

---

#### Part (b) — Convergence Rate Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Compute the displacement error $e = \vert w_{FE} - w_{exact} \vert$ at each mesh:

| Mesh | $h$ (mm) | $w_{FE}$ (mm) | $w_{exact}$ (mm) | $e = \vert w_{FE} - w_{exact}\vert$ (mm) |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| 4×4 | 250 | 0.0821 | 0.1003 | **0.0182** |
| 8×8 | 125 | 0.0954 | 0.1003 | **0.0049** |
| 16×16 | 62.5 | 0.0991 | 0.1003 | **0.0012** |
| 32×32 | 31.25 | 0.10005 | 0.1003 | **0.00025** |

**Step 2:** Compute the asymptotic convergence rate between successive meshes using:
$$r = \frac{\log(e_{i+1}/e_i)}{\log(h_{i+1}/h_i)}$$

For 4×4 → 8×8:
$$r_1 = \frac{\log(0.0049/0.0182)}{\log(125/250)} = \frac{\log(0.2692)}{\log(0.5)} = \frac{-0.5702}{-0.3010} = 1.894$$

For 8×8 → 16×16:
$$r_2 = \frac{\log(0.0012/0.0049)}{\log(62.5/125)} = \frac{\log(0.2449)}{\log(0.5)} = \frac{-0.6110}{-0.3010} = 2.030$$

For 16×16 → 32×32:
$$r_3 = \frac{\log(0.00025/0.0012)}{\log(31.25/62.5)} = \frac{\log(0.2083)}{\log(0.5)} = \frac{-0.6812}{-0.3010} = 2.263$$

**Step 3:** Asymptotic rate → **2.0** as $h \to 0$, confirming $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$ L₂ convergence for Q4 (linear, $p=1$) elements.

**Step 4:** Engineering conclusion. The asymptotic rate of 2.0 matches the theoretical prediction for Q4 elements. For engineering use:
- **16×16 mesh** (62.5 mm elements) gives error **1.2%** — adequate for general strength assessment
- **32×32 mesh** (31.25 mm elements) gives error **0.25%** — required for fatigue assessment per DNV-RP-C205
- **Pre-asymptotic regime** (coarse meshes 4×4) shows lower apparent rate — asymptotic behaviour only kicks in for sufficiently refined meshes

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. **Forgetting to use absolute values** when computing the error (sign of error doesn't matter; magnitude does)
> 2. **Computing the rate using non-adjacent meshes** (always use consecutive refinement levels)
> 3. **Confusing displacement and stress rates** — stresses converge at rate $h^p$, not $h^{p+1}$
> 4. **Ignoring the pre-asymptotic regime** — the rate stabilizes only when $h$ is small enough

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme — Alternative Choice]**
> **(a)** With reference to the **h-method** and **p-method** of finite element refinement, explain the convergence behaviour in each case. Include the mathematical statement of the convergence rate and discuss the practical advantages/disadvantages in the context of hull structural analysis. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** A re-entrant corner (hatch corner of a bulk carrier) has an included angle $\beta = 270°$. Using the stress singularity formula $\sigma(r) \sim K \cdot r^{\lambda - 1}$ with $\lambda = 1 - \pi/\beta$:
>   1. Compute the singularity exponent $\lambda$.
>   2. Determine the **rate of displacement convergence** near this corner using the singular-solution formula $\Vert e \Vert_{L_2} = \mathcal{O}(h^{2\lambda})$.
>   3. If $p = 2$ (quadratic elements) are used away from the singularity, compare the convergence rate in the smooth region versus the singular region. What practical recommendation follows for the naval architect? (7 Marks)

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — h-Method vs p-Method (7 Marks)

**The h-method (mesh refinement):**
The characteristic element size $h$ is reduced by subdividing existing elements. For a polynomial order $p$ shape function, the convergence rate is **algebraic**:

$$\Vert \mathbf{u} - \mathbf{u}_h \Vert_{L_2} = \mathcal{O}(h^{p+1})$$

For Q4 ($p=1$): rate $= h^2$; for Q8 ($p=2$): rate $= h^3$. Each $h \to h/2$ refinement increases DOFs by **4×** (in 2D) and reduces error by **factor of 4** (linear) or **8** (quadratic).

**The p-method (polynomial enrichment):**
The element size is held fixed, but the polynomial order of the shape functions is increased. For sufficiently smooth problems, the convergence is **exponential** (geometric):

$$\Vert \mathbf{u} - \mathbf{u}_h \Vert_{L_2} = \mathcal{O}\left( e^{-\gamma \sqrt[3]{N}} \right)$$

where $\gamma$ is a problem-dependent constant and $N$ is the DOF count. Doubling the DOFs through p-refinement typically reduces the error by **one to two orders of magnitude**.

**The hp-method (combined):**
In regions where the solution is smooth, p-refinement is used. Near singularities, h-refinement is applied. The combination yields **exponential convergence** even with stress singularities — the gold standard for adaptive mesh refinement.

| Method | Rate Type | Cost per Halving | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **h-method** | Algebraic | 4× DOFs | Simple, robust | Slow near singularities |
| **p-method** | Exponential (smooth) | 2× DOFs | Very fast for smooth problems | Sensitive to element shape |
| **hp-method** | Exponential (anywhere) | 4× DOFs | Best of both worlds | Complex implementation |

**In naval structural analysis:**
- **h-method** is universally used (in commercial codes like ANSYS, ABAQUS, NASTRAN) for ship hull meshing
- **p-method** is rare due to mesh distortion sensitivity (hull forms have highly distorted elements)
- **hp-method** is used in research codes (e.g., STRIPEK by Szabo) for specialised applications like stress intensity factor computation

---

#### Part (b) — Stress Singularity Analysis (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Compute the singularity exponent.

Given $\beta = 270° = \frac{3\pi}{2}$ radians:
$$\lambda = 1 - \frac{\pi}{\beta} = 1 - \frac{\pi}{3\pi/2} = 1 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 2:** Determine the displacement convergence rate.

The asymptotic convergence rate near a stress singularity is governed by:
$$\Vert e \Vert_{L_2} = \mathcal{O}(h^{2\lambda}) = \mathcal{O}(h^{2/3}) = \mathcal{O}(h^{0.667})$$

For comparison, the smooth-region rate (for $p = 2$ elements):
$$\Vert e \Vert_{L_2, smooth} = \mathcal{O}(h^{p+1}) = \mathcal{O}(h^{3}) = \mathcal{O}(h^{3.0})$$

**Step 3:** Comparison table and engineering recommendations.

| Region | Convergence Rate | Slope (Log-Log) | Required DOF Reduction for 10× Error |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| Smooth bulkhead plating (p=2) | $h^{3.0}$ | 3.0 | $h \to h/2.15$ |
| Near re-entrant corner ($270°$) | $h^{0.667}$ | 0.667 | $h \to h/0.1$ (very expensive!) |

> **Practical Recommendation:**
> 1. **Avoid** relying on h-refinement alone near re-entrant corners — it is computationally prohibitive (very slow convergence).
> 2. **Use p-refinement** (e.g., quadratic or cubic elements) in a small radius around the singularity to recover exponential convergence.
> 3. **Alternative:** Use **stress extrapolation** from a coarse mesh (e.g., linear extrapolation using two mesh levels to estimate the singular stress amplitude $K$).
> 4. **Class recommendation:** Per DNV-RP-C205, the stress at a singular point should be computed using a **locally refined mesh** combined with **p-refinement** or **sif (stress intensity factor) extraction** rather than naive h-refinement.
> 5. In practice, naval architects often use the **sub-modelling** (or **zooming**) technique: a coarse global hull mesh, with a refined sub-model at the hatch corner using higher-order elements.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Common mistakes on this question type:
> 1. **Angle conversion error:** Forgetting to convert $\beta = 270°$ to radians ($\beta = 3\pi/2$)
> 2. **Formula confusion:** Some students use $\lambda = \pi/\beta$ instead of $\lambda = 1 - \pi/\beta$
> 3. **Confusing $h^{2\lambda}$ with $h^{\lambda}$:** The L₂ norm squared carries the factor 2 (variance of Sobolev norm)
> 4. **Not discussing engineering remedies** — a complete answer must include the practical recommendation, not just the math

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence Criteria — Key Points to Remember (KTU 2024 Module 2)**

### Foundational Concepts
- [ ] Convergence = FE solution approaches the exact solution as mesh is refined ($h \to 0$)
- [ ] Two required conditions: **Completeness** (rigid body modes + constant strain) and **Compatibility** (inter-element continuity)
- [ ] **Patch Test** (Irons) is the practical validation tool
- [ ] For 2nd-order PDEs: **C⁰** continuity is sufficient; for thin plates: **C¹** continuity is needed

### Convergence Rates — Must Memorise
- [ ] L₂ displacement error: $\mathcal{O}(h^{p+1})$
- [ ] Energy / H¹ norm error: $\mathcal{O}(h^{p})$
- [ ] Stress error: $\mathcal{O}(h^p)$ (one order less than displacement)
- [ ] Element subdivision $h \to h/2$ reduces error by factor $4^p$ (energy) or $2^{p+1}$ (L₂)
- [ ] Linear element ($p=1$): halving $h$ → error × 0.25 in L₂ norm
- [ ] Quadratic element ($p=2$): halving $h$ → error × 0.125 in L₂ norm

### Element-Specific Rates (Naval Arch. Context)
- [ ] CST, Q4: $p=1$, L₂ rate $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$
- [ ] LST, Q8, Q9: $p=2$, L₂ rate $\mathcal{O}(h^3)$
- [ ] MITC4 shell: $p=1$, rate $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$
- [ ] MITC9 shell: $p=2$, rate $\mathcal{O}(h^3)$

### h-Method vs p-Method vs hp-Method
- [ ] **h-method**: algebraic convergence, simple but slow near singularities
- [ ] **p-method**: exponential convergence for smooth problems, sensitive to distortion
- [ ] **hp-method**: exponential even with singularities, complex implementation

### Singularities (Hatch Corners, Bracket Toes)
- [ ] Re-entrant corner singularity: $\sigma(r) \sim K \cdot r^{\lambda - 1}$
- [ ] Exponent: $\lambda = 1 - \pi/\beta$ where $\beta$ is the included angle
- [ ] 270° corner: $\lambda = 1/3$, displacement rate becomes $\mathcal{O}(h^{2/3})$
- [ ] **Practical fix:** p-refinement, stress extrapolation, or sub-modelling

### Bound Theorems
- [ ] **Lower bound (equilibrium / stiffness):** $\mathbf{K}_{FE} \ge \mathbf{K}_{exact}$, $\delta_{FE} \le \delta_{exact}$
- [ ] **Upper bound (flexibility):** $\delta_{FE} \ge \delta_{exact}$
- [ ] A converged solution must be **bracketed** by successive refinements

### Common Failure Modes
- [ ] **Shear locking** in Timoshenko beams with full integration → use SRI
- [ ] **Hourglassing** in under-integrated elements → use EAS or physical stabilization
- [ ] **Non-convergent elements** (no rigid body modes) → reject
- [ ] **Pre-asymptotic regime** → requires fine enough mesh for theory to hold

### KTU Exam Essentials
- [ ] Convergence rate computation: $r = \log(e_{i+1}/e_i) / \log(h_{i+1}/h_i)$
- [ ] Required mesh size for target error: $N_2 = N_1 \cdot (e_1/e_2)^{1/p}$
- [ ] Always verify with **at least 3 mesh levels** for credible convergence proof

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Equations for Calculation of Stiffness Matrix
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST753 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS IN NAVAL ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** General Equations for Calculation of Stiffness Matrix

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the Finite Element Analysis (FEA) of ship and offshore structures, the **Stiffness Matrix** (also called the **Element Stiffness Matrix**, denoted $[K]^e$) is a symmetric, positive-definite square matrix that mathematically encodes the relationship between nodal displacements and the equivalent nodal forces acting on a discretized structural element. It is formally derived from the **Principle of Minimum Potential Energy (PMP)**, which is the variational foundation upon which the entire FEA framework rests.

The general equation governing the computation of the element stiffness matrix is:

$$[K]^e = \int_{V_e} [B]^T [D] [B] \, dV$$

where:
- $[B]$ = **Strain-Displacement Matrix** (relates strains to nodal displacements)
- $[D]$ = **Material Constitutive Matrix** (relates stresses to strains, also called the elasticity matrix)
- $V_e$ = **Volume of the finite element**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The formulation $[K]^e = \int [B]^T[D][B]\,dV$ is a **board-favorite derivation** and has been asked in multiple university examinations under Module 2. Students must memorize both the matrix definitions and the integration logic.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spring Network of a Ship Hull"

Imagine a massive ship hull floating in water. Now imagine that instead of treating the hull as one continuous solid, you mentally divide it into a **jigsaw puzzle of small flat plates and beams** (the finite elements), and at the corners of each puzzle piece, you plant imaginary "pegs" (the **nodes**).

When the ocean pushes the hull, the hull deforms. Each peg moves slightly. The **stiffness matrix** is essentially a giant, organized ledger that says:

> *"If I push peg #3 by 1 millimeter in the vertical direction, how much force must I apply at peg #7 to balance it, and at all other pegs as well?"*

Each entry $K_{ij}$ in the matrix is a measure of how strongly node $i$ resists a unit displacement imposed at node $j$. The diagonal entries $K_{ii}$ represent the **self-stiffness** of a node (its direct resistance to displacement), while off-diagonal entries $K_{ij}$ represent **coupling stiffnesses** (how forces are transferred between connected nodes).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Naval FEA

- **Young's Modulus (E)** for marine steel: typically **$\mathbf{E = 210 \text{ GPa}}$**
- **Poisson's Ratio (ν)** for marine steel: typically **$\mathbf{\nu = 0.30}$**
- **Shear Modulus (G)** for marine steel: **$\mathbf{G = 80.77 \text{ GPa}}$**
- **Yield Strength (σ_y)** for typical AH36 shipbuilding steel: **$\mathbf{355 \text{ MPa}}$**
- **Standard units in FEA output**: **N (Newtons)** for forces, **mm** for displacements, **MPa (N/mm²)** for stresses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in Naval Architecture:** Ship hulls, offshore platforms, and submarine pressure hulls are analyzed using FEA to predict deflection, stress distribution, and buckling behavior under wave loads, hydrostatic pressure, and cargo loading. The stiffness matrix is the mathematical engine that powers all such analyses.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Variational Foundation — Step-by-Step Logic

The stiffness matrix is not pulled out of thin air. It emerges from a rigorous variational principle. The derivation follows a clear, logical chain of six sequential steps:

**Step 1 — Total Potential Energy (Π) of the System**
The total potential energy of a structural element is the sum of **strain energy** (U) stored in the deformed body and the **work done by external forces** (W).

$$\Pi = U - W = \frac{1}{2} \int_{V_e} \{\sigma\}^T \{\varepsilon\} \, dV - \int_{V_e} \{u\}^T \{b\} \, dV - \int_{S_e} \{u\}^T \{t\} \, dS$$

Here, $\{b\}$ is the body force vector (e.g., self-weight) and $\{t\}$ is the surface traction vector (e.g., hydrostatic pressure on the hull).

**Step 2 — Discretization via Shape Functions**
The continuous displacement field $\{u\}$ within an element is approximated using **shape functions** $[N]$ multiplied by the nodal displacement vector $\{d\}^e$:

$$\{u(x,y,z)\} = [N(x,y,z)] \{d\}^e$$

> *"Why shape functions?"* — Because we cannot solve for infinite degrees of freedom. Shape functions act as **mathematical bridges** that interpolate displacements between discrete nodes, reducing the problem to a finite set of unknowns.

**Step 3 — Strain-Displacement Relation**
Strains are spatial derivatives of displacements. Applying the appropriate differential operator matrix $[L]$ to the displacement field:

$$\{\varepsilon\} = [L]\{u\} = [L][N]\{d\}^e = [B]\{d\}^e$$

This defines the **Strain-Displacement Matrix** $[B] = [L][N]$, which is a fundamental building block of FEA.

**Step 4 — Constitutive (Stress-Strain) Relation**
For linear elastic materials (assumed for most ship steel analysis), Hooke's law gives:

$$\{\sigma\} = [D]\{\varepsilon\} = [D][B]\{d\}^e$$

The matrix $[D]$ contains the material properties (E and ν) and is constant for homogeneous materials.

**Step 5 — Substituting Back into the Energy Equation**
Plugging the discretized $\{\varepsilon\}$ and $\{\sigma\}$ into the strain energy expression:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} \int_{V_e} (\{d\}^e)^T [B]^T [D] [B] \{d\}^e \, dV$$

**Step 6 — Applying the Stationarity Condition (δΠ = 0)**
The Principle of Minimum Potential Energy states that a structure deforms such that the total potential energy is minimized. Taking the variational derivative with respect to nodal displacements and setting it to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial \{d\}^e} = 0 \implies [K]^e \{d\}^e = \{F\}^e$$

where:

$$[K]^e = \int_{V_e} [B]^T [D] [B] \, dV \quad \text{and} \quad \{F\}^e = \int_{V_e} [N]^T \{b\} \, dV + \int_{S_e} [N]^T \{t\} \, dS$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Pearl:** The equation $[K]^e \{d\}^e = \{F\}^e$ is the **fundamental FEA equilibrium equation** at the element level. When assembled across all elements (with proper summation at shared nodes), it becomes the **Global Stiffness Equation** $[K]\{D\} = \{F\}$ for the entire ship structure.

### 2.2 The Constitutive Matrix [D] for 3D Elasticity

For a 3D isotropic linear elastic material (the standard assumption for marine steel), the constitutive matrix is:

$$[D] = \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)} \begin{bmatrix} 1-\nu & \nu & \nu & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ \nu & 1-\nu & \nu & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ \nu & \nu & 1-\nu & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \frac{1-2\nu}{2} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \frac{1-2\nu}{2} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \frac{1-2\nu}{2} \end{bmatrix}$$

For 2D plane stress problems (often used for ship plate panels), $[D]$ reduces to a 3×3 matrix involving only $E$ and $\nu$, and is much simpler to integrate.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is your **exam-day cheat sheet**. Every formula here has appeared in KTU past papers or is directly related to a frequently-asked Module 2 problem.

| **Symbol / Term** | **Formula / Definition** | **Significance** |
|---|---|---|
| Element Stiffness Matrix | $[K]^e = \int_{V_e} [B]^T [D] [B] \, dV$ | Core FEA equation; derives element behavior |
| Global Stiffness Matrix | $[K] = \sum_{e=1}^{N_e} [K]^e$ (assembled) | Represents the entire structure |
| Equilibrium Equation | $[K]\{D\} = \{F\}$ | Solved for unknown nodal displacements |
| Displacement Field | $\{u\} = [N]\{d\}^e$ | Approximation of continuous field |
| Strain Field | $\{\varepsilon\} = [B]\{d\}^e$ | Obtained via $[B]$ matrix |
| Stress Field | $\{\sigma\} = [D][B]\{d\}^e$ | Used for post-processing and design checks |
| Strain Energy | $U = \frac{1}{2}(\{d\}^e)^T [K]^e \{d\}^e$ | Symmetry of $[K]$ follows from this |
| Virtual Work (Internal) | $\delta U = (\{d\}^e)^T [K]^e \{\delta d\}^e$ | Basis of the weak form |
| Symmetry Property | $[K]^e = ([K]^e)^T$ | Follows from Maxwell-Betti reciprocity theorem |
| Positive Definiteness | $\{x\}^T [K]^e \{x\} > 0$ for all $\{x\} \neq 0$ | Guarantees unique solution exists |
| Plane Stress $[D]$ | $\frac{E}{1-\nu^2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \frac{1-\nu}{2} \end{bmatrix}$ | Used for thin ship plate elements |
| Plane Strain $[D]$ | $\frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)} \begin{bmatrix} 1-\nu & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1-\nu & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \frac{1-2\nu}{2} \end{bmatrix}$ | Used for thick hull sections |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Markdown Syntax:** The vertical pipe `|` symbol has been intentionally replaced with `\\vert` or `\\mid` in the LaTeX expressions to avoid breaking the markdown table rendering.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Naval Architecture

1. **Hull Girder Bending Analysis** — The stiffness matrix of the entire ship is assembled and solved to find hogging/sagging stresses along the hull.
2. **Plate Buckling Prediction** — Plate element stiffness matrices are used to determine the critical buckling load of ship panels under compressive in-plane loads.
3. **Stiffener-Plate Interaction** — Combined beam and plate elements (with appropriate stiffness matrices) capture the composite action of stiffened panels in double-bottom structures.
4. **Dynamic Response & Vibration** — Mass matrices combined with stiffness matrices yield natural frequencies, crucial for avoiding resonance with propeller excitations.
5. **Sloshing Analysis** — Fluid-structure interaction problems in LNG carriers use specialized stiffness formulations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Element Stiffness Matrix

This is the most critical derivation in Module 2. We derive $[K]^e$ from the very foundations of variational mechanics.

---

**DERIVATION STEP 1 — Statement of Total Potential Energy**

For a linear elastic continuum occupying volume $V_e$ with boundary surface $S_e$, the total potential energy functional is:

$$\Pi = U - W$$

The strain energy $U$ is the work done by internal stresses during deformation:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} \int_{V_e} \{\sigma\}^T \{\varepsilon\} \, dV$$

The work done by external loads (body forces $\{b\}$ over the volume and surface tractions $\{t\}$ over the boundary) is:

$$W = \int_{V_e} \{u\}^T \{b\} \, dV + \int_{S_e} \{u\}^T \{t\} \, dS$$

Therefore:

$$\Pi = \frac{1}{2} \int_{V_e} \{\sigma\}^T \{\varepsilon\} \, dV - \int_{V_e} \{u\}^T \{b\} \, dV - \int_{S_e} \{u\}^T \{t\} \, dS$$

> **Logic Note:** The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ in strain energy arises because the stress-strain curve is linear — the average force during loading from 0 to final strain is half the final force.

---

**DERIVATION STEP 2 — Discretization of the Displacement Field**

Since the element has a finite number of nodes with discrete displacements $\{d\}^e$, we approximate the continuous displacement field using shape functions:

$$\{u(x)\} = [N(x)] \{d\}^e$$

where $\{d\}^e$ is the column vector of nodal displacements at all nodes of element $e$, and $[N]$ is the matrix of shape functions that interpolate the displacement between nodes.

> **Logic Note:** The accuracy of the entire FEA solution hinges on the choice of shape functions. For 1D linear bar elements, $[N]$ contains two linear Lagrange polynomials. For 2D quad elements, $[N]$ contains four bilinear functions. The choice depends on element type.

---

**DERIVATION STEP 3 — Strain-Displacement Relation**

Strain is defined as the symmetric gradient of displacement. In matrix form:

$$\{\varepsilon\} = [L]\{u\}$$

where $[L]$ is the **differential operator matrix**. Substituting the discretized displacement:

$$\{\varepsilon\} = [L][N]\{d\}^e$$

By definition, we set:

$$[B] = [L][N]$$

Therefore:

$$\{\varepsilon\} = [B]\{d\}^e$$

> **Logic Note:** The matrix $[B]$ is the heart of FEA. It is purely geometric — it depends only on the coordinates and shape functions, not on material properties. This is why FEA cleanly separates **geometry** ($[B]$) from **material behavior** ($[D]$).

---

**DERIVATION STEP 4 — Stress-Strain Relation (Constitutive Equation)**

For linear elastic, isotropic materials:

$$\{\sigma\} = [D]\{\varepsilon\}$$

Substituting $\{\varepsilon\} = [B]\{d\}^e$:

$$\{\sigma\} = [D][B]\{d\}^e$$

> **Logic Note:** The matrix $[D]$ encodes all the material behavior. For steel it depends only on $E$ and $\nu$. For composite shipbuilding materials (e.g., FRP, sandwich panels), $[D]$ becomes more complex with orthotropic or laminated formulations.

---

**DERIVATION STEP 5 — Strain Energy in Terms of Nodal Displacements**

Substituting $\{\varepsilon\}$ into the strain energy integral:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} \int_{V_e} (\{d\}^e)^T [B]^T [D] [B] \{d\}^e \, dV$$

Since $\{d\}^e$ does not depend on the integration variable, it can be factored out:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} (\{d\}^e)^T \left( \int_{V_e} [B]^T [D] [B] \, dV \right) \{d\}^e$$

---

**DERIVATION STEP 6 — Work Done by External Loads in Discretized Form**

The external work becomes:

$$W = (\{d\}^e)^T \left( \int_{V_e} [N]^T \{b\} \, dV + \int_{S_e} [N]^T \{t\} \, dS \right)$$

Defining the **equivalent nodal force vector** $\{F\}^e$:

$$\{F\}^e = \int_{V_e} [N]^T \{b\} \, dV + \int_{S_e} [N]^T \{t\} \, dS$$

---

**DERIVATION STEP 7 — Application of the Stationarity Condition**

The Principle of Minimum Potential Energy requires $\delta \Pi = 0$ for all admissible virtual displacements $\{\delta d\}^e$:

$$\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial \{d\}^e} = \frac{\partial U}{\partial \{d\}^e} - \frac{\partial W}{\partial \{d\}^e} = 0$$

Computing each term:

$$\frac{\partial U}{\partial \{d\}^e} = \left( \int_{V_e} [B]^T [D] [B] \, dV \right) \{d\}^e$$

$$\frac{\partial W}{\partial \{d\}^e} = \{F\}^e$$

Setting them equal:

$$\left( \int_{V_e} [B]^T [D] [B] \, dV \right) \{d\}^e = \{F\}^e$$

---

**DERIVATION STEP 8 — Final Identification of the Stiffness Matrix**

By direct comparison with the equilibrium equation $[K]^e \{d\}^e = \{F\}^e$:

$$\boxed{[K]^e = \int_{V_e} [B]^T [D] [B] \, dV}$$

This is the **general equation for the calculation of the stiffness matrix**, valid for any element type (1D bar, 2D triangle/quad, 3D tetrahedron/hexahedron) in linear elastic FEA.

> **QED — Quod Erat Demonstrandum (That Which Was to Be Demonstrated).**

### 3.2 Worked Example — 1D Linear Bar Element

To make the abstract formula concrete, consider a 1D bar element of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and Young's modulus $E$. The shape functions are:

$$N_1(x) = 1 - \frac{x}{L}, \quad N_2(x) = \frac{x}{L}$$

The strain-displacement matrix is constant:

$$[B] = \frac{1}{L} \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

The constitutive scalar (1D version of $[D]$) is simply $E$. Therefore:

$$[K]^e = \int_0^L [B]^T E [B] \, A \, dx$$

$$= E A \int_0^L \frac{1}{L} \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \frac{1}{L} \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} dx$$

$$= \frac{EA}{L^2} \int_0^L \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} dx$$

$$= \frac{EA}{L^2} \begin{bmatrix} L & -L \\ -L & L \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\boxed{[K]^e = \frac{EA}{L} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}$$

> **Verification of Properties:**
> - **Symmetric:** Yes, $[K]^e = ([K]^e)^T$ ✓
> - **Positive Semi-Definite:** Sum of rows equals zero (rigid body mode) — needs boundary conditions to become positive definite.
> - **Units Check:** $E$ [N/m²] × $A$ [m²] / $L$ [m] = N/m ✓ (correct stiffness units)

This single result is the basis for **truss and frame analysis of ship stiffeners** and is the simplest illustration of the general formula.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — From Physics to Solution

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete computational pipeline from the physical ship structure to the solved displacement field, with the stiffness matrix as the central computation node.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Ship Structure Hull Plate Stiffener] --> B[Discretization into Finite Elements]
    B --> C[Element Type Selection Bar Tri Quad Hex]
    C --> D[Choice of Shape Functions N1 N2 N3 N4]
    D --> E[Compute Strain Displacement Matrix B]
    E --> F{B is Geometric Only}
    F --> G[Material Property Input E and nu]
    G --> H[Form Constitutive Matrix D]
    H --> I[Compute Element Stiffness Matrix K times Area Volume Integral]
    I --> J[Element Stiffness Matrix K superscript e]
    J --> K[Assembly into Global K Matrix]
    K --> L[Apply Boundary Conditions Fixed Hull Supports]
    L --> M[Apply Loads Hydrostatic Wave Cargo]
    M --> N[Solve K times D equals F]
    N --> O[Nodal Displacement Vector D]
    O --> P[Post Processing Stresses Strains]
    P --> Q[Design Check Against Classification Rules]
```

### 4.2 Schematic — The Stiffness Matrix in Context

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Quantities]
        A1[Geometry and Coordinates]
        A2[Shape Functions N]
        A3[Material Properties E nu]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE[Computation Block]
        B1[Differential Operator L]
        B2[B equals L times N]
        B3[D Matrix from E and nu]
    end

    subgraph CORE[Core Stiffness Computation]
        C1[Integrand B transpose times D times B]
        C2[Volume Integral over V subscript e]
        C3[Element Stiffness Matrix K superscript e]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output and Downstream Use]
        D1[Element Equilibrium K times d equals F]
        D2[Global Assembly]
        D3[Solved Displacements]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    A3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Symmetry & Definiteness Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[K Matrix Element] --> S1[Property 1 Symmetric K equals K transpose]
    M --> S2[Property 2 Positive Semi Definite]
    M --> S3[Property 3 Singular Before BC Application]
    M --> S4[Property 4 Diagonal Dominant]
    S1 --> N1[Follows from Reciprocity Theorem of Maxwell Betti]
    S2 --> N2[Strain Energy Always Non Negative]
    S3 --> N3[Due to Rigid Body Modes]
    S4 --> N4[Diagonal Entry Greater than Sum of Off Diagonals in Same Row]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The flowcharts above are designed for KTU exam revision. The student can reproduce the key blocks (Input → Computation → Core → Output) in a 2-mark conceptual answer.
