<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*State and explain the general equation for the calculation of the element stiffness matrix in finite element analysis. Define each term in the equation.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The general equation for the element stiffness matrix is:

$$[K]^e = \int_{V_e} [B]^T [D] [B] \, dV$$

**Term Definitions:**

- **$[B]$ — Strain-Displacement Matrix:** It is the product of the differential operator $[L]$ and the shape function matrix $[N]$, i.e., $[B] = [L][N]$. It geometrically relates the strains inside the element to the nodal displacements $\{d\}^e$.
- **$[D]$ — Material Constitutive (Elasticity) Matrix:** It contains the material properties of the element. For isotropic linear elastic marine steel, it depends on Young's Modulus $E$ and Poisson's Ratio $\nu$.
- **$V_e$ — Volume of the Element:** The integration is performed over the entire volume of the element to account for the contribution of every infinitesimal point within it.
- **$[K]^e$ — Element Stiffness Matrix:** The resulting symmetric, positive semi-definite matrix that maps nodal displacements $\{d\}^e$ to nodal forces $\{F\}^e$ through the relation $[K]^e \{d\}^e = \{F\}^e$.

The matrix is **symmetric** due to Maxwell-Betti's reciprocity theorem and **banded** when properly assembled, which is exploited for efficient numerical solution.

*(Word count and depth calibrated for 3-mark valuation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any four properties of the element stiffness matrix $[K]^e$ and explain the physical significance of the diagonal and off-diagonal terms.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The element stiffness matrix possesses the following key properties:

1. **Symmetry:** $[K]^e = ([K]^e)^T$, which follows from the Maxwell-Betti reciprocal work theorem. If a unit force at node $j$ causes displacement $\delta$ at node $i$, then a unit force at node $i$ causes the same displacement $\delta$ at node $j$.

2. **Positive Semi-Definiteness:** The strain energy $U = \frac{1}{2}\{d\}^T [K]^e \{d\}$ is always non-negative, which forces $[K]^e$ to be positive semi-definite. After applying displacement boundary conditions, it becomes positive definite.

3. **Singularity (Before BCs):** The element stiffness matrix is singular because rigid body motions cause zero strain energy. The sum of all rows (and columns) is zero.

4. **Diagonal Dominance:** The diagonal term $K_{ii}$ is always greater than or equal to the sum of absolute values of off-diagonal terms in the same row, ensuring numerical stability.

**Physical Significance of Terms:**

- **Diagonal term $K_{ii}$:** Represents the **direct stiffness** — the force required at node $i$ to produce a unit displacement at node $i$ while all other nodes are held fixed.
- **Off-diagonal term $K_{ij}$ ($i \neq j$):** Represents the **coupling stiffness** — the force induced at node $i$ due to a unit displacement at node $j$ while all other nodes are fixed. It quantifies load transfer between connected nodes.

---

### 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Module 2 Choice]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** *Derive the general expression for the element stiffness matrix $[K]^e$ starting from the Principle of Minimum Potential Energy. Clearly state the assumptions made. (7 Marks)*

**(b)** *For a 1D linear bar element of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and Young's modulus $E$, derive the element stiffness matrix. State and verify the symmetry property. (7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q3(a) — 7 Marks:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Material is **linear elastic** and **isotropic**.
2. Displacements are **small** (infinitesimal strain theory).
3. Static loading (no inertia or damping effects).
4. Body forces and surface tractions are conservative.

**Derivation:**

The total potential energy of an elastic continuum is:

$$\Pi = \frac{1}{2} \int_{V_e} \{\sigma\}^T \{\varepsilon\} \, dV - \int_{V_e} \{u\}^T \{b\} \, dV - \int_{S_e} \{u\}^T \{t\} \, dS$$

Approximating displacements using shape functions:

$$\{u\} = [N]\{d\}^e$$

The strain field becomes:

$$\{\varepsilon\} = [L]\{u\} = [L][N]\{d\}^e = [B]\{d\}^e$$

The stress field (using Hooke's law) becomes:

$$\{\sigma\} = [D]\{\varepsilon\} = [D][B]\{d\}^e$$

Substituting into the strain energy:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} \int_{V_e} (\{d\}^e)^T [B]^T [D] [B] \{d\}^e \, dV$$

The work done by external loads is:

$$W = (\{d\}^e)^T \left[ \int_{V_e} [N]^T \{b\} \, dV + \int_{S_e} [N]^T \{t\} \, dS \right] = (\{d\}^e)^T \{F\}^e$$

Total potential energy:

$$\Pi = \frac{1}{2} (\{d\}^e)^T \left( \int_{V_e} [B]^T [D] [B] \, dV \right) \{d\}^e - (\{d\}^e)^T \{F\}^e$$

Applying the stationarity condition $\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial \{d\}^e} = 0$:

$$\left( \int_{V_e} [B]^T [D] \, dV \right) \{d\}^e = \{F\}^e \cdot \text{(residual terms)}$$

Leading to the stiffness matrix:

$$\boxed{[K]^e = \int_{V_e} [B]^T [D] [B] \, dV}$$

---

**Model Solution for Q3(b) — 7 Marks:**

For a 1D bar element, the shape functions are:

$$N_1(x) = 1 - \frac{x}{L}, \quad N_2(x) = \frac{x}{L}$$

The strain is the axial strain $\varepsilon_x = \frac{du}{dx}$:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{dN_1}{dx} d_1 + \frac{dN_2}{dx} d_2 = -\frac{1}{L} d_1 + \frac{1}{L} d_2$$

So:

$$[B] = \frac{1}{L} \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

The constitutive scalar is simply $E$ (1D Hooke's law: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$), and the differential element volume is $dV = A \, dx$.

Therefore:

$$[K]^e = \int_0^L \frac{1}{L} \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} E \frac{1}{L} \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} A \, dx$$

$$= \frac{EA}{L^2} \int_0^L \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} dx = \frac{EA}{L^2} \begin{bmatrix} L & -L \\ -L & L \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\boxed{[K]^e = \frac{EA}{L} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Symmetry Verification:**

$$([K]^e)^T = \frac{EA}{L} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}^T = \frac{EA}{L} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = [K]^e$$

Hence, $[K]^e$ is **symmetric**, confirming Maxwell-Betti's reciprocity theorem. ∎

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** *Explain the significance of the strain-displacement matrix $[B]$ and the constitutive matrix $[D]$ in the stiffness matrix formulation. Derive the $[D]$ matrix for a 2D plane stress problem. (7 Marks)*

**(b)** *A steel plate of dimensions 1 m × 0.5 m × 0.01 m is subjected to uniform in-plane loading. For an idealized 4-noded quad element, outline the steps to compute the element stiffness matrix given $E = 200$ GPa and $\nu = 0.3$. (7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution Outline for Q3B(a) — 7 Marks:**

**Significance of $[B]$:**
The strain-displacement matrix $[B]$ is purely geometric. It contains the derivatives of shape functions and transforms the discrete nodal displacement vector $\{d\}^e$ into the continuous strain field $\{\varepsilon\}$ inside the element. Without $[B]$, we cannot connect discrete nodal variables to internal field quantities.

**Significance of $[D]$:**
The constitutive matrix $[D]$ encodes the material law. It converts strains into stresses via $\{\sigma\} = [D]\{\varepsilon\}$. The form of $[D]$ depends on:
- Material type (isotropic, orthotropic, anisotropic)
- Problem dimensionality (2D plane stress, 2D plane strain, 3D)
- Assumed behavior (linear elastic, elasto-plastic, visco-elastic)

**Derivation of $[D]$ for 2D Plane Stress:**

Plane stress assumes $\sigma_z = \tau_{xz} = \tau_{yz} = 0$. Hooke's law in 2D gives:

$$\begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_x \\ \sigma_y \\ \tau_{xy} \end{Bmatrix} = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \frac{1-\nu}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_x \\ \varepsilon_y \\ \gamma_{xy} \end{Bmatrix}$$

Therefore:

$$[D]_{plane\ stress} = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \frac{1-\nu}{2} \end{bmatrix}$$

---

**Model Solution Outline for Q3B(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Steps to compute $[K]^e$ for the 4-noded quad element:**

**Step 1:** Identify the four nodes and their coordinates. Use bilinear shape functions:

$$N_i(\xi, \eta) = \frac{1}{4}(1 + \xi\xi_i)(1 + \eta\eta_i), \quad i = 1,2,3,4$$

**Step 2:** Compute the Jacobian matrix $[J]$ relating natural coordinates $(\xi, \eta)$ to physical coordinates $(x, y)$:

$$[J] = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial x}{\partial \xi} & \frac{\partial y}{\partial \xi} \\ \frac{\partial x}{\partial \eta} & \frac{\partial y}{\partial \eta} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the $[B]$ matrix in natural coordinates:

$$[B] = [L][N] = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial x} & 0 \\ 0 & \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial y} \\ \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial y} & \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial x} \end{bmatrix}$$

where $\frac{\partial N_i}{\partial x}$ and $\frac{\partial N_i}{\partial y}$ are obtained via the Jacobian inverse.

**Step 4:** Form the $[D]$ matrix using the given $E$ and $\nu$:

$$[D] = \frac{200{,}000}{1 - 0.09} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.3 & 0 \\ 0.3 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.35 \end{bmatrix} \text{ MPa} = \begin{bmatrix} 219{,}780 & 65{,}934 & 0 \\ 65{,}934 & 219{,}780 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 76{,}923 \end{bmatrix} \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5:** Perform Gauss quadrature integration (typically 2×2) to evaluate:

$$[K]^e = \int_{-1}^{1} \int_{-1}^{1} [B]^T [D] [B] \, t \, \det[J] \, d\xi \, d\eta$$

where $t = 0.01$ m is the plate thickness.

**Step 6:** Assemble into the global stiffness matrix using the connectivity (incidence) table of the element.

> The explicit numerical evaluation requires careful tracking of shape function derivatives, and is best done with the help of a computer algebra system — but the **methodology and the integrand** form the core of the answer.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the transpose on $[B]$:** A shocking number of students write $[K]^e = \int [B][D][B]^T dV$ or even $\int [B][B]^T dV$. **The correct order is $[B]^T [D] [B]$** — this is the most heavily penalized error in FEA exam scripts.
> 2. **Mixing up $[B]$ and $[D]$:** Remember — $[B]$ is geometry, $[D]$ is material. They cannot be interchanged.
> 3. **Not stating the integration domain:** The integral is over the **volume** $V_e$ of the element, not its surface or boundary. Markers look for this explicitly.
> 4. **Omitting the assumptions:** Most derivations require the linear elasticity assumption. Failing to state it loses 1-2 marks.
> 5. **Units mismatch:** In the bar element derivation, $\frac{EA}{L}$ must yield units of N/m. Many students forget to convert mm to m.
> 6. **Confusing the global and element stiffness matrices:** $[K]^e$ is for one element; $[K]$ is the assembled global matrix. Examiners expect students to distinguish these clearly.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2: General Equations for the Stiffness Matrix**

- **Master Formula:** $[K]^e = \int_{V_e} [B]^T [D] [B] \, dV$ — this is the single most important equation in Module 2.
- **Three Pillars of FEA:** Shape functions $[N]$, Strain-displacement matrix $[B] = [L][N]$, Constitutive matrix $[D]$.
- **Variational Root:** The equation is derived from the Principle of Minimum Potential Energy ($\delta \Pi = 0$).
- **Equilibrium Equation:** $[K]^e \{d\}^e = \{F\}^e$ at the element level; $[K]\{D\} = \{F\}$ at the global level.
- **Properties of $[K]^e$:** Symmetric, positive semi-definite, singular before BCs, diagonally dominant.
- **Diagonal terms = Direct stiffness**; **Off-diagonal terms = Coupling stiffness**.
- **Assumptions:** Linear elastic, isotropic, small displacements, static loading.
- **2D Plane Stress $[D]$:** $\frac{E}{1-\nu^2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \frac{1-\nu}{2} \end{bmatrix}$
- **2D Plane Strain $[D]$:** $\frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)} \begin{bmatrix} 1-\nu & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1-\nu & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \frac{1-2\nu}{2} \end{bmatrix}$
- **1D Bar Element Result:** $[K]^e = \frac{EA}{L} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ — the simplest illustration of the general formula.
- **Naval Application Context:** Hull plate analysis, stiffener modeling, double-bottom buckling, propeller-shaft vibration.
- **Numerical Tip:** For 2D/3D problems, $[K]^e$ is evaluated using **Gauss quadrature** (2×2 for quad elements, 3×3 for higher accuracy).
- **Symmetry Verification:** Always check $[K]^e = ([K]^e)^T$ after derivation.
- **Symmetry Origin:** Maxwell-Betti reciprocal work theorem.
- **Singularity:** The element stiffness matrix is singular because of rigid body modes; boundary conditions resolve this.
- **Global Assembly:** $[K] = \sum_e [K]^e$ after appropriate mapping via the element connectivity (incidence) table.
- **Common Exam Format:** Derivation (7 marks) + Numerical/Specialized Element (7 marks) = 14 marks total.

> **Final Mantra for the KTU Board Exam:** *"[B] transpose times [D] times [B] integrated over the volume — that is the stiffness matrix; the rest is assembly and boundary conditions."* Memorize this, and Module 2 is conquered. ⚓

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction: Why These Three Specializations Matter in Naval Architecture

In Finite Element Analysis (FEA) of marine structures, the analyst rarely solves a fully general **3D** problem end-to-end. This is because most ship and offshore components fall into one of three idealized stress states, which allow a **drastic reduction** in problem size (and hence CPU time) without loss of engineering accuracy.

The three principal simplified states studied in this module are:

| State | Dominant Geometry | Naval / Marine Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Plane Stress** | Thin plates, shells loaded in their mid-plane | Ship hull plating, bulkhead stiffener panels, deck plates |
| **Plane Strain** | Long prismatic bodies with constant cross-section | Longitudinally stiffened girders, long propeller shaft sections, slipway foundations |
| **Axisymmetric** | Bodies of revolution under symmetric loading | Pressure hulls of submarines, torpedo casings, rudder stocks, propeller hubs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus:** The student must learn to identify which idealization applies, write the correct **strain–displacement (B) matrix**, use the correct **constitutive (D) matrix**, and assemble the correct **element stiffness (k) matrix** for the chosen idealization. Marks are routinely awarded for correctly identifying which stress components vanish.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definition — Plane Stress

A state of **Plane Stress** exists at a point in a body when the stress components acting on the planes perpendicular to one coordinate axis (conventionally the *z*-axis) are zero.

Mathematically, the stress tensor reduces to:

$$
\sigma = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x & \tau_{xy} & 0 \\ \tau_{xy} & \sigma_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Hence **σz = 0, τxz = 0, τyz = 0**, while strains εz, γxz, γyz are generally **non-zero** and must be recovered post-solution from the in-plane strains via the generalized Hooke's Law.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Picture:** Imagine a postcard lying flat on a table. Push it sideways — only the in-plane fibres (σx, σy, τxy) carry load. The fibres pointing out of the page carry nothing because the postcard is too thin to develop through-thickness stress. This is *Plane Stress*.

**KTU Condition Checklist:**
- Geometry: One dimension (thickness *t*) is **very small** compared to the in-plane dimensions.
- Loading: All loads act **in the mid-plane**.
- Thickness *t* is uniform and remains so (no bending of the thickness).

---

## 1.3 Formal Definition — Plane Strain

A state of **Plane Strain** exists at a point when the strain components associated with one coordinate axis vanish, leaving only in-plane strain components active.

Mathematically:

$$
\epsilon = \begin{bmatrix} \epsilon_x & \gamma_{xy} & 0 \\ \gamma_{xy} & \epsilon_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Hence **εz = 0, γxz = 0, γyz = 0**, while σz is generally **non-zero** and is treated as a **reaction stress** computed after the in-plane analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Picture:** Imagine a long retaining wall of a dry dock. A cross-section taken far from the ends cannot shorten in the longitudinal (*z*) direction because the wall behind it prevents it. So εz = 0, but the wall still squeezes back with σz. This is *Plane Strain*.

**KTU Condition Checklist:**
- Geometry: One dimension (length *L*) is **very large** compared to cross-section.
- Loading: Loads are uniform along the long axis and act **perpendicular** to it.
- End effects are neglected (infinite-length assumption).

---

## 1.4 Formal Definition — Axisymmetric Solid

An **Axisymmetric** problem is one in which the geometry, material properties, loading, and boundary conditions are all **symmetric about a central axis** (conventionally the *z*-axis).

The 3D body is generated by revolving a 2D meridian (cross-section in the *r–z* plane) through **360°** about the *z*-axis. Because of symmetry:
- No displacement or strain varies with the circumferential angle **θ**.
- The 3D problem **collapses to a 2D problem** in the *r–z* plane.
- A new strain component, **circumferential (hoop) strain εθ = u/r**, appears — where *u* is the radial displacement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Picture:** Picture a submarine pressure hull. Slice it vertically and you see a 2D cross-section (meridian). Spin that 2D cross-section around the central axis of the hull and you regenerate the full 3D hull. A uniform external pressure creates the same hoop stress σθ at every θ-angle, so we only analyse one 2D slice.

**KTU Condition Checklist:**
- Geometry: Body of revolution.
- Loading: Distributed symmetrically about the axis (e.g., hydrostatic pressure, self-weight).
- Boundary conditions: Symmetric — no rotation about the axis.

---

## 1.5 Comparative Summary Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize This Comparison — It Appears In Nearly Every KTU Module 2 Question.**

| Feature | Plane Stress | Plane Strain | Axisymmetric |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Vanishing stresses | σz, τxz, τyz | None (σz is reaction) | τrθ, τzθ |
| Vanishing strains | None (εz, γ are derived) | εz, γxz, γyz | γrθ, γzθ |
| Independent strains | εx, εy, γxy (3) | εx, εy, γxy (3) | εr, εz, εθ, γrz (4) |
| DOF per node | 2 (*u, v*) | 2 (*u, v*) | 2 (*u, w*) |
| Naval example | Hull plate panel | Long girder | Submarine hull |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Strain–Displacement Relations: The Three Idealizations

The three idealizations differ **only** in their strain–displacement (kinematic) relations. Master these three equation sets — they are the heart of Module 2.

### (a) Plane Stress / Plane Strain (Cartesian)

$$
\epsilon = \begin{Bmatrix} \epsilon_x \\ \epsilon_y \\ \gamma_{xy} \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \partial/\partial x & 0 \\ 0 & \partial/\partial y \\ \partial/\partial y & \partial/\partial x \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u \\ v \end{Bmatrix}
$$

### (b) Axisymmetric (Cylindrical *r–z*)

$$
\epsilon = \begin{Bmatrix} \epsilon_r \\ \epsilon_z \\ \epsilon_\theta \\ \gamma_{rz} \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \partial/\partial r & 0 \\ 0 & \partial/\partial z \\ 1/r & 0 \\ \partial/\partial z & \partial/\partial r \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u \\ w \end{Bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The `1/r` factor in the third row is the **single most important feature** distinguishing axisymmetric strain from plane strain. It produces **non-constant B-matrix entries** even when shape functions are linear — making the stiffness integral a non-trivial numerical task.

---

## 2.2 Constitutive (D) Matrix for Each Idealization

The constitutive (material) matrix **D** is what enforces the difference between plane stress and plane strain when the geometry itself might be similar.

### Plane Stress D-Matrix

$$
D_{PS} = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \dfrac{1-\nu}{2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Plane Strain D-Matrix

$$
D_{PE} = \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}\begin{bmatrix} 1-\nu & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1-\nu & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \dfrac{1-2\nu}{2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Axisymmetric D-Matrix

$$
D_{AX} = \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}\begin{bmatrix} 1-\nu & \nu & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1-\nu & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & \nu & 1-\nu & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \dfrac{1-2\nu}{2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Verification Trick:** Notice that $D_{PE}$ has the same denominator as $D_{AX}$ but one less row/column. The numerator $(1-\nu)$ in the diagonal of $D_{PE}$ becomes $(1-2\nu)$ off-diagonal in $D_{PE}$ — this is the signature you should hunt for in MCQs.

---

## 2.3 Element Library Used for These Problems

| Element | Nodes | DOF | Typical Use in Naval Architecture |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| CST (Constant Strain Triangle) | 3 | 2 per node | Quick hull-plate stress maps |
| LST (Linear Strain Triangle) | 6 | 2 per node | Better accuracy for the same mesh |
| 4-Node Quad (Q4) | 4 | 2 per node | General 2D meshing |
| 8-Node Quad (Q8) | 8 | 2 per node | Curved boundaries (rudder profiles) |
| 4-Node Axisymmetric Ring | 4 | 2 per node | Pressure hulls, propeller hubs |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Every formula here is fair game in ESE.**

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Element stiffness matrix | $k = \int_{V_e} B^T D B \, dV$ | $dV = t \, dA$ for plane problems |
| Plane stress reduced modulus | $E \big/ (1-\nu^2)$ | Pa |
| Plane strain reduced modulus | $E \big/ \left[(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)\right]$ | Pa |
| Hoop (circumferential) strain | $\epsilon_\theta = u / r$ | Dimensionless |
| Relation between plane-stress & plane-strain E | $E_{PS}^* = E_{PE}^* \big/ (1-\nu^2)$ | Effective modulus link |
| Poisson coupling term in D_PS | $\nu$ | Multiplies off-diagonal entries |
| CST element area | $A = \tfrac{1}{2} \vert (x_2-x_1)(y_3-y_1) - (x_3-x_1)(y_2-y_1) \vert$ | m² |
| CST shape functions | $N_i = (a_i + b_i x + c_i y) / (2A)$ | Linear in x, y |
| CST β and γ coefficients | $\beta_i = y_j - y_k$, $\gamma_i = x_k - x_j$ | Cyclic permutation |
| Axisymmetric volume element | $dV = 2\pi r \, dA$ | *r* is the radial coordinate of integration point |
| Stress recovery from strains | $\sigma = D \epsilon$ | Direct matrix product |

---

## 2.5 Why These Idealizations Matter — Real Engineering Utility

- **Computational Savings:** A 3D mesh of a 100 m ship hull plate might have 10⁶ DOF; the equivalent plane-stress mesh has ~10⁴ DOF. Same accuracy for the in-plane response.
- **Mesh Quality:** Plane models are far easier to grade-check (aspect ratio, Jacobian, warpage) than 3D shells or solids.
- **Sanity Check:** Most classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) accept plate analysis done under plane-stress assumptions for local scantling determination.
- **Axisymmetric is the "sweet spot":** Many marine components are bodies of revolution (submarines, propellers, shafts, nozzles). Axisymmetric analysis gives 3D fidelity at 2D cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When a question says "long structure with uniform cross-section" → Plane Strain. When it says "thin plate loaded in its own plane" → Plane Stress. When it says "revolve this 2D sketch" or "body of revolution under symmetric load" → Axisymmetric.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the CST (Constant Strain Triangle) Stiffness Matrix

The CST is the simplest 2D element used in both plane stress and plane strain analysis and is the canonical KTU derivation question. We derive it here exhaustively.

### Step 1 — Geometry and Shape Functions

Consider a triangular element with nodes *i, j, k* numbered counter-clockwise, having coordinates (xᵢ, yᵢ), (x_j, y_j), (xₖ, yₖ).

The linear shape functions are:

$$
N_i(x,y) = \frac{1}{2A}\,(a_i + b_i x + c_i y)
$$

where

$$
a_i = x_j y_k - x_k y_j, \quad b_i = y_j - y_k, \quad c_i = x_k - x_j
$$

and the other shape functions follow by cyclic permutation *i → j → k → i*.

The element area is:

$$
A = \frac{1}{2}\big[(x_j-x_i)(y_k-y_i) - (x_k-x_i)(y_j-y_i)\big]
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The shape functions are deliberately written so that $N_i = 1$ at node *i* and $N_i = 0$ at the other two nodes. This is the Kronecker-delta property required for C⁰ continuity.

### Step 2 — Displacement Field

$$
\begin{Bmatrix} u(x,y) \\ v(x,y) \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} N_i & 0 & N_j & 0 & N_k & 0 \\ 0 & N_i & 0 & N_j & 0 & N_k \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_i \\ v_i \\ u_j \\ v_j \\ u_k \\ v_k \end{Bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Strain–Displacement (B) Matrix

Differentiating and noting that *bᵢ, cᵢ* are **constants**:

$$
B = \frac{1}{2A}\begin{bmatrix} b_i & 0 & b_j & 0 & b_k & 0 \\ 0 & c_i & 0 & c_j & 0 & c_k \\ c_i & b_i & c_j & b_j & c_k & b_k \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Because the shape functions are linear in *x, y*, their partial derivatives are constants. **Therefore B is constant** over the element — hence the name "Constant Strain Triangle."

### Step 4 — Constitutive (D) Matrix

Substitute $D_{PS}$ (plane stress) or $D_{PE}$ (plane strain) from Section 2.2.

### Step 5 — Element Stiffness Matrix

The general formula is:

$$
k = \int_{V_e} B^T D B \, dV = t \int_{A_e} B^T D B \, dA
$$

Since **B is constant** for a CST, this integral collapses trivially:

$$
\boxed{\, k = t \cdot A \cdot B^T D B \,}
$$

where *t* is thickness and *A* is the element area.

### Step 6 — Numerical Demonstration (Plane Stress)

Let *E* = 200 GPa, *ν* = 0.3, *t* = 0.01 m, *A* = 0.001 m², and assume an equilateral triangle with side 0.05 m. For an equilateral triangle:

$$
b_i = b_j = b_k = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot s, \quad c_i = c_j = c_k = \frac{s}{2}, \quad \text{with cyclic signs}
$$

The resulting k-matrix has units of **N/m** (consistent with 2D analysis), and the diagonal entry for in-plane translation evaluates to roughly:

$$
k_{2D} \approx \frac{E \cdot t}{2(1-\nu)} \approx \frac{200\times10^9 \times 0.01}{2 \times 0.7} \approx 1.43 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the stiffness per unit in-plane translation for a 1 cm thick steel plate under plane stress — the correct order of magnitude for ship plate work.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the 4-Node Axisymmetric Quadrilateral Stiffness Matrix

This is the **most marks-heavy derivation** in KTU Module 2 for the axisymmetric portion. We derive it in full.

### Step 1 — Geometry in the *r–z* Plane

A 4-node quad in the *r–z* plane has nodes 1, 2, 3, 4 at positions $(r_i, z_i)$ for *i = 1..4*. Bilinear shape functions are:

$$
N_i(r,z) = \tfrac{1}{4}(1+\xi\xi_i)(1+\eta\eta_i), \quad i=1,2,3,4
$$

where $(\xi, \eta) \in [-1, 1]^2$ are the natural (parent) coordinates and $(\xi_i, \eta_i)$ are the corner coordinates of a reference square.

### Step 2 — Displacement Field

$$
u(r,z) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} N_i u_i, \qquad w(r,z) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} N_i w_i
$$

### Step 3 — The Critical B-Matrix for Axisymmetric Problems

$$
B = \begin{bmatrix} \partial N_1/\partial r & 0 & \partial N_2/\partial r & 0 & \partial N_3/\partial r & 0 & \partial N_4/\partial r & 0 \\ 0 & \partial N_1/\partial z & 0 & \partial N_2/\partial z & 0 & \partial N_3/\partial z & 0 & \partial N_4/\partial z \\ N_1/r & 0 & N_2/r & 0 & N_3/r & 0 & N_4/r & 0 \\ \partial N_1/\partial z & \partial N_1/\partial r & \partial N_2/\partial z & \partial N_2/\partial r & \partial N_3/\partial z & \partial N_3/\partial r & \partial N_4/\partial z & \partial N_4/\partial r \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Observation:** The third row contains the **non-derivative, non-constant** term $N_i / r$. This row produces the hoop strain εθ = u/r. Because *r* is the integration variable, this row makes the B-matrix position-dependent **even when the shape functions are linear**.

### Step 4 — The Volume Element for Axisymmetric Solids

A ring of thickness $dr \, dz$ swept about the *z*-axis has volume:

$$
dV = (2\pi r) \cdot dr \cdot dz
$$

The factor $2\pi$ factors out of every element's stiffness matrix; we absorb it into the global stiffness at the end:

$$
k_e = 2\pi \int_{A_e} B^T D B \, r \, dr \, dz
$$

### Step 5 — Numerical (Gauss) Integration

Because *r* varies inside the element, we **cannot** pull it out of the integral. The element stiffness is evaluated using $2 \times 2$ (or $3 \times 3$) Gauss quadrature:

$$
k_e = 2\pi \sum_{g=1}^{n_g} B_g^T D B_g \cdot r_g \cdot \vert J_g \vert \cdot W_g
$$

where $W_g$ is the Gauss weight, $J_g$ is the Jacobian at Gauss point *g*, and $r_g$ is the radial coordinate at that Gauss point.

> **Conversion Logic:** The factor $r_g \cdot \vert J_g \vert$ is the **detour** that makes axisymmetric integration fundamentally different from plane-stress integration. Skipping it gives a wrong answer — a common KTU answer-script error.

### Step 6 — Stress Recovery (Post-Processing)

After solving $K U = F$, strains and stresses are recovered at each Gauss point:

$$
\epsilon_g = B_g \, u_e, \qquad \sigma_g = D \, \epsilon_g
$$

The radial stress $\sigma_r$, axial stress $\sigma_z$, hoop stress $\sigma_\theta$, and shear stress $\tau_{rz}$ are obtained directly.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — CST Under Plane Stress

**Problem:** An equilateral triangular CST element has vertices at (0, 0), (0.05, 0), and (0.025, 0.0433). The material is steel: *E* = 200 GPa, *ν* = 0.3, *t* = 0.005 m. Compute the **2×2 sub-block** $k_{ii}$ of the stiffness matrix corresponding to node 1.

**Step 1 — Element area:**

$$
A = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[(0.05-0)(0.0433-0) - (0.025-0)(0-0)\big] = \tfrac{1}{2}(0.002165) = 0.001083 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Coefficients for node 1 (using the cyclic convention with (j,k) = (2,3)):**

$$
b_1 = y_2 - y_3 = 0 - 0.0433 = -0.0433
$$

$$
c_1 = x_3 - x_2 = 0.025 - 0.05 = -0.025
$$

**Step 3 — Sub-block of B for node 1:**

$$
B_1 = \frac{1}{2A}\begin{bmatrix} b_1 & 0 \\ 0 & c_1 \\ c_1 & b_1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{0.002166}\begin{bmatrix} -0.0433 & 0 \\ 0 & -0.025 \\ -0.025 & -0.0433 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
B_1 = \begin{bmatrix} -19.99 & 0 \\ 0 & -11.54 \\ -11.54 & -19.99 \end{bmatrix} \text{ m}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — D matrix (plane stress):**

$$
D_{PS} = \frac{200\times10^9}{1-0.09}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.3 & 0 \\ 0.3 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.35 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 219.78 & 65.93 & 0 \\ 65.93 & 219.78 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 76.92 \end{bmatrix} \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Sub-block $k_{11}$:**

$$
k_{11} = t \cdot A \cdot B_1^T D_{PS} B_1
$$

Computing $D_{PS} B_1$ first:

$$
D_{PS} B_1 = \begin{bmatrix} -4393.4 & -761.5 \\ -1318.4 & -2538.3 \\ -887.7 & -1537.9 \end{bmatrix} \times 10^9
$$

Now $B_1^T D_{PS} B_1$ is a 2×2 matrix:

$$
B_1^T D_{PS} B_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 19.99 \cdot 4393.4 + 11.54 \cdot 887.7 & 19.99 \cdot 761.5 + 11.54 \cdot 1537.9 \\ 11.54 \cdot 1318.4 + 19.99 \cdot 887.7 & 11.54 \cdot 2538.3 + 19.99 \cdot 1537.9 \end{bmatrix} \times 10^9
$$

$$
B_1^T D_{PS} B_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 98.05 & 32.96 \\ 32.96 & 60.04 \end{bmatrix} \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^3
$$

Multiplying by $t \cdot A = 0.005 \times 0.001083 = 5.413 \times 10^{-6}$:

$$
\boxed{\, k_{11} = \begin{bmatrix} 530.9 & 178.5 \\ 178.5 & 325.1 \end{bmatrix} \text{ MN/m} \,}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This 2×2 block tells us that pulling node 1 in the *x*-direction with unit displacement (with all other nodes fixed) requires ~531 MN/m force. Pulling it in *y* requires ~325 MN/m. The off-diagonal 178.5 MN/m is the coupling — pulling in *x* also drags *y*.

---

## 3.4 Consistency Check — Plane Strain ↔ Plane Stress Conversion

For the **same** 2D geometry, the relationship between plane strain and plane stress D matrices is:

$$
D_{PE} = D_{PS} - \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} \nu^2 & \nu^2 & 0 \\ \nu^2 & \nu^2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The extra terms come from suppressing εz = 0 via Poisson coupling. This is why plane strain always produces **stiffer** response than plane stress for the same load — the out-of-plane constraint stiffens the in-plane response.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

### **Q1. Distinguish between Plane Stress and Plane Strain conditions. Give one marine structural example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Plane Stress:** A state of stress where the stress components normal and parallel to the planes perpendicular to one axis are zero. Mathematically, $\sigma_z = \tau_{xz} = \tau_{yz} = 0$. The out-of-plane strain $\epsilon_z$ is **non-zero** and is computed from $\epsilon_z = -\nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)/E$. **Marine Example:** A ship hull plate loaded in its own plane by in-plane hull-girder shear.

**Plane Strain:** A state where the strain components in one direction vanish. Mathematically, $\epsilon_z = \gamma_{xz} = \gamma_{yz} = 0$. The out-of-plane stress $\sigma_z = \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)$ is a **reaction stress** computed post-solution. **Marine Example:** A long-section of a propeller shaft sleeve far from the free ends.

The **key distinction**: in plane stress, stresses are set to zero and strains are derived; in plane strain, strains are set to zero and stresses are derived. *(3 marks: 1 mark for each pair of conditions + 1 mark for examples.)*

---

> **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

### **Q2. What is an axisymmetric element? Why is the row $\epsilon_\theta = u/r$ important in its B-matrix?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **axisymmetric element** is a 2D finite element used to model a 3D body of revolution (geometry, material, loading, and boundary conditions all symmetric about an axis). The 2D meridian in the *r–z* plane is meshed and analysed; results are revolved 360° to reconstruct the 3D stress field. *(1 mark)*

The **hoop strain** $\epsilon_\theta = u/r$ is essential because:
1. It couples radial displacement *u* to a strain in the circumferential direction, which is the dominant failure mode in pressure vessels and submarine hulls. *(1 mark)*
2. Because *r* is the integration variable inside the element, the term $N_i/r$ makes the B-matrix **position-dependent**, requiring numerical (Gauss) integration with the volume element $dV = 2\pi r \, dr \, dz$. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INSTRUCTIONS (KTU ESE Standard):** *Answer ONE full question. Each carries 14 marks split as 7 + 7. Show all derivations and intermediate steps.*

---

### **Question A — Plane Stress & CST Element**

> **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]`**

**(a)** Derive the element stiffness matrix for a **Constant Strain Triangle (CST)** under plane stress, starting from the displacement field and clearly stating the B-matrix and D-matrix used. Show that the **B-matrix is constant** over the element. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the CST element with vertices at **(0, 0)**, **(0.04, 0)**, and **(0.02, 0.0346)** m, with *E* = 70 GPa, *ν* = 0.33, and thickness *t* = 0.002 m, compute the **complete 6×6 element stiffness matrix** under plane stress. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks)**

The displacement field inside the CST is approximated as:

$$
\begin{Bmatrix} u \\ v \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} N_i & 0 & N_j & 0 & N_k & 0 \\ 0 & N_i & 0 & N_j & 0 & N_k \end{bmatrix} \{d\}
$$

with linear shape functions $N_i = (a_i + b_i x + c_i y)/(2A)$. The strain vector is:

$$
\{\epsilon\} = \begin{Bmatrix} \epsilon_x \\ \epsilon_y \\ \gamma_{xy} \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \partial/\partial x & 0 \\ 0 & \partial/\partial y \\ \partial/\partial y & \partial/\partial x \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u \\ v \end{Bmatrix}
$$

Since $b_i, c_i$ are constants (they depend only on nodal coordinates), the partial derivatives are constants, and we obtain the **constant B-matrix**:

$$
B = \frac{1}{2A}\begin{bmatrix} b_i & 0 & b_j & 0 & b_k & 0 \\ 0 & c_i & 0 & c_j & 0 & c_k \\ c_i & b_i & c_j & b_j & c_k & b_k \end{bmatrix}
$$

The plane-stress D-matrix is:

$$
D = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & (1-\nu)/2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Since B is constant, the stiffness integral collapses:

$$
\boxed{\, k_e = t \cdot A \cdot B^T D B \,} \quad \text{(7 marks: derivation complete)}
$$

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Element area:**

$$
A = \tfrac{1}{2}[(0.04-0)(0.0346-0) - (0.02-0)(0-0)] = \tfrac{1}{2}(0.001384) = 6.92 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Coefficients:**

For node 1 (j = 2, k = 3):
- $b_1 = y_2 - y_3 = 0 - 0.0346 = -0.0346$
- $c_1 = x_3 - x_2 = 0.02 - 0.04 = -0.02$

For node 2 (j = 3, k = 1):
- $b_2 = y_3 - y_1 = 0.0346 - 0 = 0.0346$
- $c_2 = x_1 - x_3 = 0 - 0.02 = -0.02$

For node 3 (j = 1, k = 2):
- $b_3 = y_1 - y_2 = 0 - 0 = 0$
- $c_3 = x_2 - x_1 = 0.04 - 0 = 0.04$

**Step 3 — B-matrix** (factor $1/2A = 1/0.001384 = 722.5 \text{ m}^{-1}$):

$$
B = 722.5\begin{bmatrix} -0.0346 & 0 & 0.0346 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -0.02 & 0 & -0.02 & 0 & 0.04 \\ -0.02 & -0.0346 & -0.02 & 0.0346 & 0.04 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
B = \begin{bmatrix} -25.0 & 0 & 25.0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -14.45 & 0 & -14.45 & 0 & 28.9 \\ -14.45 & -25.0 & -14.45 & 25.0 & 28.9 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — D-matrix:**

$$
D = \frac{70\times10^9}{1-0.1089}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.33 & 0 \\ 0.33 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.335 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 78.55 & 25.92 & 0 \\ 25.92 & 78.55 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 26.32 \end{bmatrix} \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $k = tAB^TD B$:**

The factor $tA = 0.002 \times 6.92 \times 10^{-4} = 1.384 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$.

After matrix multiplication (in SI units, then scaling):

$$
k_e = \begin{bmatrix}
1381.0 & 451.0 & -710.5 & -213.6 & -670.5 & -237.4 \\
451.0 & 1015.5 & -213.6 & -422.4 & -237.4 & -593.1 \\
-710.5 & -213.6 & 1381.0 & 451.0 & -670.5 & -237.4 \\
-213.6 & -422.4 & 451.0 & 1015.5 & -237.4 & -593.1 \\
-670.5 & -237.4 & -670.5 & -237.4 & 1341.0 & 474.8 \\
-237.4 & -593.1 & -237.4 & -593.1 & 474.8 & 1186.2
\end{bmatrix} \text{ kN/m}
$$

> *(7 marks awarded for complete numerical computation with all intermediate steps.)*

---

### **Question B — Axisymmetric Element**

> **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]`**

**(a)** For an axisymmetric problem, derive the **4×4 constitutive (D) matrix** and the **B-matrix** for a 4-node quadrilateral ring element, clearly showing the origin of the term $\epsilon_\theta = u/r$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical pressure hull has internal radius 1.0 m, wall thickness 0.05 m, and is subjected to an internal pressure of 5 MPa. Using **one** 4-node axisymmetric element (with nodes placed at *r* = 1.0, 1.025, 1.05 and *z* = 0, 0.1, 0.2), set up the equation $k_e u_e = f_e$ and identify the **boundary conditions** and **applied nodal forces**. Use *E* = 210 GPa, *ν* = 0.3. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks)**

In cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$, the only non-zero strain components for an axisymmetric body are:

$$
\epsilon_r = \frac{\partial u}{\partial r}, \quad \epsilon_z = \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}, \quad \epsilon_\theta = \frac{u}{r}, \quad \gamma_{rz} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial r}
$$

The third relation comes from the kinematics of a circular ring: a point at radius *r* moving radially by *u* traces an arc whose length changes from $r\theta$ to $(r+u)\theta$, giving a circumferential strain of $u/r$. *(2 marks)*

Using the constitutive law $\sigma = D\epsilon$ with $\sigma = \{\sigma_r, \sigma_z, \sigma_\theta, \tau_{rz}\}^T$ and applying Hooke's law in cylindrical coordinates:

$$
D_{AX} = \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}\begin{bmatrix} 1-\nu & \nu & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1-\nu & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & \nu & 1-\nu & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & (1-2\nu)/2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*(2 marks for D-matrix)*

The B-matrix for a 4-node quadrilateral (with shape functions $N_i$) is:

$$
B = \begin{bmatrix} \partial N_1/\partial r & 0 & \cdots & \partial N_4/\partial r & 0 \\ 0 & \partial N_1/\partial z & \cdots & 0 & \partial N_4/\partial z \\ N_1/r & 0 & \cdots & N_4/r & 0 \\ \partial N_1/\partial z & \partial N_1/\partial r & \cdots & \partial N_4/\partial z & \partial N_4/\partial r \end{bmatrix}
$$

*(3 marks: 2 for the structure, 1 for highlighting the $N_i/r$ row.)*

**(b) Numerical Setup (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Material matrix D:**

$$
D_{AX} = \frac{210\times10^9}{1.3 \times 0.4}\begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.3 & 0.3 & 0 \\ 0.3 & 0.7 & 0.3 & 0 \\ 0.3 & 0.3 & 0.7 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
D_{AX} = \begin{bmatrix} 282.7 & 121.2 & 121.2 & 0 \\ 121.2 & 282.7 & 121.2 & 0 \\ 121.2 & 121.2 & 282.7 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 80.77 \end{bmatrix} \text{ GPa}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Element volume and force vector:**

The internal pressure of 5 MPa acts on the inner cylindrical surface (at *r* = 1.0 m). For a one-element model, this pressure is equivalent to a distributed load. By consistent nodal load formulation:

$$
f_e = \int_{S} N^T \, p \, dS
$$

where *p* = 5 MPa acts radially inward at the inner surface.

**Step 3 — Boundary conditions:**

For a long pressure hull with closed ends neglected, **axisymmetric boundary conditions**:
- Nodes on the axis (*r* = 0, if any): *u* = 0
- Nodes at mid-length symmetry plane: *w* = 0 (to enforce symmetry)
- Outer surface: traction-free $\sigma_r = 0$
- Inner surface: $\sigma_r = -5$ MPa *(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Equation form:**

$$
\boxed{\, k_e u_e = f_e \quad \text{with} \quad k_e = 2\pi \int_{A_e} B^T D B \, r \, dr \, dz \,}
$$

The numerical evaluation uses $2 \times 2$ Gauss quadrature, summing over the four Gauss points with appropriate weights $W_g$ and Jacobian $\vert J_g \vert$. *(3 marks: 1 for the equation, 1 for the integration scheme, 1 for the force vector identification.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Marks-Loss Traps in Module 2:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `2π` factor in axisymmetric stiffness** — or worse, including `2πr` everywhere *and* an extra `2π` at the end. Result: stiffness off by 6.28×.
> 2. **Using the plane-stress D-matrix for a long retaining wall** — examiners deduct 3–4 marks for this single conceptual blunder.
> 3. **Omitting the $N_i/r$ row in the axisymmetric B-matrix** — this silently zeroes out the hoop strain. Marks lost: 3.
> 4. **Treating the axisymmetric problem as a 2D plane-strain problem** — both have *r* and *z*, but the third row of B is fundamentally different. Examiners spot this instantly.
> 5. **Sign errors in β, γ coefficients** — write the cyclic order (i, j, k) explicitly on your answer sheet. This single safeguard prevents 2-mark losses on every derivation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read before entering the exam hall:**

- ☐ **Plane Stress:** $\sigma_z = 0$, derive $\epsilon_z = -\nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)/E$ post-solution. **Use when:** thin plates loaded in-plane.
- ☐ **Plane Strain:** $\epsilon_z = 0$, derive $\sigma_z = \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)$ post-solution. **Use when:** long prismatic bodies, far from ends.
- ☐ **Axisymmetric:** Geometry, load, BCs all symmetric about an axis. The 2D meridian in *r–z* is analysed. **Key strain:** $\epsilon_\theta = u/r$.
- ☐ **D-matrix denominators:** Plane stress uses $1 - \nu^2$; plane strain and axisymmetric both use $(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)$.
- ☐ **CST B-matrix is constant** because the shape functions are linear. This makes the stiffness integral trivial.
- ☐ **Q4/Q8 axisymmetric B-matrix has a $N_i/r$ row** that breaks constant-B property; Gauss integration with the volume element $2\pi r \, dr \, dz$ is mandatory.
- ☐ **Per-node DOFs:** 2 for all three idealizations — (*u, v*) for plane problems; (*u, w*) for axisymmetric.
- ☐ **Element stiffness formula:** Always $k_e = \int B^T D B \, dV$ with the correct $dV$:
    - Plane stress/strain: $dV = t \, dA$
    - Axisymmetric: $dV = 2\pi r \, dr \, dz$
- ☐ **Stress recovery:** $\sigma = D\epsilon$ at Gauss points, then extrapolate to nodes if needed.
- ☐ **Naval architecture quick-identification:**
    - Hull plate, deck plate, bulkhead panel → **Plane Stress**
    - Long girder, long shaft section → **Plane Strain**
    - Pressure hull, propeller hub, torpedo casing → **Axisymmetric**

> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU Module 2 questions almost always begin with the phrase *"Distinguish between…"* or *"Derive the stiffness matrix for…"*. Master the B-matrix and D-matrix derivations for all three idealizations — these alone secure 60–70% of the marks available in this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formulation of Plate Finite Elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST753 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS IN NAVAL ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Formulation of Plate Finite Elements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Plate Finite Elements?

A **Plate Finite Element** is a two-dimensional structural element formulated to model the flexural (bending) behavior of thin or moderately thick flat structural components. In naval architecture, plate elements are fundamental for analyzing ship hull plating, deck panels, bulkheads, and bottom shells — all of which are subjected to transverse loads (such as hydrostatic pressure, sea loads, and cargo weights).

Mathematically, a plate element discretizes a continuous plate domain $\Omega$ into a finite number of sub-domains (elements), over which the governing differential equation of plate bending is approximated using assumed displacement fields (shape functions).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> A plate finite element is an element whose kinematic behavior is governed by the deflection $w(x,y)$ of the plate mid-surface, with additional rotational degrees of freedom $\theta_x$ and $\theta_y$ for thick plate formulations. The element stiffness matrix $[K]^e$ is derived by satisfying the weak form (Galerkin) of the plate equilibrium equation.

## 1.2 Two Governing Theories in Plate FEM

| Theory | Assumption | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Classical Plate Theory (CPT)** — Kirchhoff-Love | Transverse shear deformation is neglected (straight normals remain straight AND normal to the mid-surface) | Thin plates where $h/L < 1/20$ |
| **First-Order Shear Deformation Theory (FSDT)** — Mindlin-Reissner | Transverse shear deformation is included (straight normals remain straight but NOT necessarily normal) | Moderately thick plates where $1/20 < h/L < 1/10$ |

Where $h$ is the plate thickness and $L$ is the characteristic length (span).

> [!NOTE]
> For ship plating, both theories are used. The choice depends on the **span-to-thickness ratio** of the plate panel under analysis. The hull bottom plating in a tanker is often a thick plate, while deck plating in a frigate may behave as thin.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Jigsaw Plate"

Imagine a square table top (a plate) supported at four corners. Instead of solving the complex differential equation governing the deflection of the entire table, we break the table into a jigsaw of smaller rectangular pieces (finite elements). For each piece, we **assume a simple deflection shape** (a polynomial), then glue the pieces back together at the edges ensuring continuity.

- Each jigsaw piece ≈ **A plate element**
- The assumed deflection shape ≈ **Shape function ($N_i$)**
- The stiffness of each piece ≈ **Element stiffness matrix $[K]^e$**
- The glued-back-together assembly ≈ **Global stiffness matrix $[K]$**

This is the essence of the **Finite Element Method (FEM)** applied to plates.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard quantities appear in plate FEM formulation:

- **Young's Modulus** $E$ — measured in **N/m²** or **Pa**
- **Poisson's Ratio** $\nu$ — dimensionless, typically **0.3** for ship steel
- **Plate thickness** $h$ — measured in **m**
- **Flexural rigidity** $D = \dfrac{Eh^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ — measured in **N·m**
- **Shear rigidity** $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ — measured in **N/m²**
- **Mass density** $\rho$ — measured in **kg/m³**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For naval architecture plate FEM, the plate material is **isotropic mild steel** (or aluminium for fast craft), so the **constitutive matrix [D]** is symmetric with the same $D$ in both directions. The Poisson's ratio $\nu$ is a critical parameter — it couples bending in the $x$ and $y$ directions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Displacement Field in a Plate Element

The mid-surface of a plate undergoes a transverse deflection $w(x,y)$. The complete displacement field in the **Kirchhoff (CPT) formulation** is:

$$
u(x,y,z) = -z \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}, \quad v(x,y,z) = -z \frac{\partial w}{\partial y}, \quad w(x,y,z) = w(x,y)
$$

For the **Mindlin (FSDT) formulation**, independent rotations are introduced:

$$
\theta_x = \frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + \phi_x, \quad \theta_y = \frac{\partial w}{\partial y} + \phi_y
$$

Where $\phi_x$ and $\phi_y$ are the additional shear-induced rotations.

## 2.2 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) Per Node

The number of DOFs depends on the element type:

| Element Type | DOFs per Node | DOFs per Rectangular Element |
|---|---|---|
| **Kirchhoff Plate Element (ACM)** | $w, \theta_x, \theta_y$ (3 DOFs) | 12 (4 nodes × 3) |
| **Mindlin Plate Element (DKT, MITC)** | $w, \theta_x, \theta_y$ (3 DOFs) | 12 (4 nodes × 3) |
| **Discrete Kirchhoff Triangle (DKT)** | $w, \theta_x, \theta_y$ (3 DOFs) | 9 (3 nodes × 3) |

> [!NOTE]
> In CPT, $\theta_x = \partial w/\partial x$ and $\theta_y = \partial w/\partial y$ are **dependent** on $w$. In FSDT, they are **independent** DOFs — this makes FSDT formulations richer and able to capture shear deformation.

## 2.3 Strain-Displacement Relations (Curvatures)

The strain state in a plate is described by curvatures $\kappa$. For CPT:

$$
\boldsymbol{\kappa} = \begin{Bmatrix} -\dfrac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} \\[6pt] -\dfrac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} \\[6pt] -2\dfrac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x \partial y} \end{Bmatrix}
$$

For FSDT, the strain state includes curvatures and shear strains:

$$
\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} = \begin{Bmatrix} \kappa_x \\ \kappa_y \\ \gamma_{xy} \\ \gamma_{xz} \\ \gamma_{yz} \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} B_b & 0 \\ 0 & B_s \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \theta \\ w \end{Bmatrix}
$$

## 2.4 Constitutive Relations (D-Matrix)

For an **isotropic plate**, the bending constitutive matrix is:

$$
[D_b] = \frac{E h^3}{12(1-\nu^2)} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \dfrac{1-\nu}{2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

And the transverse shear constitutive matrix is:

$$
[D_s] = \frac{E h}{2(1+\nu)} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \times \kappa_s
$$

Where $\kappa_s$ is the **shear correction factor** (typically $\kappa_s = 5/6$ for rectangular sections).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lbrace d \rbrace^e = [N] \lbrace q \rbrace$ | Element displacement field |
| 2 | $\lbrace \kappa \rbrace = [B] \lbrace q \rbrace$ | Strain-nodal relation |
| 3 | $\lbrace M \rbrace = [D] \lbrace \kappa \rbrace$ | Moment-curvature relation |
| 4 | $[K]^e = \int\!\!\int [B]^T [D] [B] \, dA$ | Element stiffness matrix (CPT) |
| 5 | $[K]^e = \int\!\!\int [B_b]^T [D_b] [B_b] \, dA + \int\!\!\int [B_s]^T [D_s] [B_s] \, dA$ | Element stiffness matrix (FSDT) |
| 6 | $\lbrace F \rbrace^e = \int\!\!\int [N]^T q \, dA + \int [N]^T t \, ds$ | Equivalent nodal load vector |
| 7 | $D = \dfrac{E h^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ | Flexural rigidity |
| 8 | $\nu_{xy} = \nu_{yx}$ | Symmetry of Poisson's ratio |
| 9 | $\xi = \dfrac{x - x_c}{a/2}, \quad \eta = \dfrac{y - y_c}{b/2}$ | Natural coordinates (mapping) |
| 10 | $\lbrace F \rbrace_{eq} = \int_{-1}^{1}\int_{-1}^{1} [N]^T q \lvert J \rvert \, d\xi \, d\eta$ | Load vector in natural coords |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Exam Tip:** The Poisson's ratio $\nu$ appears both in the constitutive matrix $[D_b]$ AND in the flexural rigidity $D$. A common error is forgetting the factor $(1-\nu^2)$ in the denominator of $D$. Always write the full form.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In naval architecture, plate finite elements are used to:

1. **Analyze local strength of ship hull plating** under hydrostatic pressure at various drafts
2. **Predict buckling loads** of bottom and side shell plates
3. **Compute vibration frequencies** of deck panels (important for submarine stealth)
4. **Evaluate fatigue hotspots** at plate intersections
5. **Optimize scantlings** (plate thicknesses) per classification society rules (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for ship design:** A typical oil tanker has hull plating thicknesses between 12–25 mm. Misjudging plate stiffness by even 10% can lead to either **over-design** (excess steel, wasted deadweight) or **under-design** (fatigue cracks, structural failure). Plate FEM is the engineering tool that prevents both.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Plate Element Stiffness Matrix — Classical Thin Plate (CPT) Formulation

We will derive $[K]^e$ for a **4-noded rectangular Kirchhoff plate element** (also called the **ACM element** — Adini, Clough, Melosh) following the KTU-expected step-by-step methodology.

### Step 1: Define the Nodal Degrees of Freedom

For a 4-noded rectangular plate element of dimensions $2a \times 2b$ (in natural coordinates $\xi, \eta$), each node has 3 DOFs:

$$
\{q_i\} = \begin{Bmatrix} w_i \\ \theta_{xi} \\ \theta_{yi} \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} w_i \\ \left(\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial y}\right)_i \\ -\left(\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial x}\right)_i \end{Bmatrix}
$$

The total nodal displacement vector (12 DOFs) is:

$$
\{q\}^e = \{w_1 \; \theta_{x1} \; \theta_{y1} \; w_2 \; \theta_{x2} \; \theta_{y2} \; w_3 \; \theta_{x3} \; \theta_{y3} \; w_4 \; \theta_{x4} \; \theta_{y4}\}^T
$$

### Step 2: Choose Shape Functions (Hermitian Polynomials)

The transverse deflection within the element is interpolated using **Hermitian shape functions** to ensure $C^1$ continuity (continuity of $w$ and its slopes across element boundaries — required for Kirchhoff theory).

$$
w(\xi, \eta) = [N_1 \; N_2 \; N_3 \; \ldots \; N_{12}] \{q\}^e
$$

The shape functions are products of 1D Hermite cubics in $\xi$ and $\eta$:

$$
N_i = H_{i1}(\xi) H_{i1}(\eta), \quad \text{etc.}
$$

Explicitly, the 1D Hermite shape functions are:

$$
H_{01} = \frac{1}{4}(2 - 3\xi + \xi^3), \quad H_{02} = \frac{1}{4}(1 - \xi - \xi^2 + \xi^3)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Hermite and not Lagrange?** Kirchhoff plate theory requires the second derivatives of $w$ to exist and be continuous (because curvatures depend on $\partial^2 w/\partial x^2$). Lagrange polynomials of sufficient order would require 16 nodes for a rectangular element. Hermite polynomials achieve $C^1$ continuity with only 4 nodes and 12 DOFs.

### Step 3: Express Curvatures in Terms of Nodal DOFs

The curvatures are the second derivatives of $w$:

$$
\{\kappa\} = \begin{Bmatrix} -\dfrac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} \\[4pt] -\dfrac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} \\[4pt] -2\dfrac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x \partial y} \end{Bmatrix} = [B] \{q\}^e
$$

Where $[B]$ is the **strain-displacement matrix** (12 columns, 3 rows). The transformation from natural $(\xi, \eta)$ to physical $(x, y)$ coordinates is needed for the second derivatives, using the Jacobian:

$$
[J] = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial \xi} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \xi} \\[4pt] \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial \eta} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \eta} \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4: Apply Constitutive Law

The bending moments per unit length are:

$$
\{M\} = \begin{Bmatrix} M_x \\ M_y \\ M_{xy} \end{Bmatrix} = [D_b] \{\kappa\} = [D_b] [B] \{q\}^e
$$

### Step 5: Derive the Stiffness Matrix Using the Principle of Minimum Potential Energy

The total strain energy of the plate element is:

$$
U^e = \frac{1}{2} \int\!\!\int_A \{\kappa\}^T [D_b] \{\kappa\} \, dA
$$

Substituting $\{\kappa\} = [B]\{q\}^e$:

$$
U^e = \frac{1}{2} \{q\}^{eT} \left( \int\!\!\int_A [B]^T [D_b] [B] \, dA \right) \{q\}^e
$$

The external work done by transverse load $q(x,y)$ is:

$$
W^e = \int\!\!\int_A w \cdot q \, dA = \{q\}^{eT} \int\!\!\int_A [N]^T q \, dA
$$

The total potential energy is $\Pi = U^e - W^e$. Applying the stationarity condition $\partial \Pi / \partial \{q\}^e = 0$:

$$
[K]^e \{q\}^e = \{F\}^e
$$

Therefore, the **element stiffness matrix** is:

$$
[K]^e = \int\!\!\int_A [B]^T [D_b] [B] \, dA
$$

Converting to natural coordinates $(\xi, \eta)$ where $dA = \lvert J \rvert \, d\xi \, d\eta$:

$$
[K]^e = \int_{-1}^{1}\!\!\int_{-1}^{1} [B(\xi,\eta)]^T [D_b] [B(\xi,\eta)] \lvert J(\xi,\eta) \rvert \, d\xi \, d\eta
$$

### Step 6: Numerical Integration (Gauss Quadrature)

Since $[B]$ contains polynomial functions, exact integration is possible but cumbersome. **2 × 2 Gauss quadrature** is typically used:

$$
[K]^e = \sum_{i=1}^{2} \sum_{j=1}^{2} [B(\xi_i, \eta_j)]^T [D_b] [B(\xi_i, \eta_j)] \lvert J(\xi_i, \eta_j) \rvert W_i W_j
$$

Where $W_i = W_j = 1$ and Gauss points are $\xi_i, \eta_j = \pm \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The minimum Gauss rule for $2a \times 2b$ rectangular Kirchhoff elements is **2 × 2** to integrate the polynomial products exactly.

## 3.2 Derivation of Equivalent Nodal Load Vector

For a uniformly distributed transverse load $q_0$ (such as hydrostatic pressure on a hull plate), the equivalent nodal load vector is:

$$
\{F\}^e = \int\!\!\int_A [N]^T q_0 \, dA
$$

In natural coordinates:

$$
\{F\}^e = q_0 \int_{-1}^{1}\!\!\int_{-1}^{1} [N(\xi,\eta)]^T \lvert J \rvert \, d\xi \, d\eta
$$

Since the load is uniform and $[N]$ is Hermitian, the load vector simplifies to specific nodal values. The **corner nodes** receive $\frac{1}{4} q_0 ab$ each, and the **rotational DOFs** receive moments derived from the load distribution.

## 3.3 Summary of the Formulation Pipeline

The complete formulation chain is:

$$
\text{Shape functions } [N] \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Strain matrix } [B] \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Stiffness } [K]^e
$$

$$
\text{Load } q \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Nodal loads } \{F\}^e \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Assembly } [K], \{F\}
$$

$$
\text{Solve } [K]\{Q\} = \{F\} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Post-process: } w, \theta_x, \theta_y, M_x, M_y, M_{xy}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Plate Element Formulation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT: Plate Geometry h, a, b, Material E, nu, Load q0"] --> B["Step 1: Define Nodal DOFs 12 DOFs per element"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Construct Hermitian Shape Functions N_i"]
    C --> D["Step 3: Compute Strain-Displacement Matrix B"]
    D --> E["Step 4: Apply Constitutive Law D matrix"]
    E --> F["Step 5: Assemble Element Stiffness K_e via Gauss Quadrature"]
    A --> G["Step 6: Compute Equivalent Nodal Loads F_e"]
    F --> H["Step 7: Assembly Global K and Global F"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Step 8: Apply Boundary Conditions SS, CC, FF"]
    I --> J["Step 9: Solve Linear System K Q = F"]
    J --> K["Step 10: Post-Process w, theta, M, V"]
    K --> L["OUTPUT: Deflections and Stress Resultants"]
```

## 4.2 Plate Element Discretization Concept

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Continuous Plate Domain Omega"] --> B["Discretized Mesh: N_e elements"]
    B --> C["Element 1: Nodes 1-2-3-4"]
    B --> D["Element 2: Nodes 3-4-5-6"]
    B --> E["Element 3: Nodes 5-6-7-8"]
    C --> F["Assumed Shape Function N_i on each element"]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G["Stiffness Matrix K_e per element"]
    G --> H["Global Assembly: K = sum K_e"]
    H --> I["Solve: K Q = F for Q"]
```

## 4.3 Flowchart: CPT vs FSDT Decision Logic for Naval Plates

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Identify Plate Panel"] --> Check1["Compute span-to-thickness ratio L/h"]
    Check1 --> Dec1{"L/h greater than 20?"}
    Dec1 -->|"Yes"| UseCPT["Use Kirchhoff CPT Formulation: ACM Element, 3 DOF per node, 12 per element"]
    Dec1 -->|"No"| Dec2{"L/h between 10 and 20?"}
    Dec2 -->|"Yes"| UseFSDT["Use Mindlin FSDT Formulation: DKT or MITC, 3 DOF per node, 12 per element"]
    Dec2 -->|"No"| UseSolid["Use 3D Solid Element: 20-noded brick, 60 DOF per element"]
    UseCPT --> Output["Result: Bending stresses only, no shear"]
    UseFSDT --> Output2["Result: Bending plus transverse shear"]
    UseSolid --> Output3["Result: Full 3D stress state"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Naval Architecture Mapping:** Ship deck plating ($L/h \approx 30$–$50$) → CPT. Bulkhead plates ($L/h \approx 12$–$18$) → FSDT. Local stress concentrations at cutouts → 3D solid.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the basic assumptions of Classical Plate Theory (Kirchhoff-Love) used in the formulation of thin plate finite elements.**

**Model Answer:**

The Classical Plate Theory (CPT) is based on the following assumptions:

1. **Straight normal hypothesis:** A line originally normal to the mid-surface remains straight, inextensible, and normal to the deformed mid-surface after deformation.
2. **Negligible transverse shear deformation:** The transverse shear strain $\gamma_{xz}$ and $\gamma_{yz}$ are set to zero.
3. **Negligible transverse normal stress:** $\sigma_z \approx 0$ compared to in-plane stresses $\sigma_x$ and $\sigma_y$.
4. **Thin plate assumption:** The plate thickness $h$ is much smaller than the in-plane dimensions, i.e., $h/L < 1/20$.

Under these assumptions, the rotations $\theta_x$ and $\theta_y$ are derived from the deflection $w$ as $\theta_x = \partial w/\partial y$ and $\theta_y = -\partial w/\partial x$.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the flexural rigidity $D$ of a plate? Write its expression and explain the significance of Poisson's ratio in it.**

**Model Answer:**

The **flexural rigidity** $D$ of a plate is a measure of its resistance to bending deformation. It depends on the plate's material properties and thickness cubed.

**Expression:**

$$
D = \frac{E h^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)}
$$

Where:
- $E$ = Young's Modulus (Pa)
- $h$ = plate thickness (m)
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio (dimensionless)

**Significance of Poisson's Ratio:** The term $(1-\nu^2)$ in the denominator of $D$ accounts for the **constraint against lateral contraction** when the plate bends. When a plate bends in the $x$-direction, it would naturally contract in the $y$-direction, but the surrounding material prevents this, effectively **stiffening** the plate. For $\nu = 0.3$, the factor $1/(1-\nu^2) \approx 1.10$, increasing the effective rigidity by 10% compared to a uniaxial beam.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks): Module 3 Choice Option A**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the element stiffness matrix for a 4-noded rectangular Kirchhoff thin plate element. Clearly state the degrees of freedom, the shape functions used, and the strain-displacement relations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A square plate of side $2 \text{ m} \times 2 \text{ m}$, thickness $h = 0.05 \text{ m}$, is simply supported on all four edges and subjected to a uniformly distributed load of $q_0 = 10 \text{ kN/m}^2$. The material is mild steel with $E = 210 \text{ GPa}$ and $\nu = 0.3$. Using a single 4-noded rectangular plate element, compute the central deflection and the bending moment at the center. Compare with the classical analytical solution.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A(a):**

**Step 1 — Degrees of Freedom per node:** 3 DOFs: $\{w_i, \theta_{xi}, \theta_{yi}\}$ = $\{w_i, (\partial w/\partial y)_i, -(\partial w/\partial x)_i\}$. Total per element = **12 DOFs**.

**Step 2 — Shape functions:** Hermitian polynomials to enforce $C^1$ continuity:

$$
w(\xi,\eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} \left[ H_{01}(\xi_i) H_{01}(\eta_i) w_i + H_{02}(\xi_i) H_{01}(\eta_i) a \theta_{xi} + H_{01}(\xi_i) H_{02}(\eta_i) b \theta_{yi} \right]
$$

Where $H_{01} = \frac{1}{4}(2 - 3\xi + \xi^3)$ and $H_{02} = \frac{1}{4}(1 - \xi - \xi^2 + \xi^3)$.

**Step 3 — Strain-Displacement Relations:**

$$
\{\kappa\} = \begin{Bmatrix} -\partial^2 w/\partial x^2 \\ -\partial^2 w/\partial y^2 \\ -2\partial^2 w/\partial x \partial y \end{Bmatrix} = [B] \{q\}^e
$$

**Step 4 — Stiffness Matrix Derivation (using Principle of Minimum Potential Energy):**

$$
[K]^e = \int_{-1}^{1}\!\!\int_{-1}^{1} [B]^T [D_b] [B] \lvert J \rvert \, d\xi \, d\eta
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate using 2 × 2 Gauss quadrature:**

$$
[K]^e = \sum_{i=1}^{2} \sum_{j=1}^{2} [B(\xi_i, \eta_j)]^T [D_b] [B(\xi_i, \eta_j)] \lvert J \rvert W_i W_j
$$

The resulting $[K]^e$ is a $12 \times 12$ symmetric positive-definite matrix expressed in terms of $a$, $b$, $E$, $h$, and $\nu$.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute Flexural Rigidity:**

$$
D = \frac{(210 \times 10^9)(0.05)^3}{12(1 - 0.3^2)} = \frac{210 \times 10^9 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}}{12 \times 0.91} = 2.404 \times 10^6 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 2 — Assemble the 12 × 12 Element Stiffness Matrix:**
Using the standard ACM element stiffness coefficients (tabulated in FEA textbooks), the diagonal entry for the central transverse deflection coupling is:

$$
K_{ww} = \frac{4D}{a^2 b^2} \times \text{geometric factor}
$$

For a square plate with $a = b = 1 \text{ m}$:

$$
K_{ww} = \frac{4 \times 2.404 \times 10^6}{1^2 \times 1^2} \times 4 = 3.846 \times 10^7 \text{ N/m}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Boundary Conditions:**
For simply supported edges, $w = 0$ and $M_n = 0$ on the boundary. This sets $w = 0$ at all 4 corner nodes and imposes $\theta = 0$ for the natural boundary condition (in a single-element model, this is approximated by symmetry, leaving 3 unknown DOFs at the center: $w, \theta_x, \theta_y$).

**Step 4 — Compute the Equivalent Nodal Load:**

$$
F_w = q_0 \times ab = 10{,}000 \times 1 \times 1 = 10{,}000 \text{ N at each corner node}
$$

By symmetry, the equivalent load at the center (in a refined model) is $q_0 \times a \times b$.

**Step 5 — Solve for Central Deflection:**

For the simplified single-element model, the central deflection is:

$$
w_{center} = \frac{0.00126 \cdot q_0 a^4}{D} = \frac{0.00126 \times 10{,}000 \times 1^4}{2.404 \times 10^6} = 5.24 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 5.24 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare with Classical Analytical Solution (Timoshenko):**
For a simply supported square plate under uniform load:

$$
w_{max} = \frac{0.00406 \cdot q_0 a^4}{D} = \frac{0.00406 \times 10{,}000 \times 1^4}{2.404 \times 10^6} = 1.69 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 16.9 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

**Conclusion:** The single ACM element severely underestimates the deflection because it assumes a specific polynomial variation. A refined mesh ($4 \times 4$ or finer) is required to approach the Timoshenko solution. The bending moment at the center from FEM is $M_x = M_y = 0.0479 q_0 a^2 = 479 \text{ N·m/m}$, which is close to the analytical $0.0479 q_0 a^2$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Module 3 Choice Option B**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the Classical Plate Theory (Kirchhoff) with the First-Order Shear Deformation Theory (Mindlin) for the formulation of plate finite elements. Discuss when each is appropriate in naval architecture applications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a 4-noded Mindlin plate element, derive the element stiffness matrix showing explicitly the contributions from bending and transverse shear. Explain the phenomenon of **shear locking** and how it can be prevented.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B(a):**

| Aspect | Classical Plate Theory (CPT) | First-Order Shear Deformation Theory (FSDT) |
|---|---|---|
| Displacement assumption | $u = -z \partial w/\partial x$, $v = -z \partial w/\partial y$ | $u = z \theta_y$, $v = -z \theta_x$ (with $\theta_x, \theta_y$ independent) |
| Shear deformation | Neglected | Included |
| Rotations | Derived from $w$ | Independent DOFs |
| Continuity required | $C^1$ (continuous $w$ and slopes) | $C^0$ (only $w$ continuous) |
| Element DOFs (4-node rect) | 12 (3 per node) | 12 (3 per node) |
| Shape functions | Hermitian cubics | Lagrange bilinear or higher |
| Constitutive matrix | $[D_b]$ only | $[D_b]$ and $[D_s]$ |
| Applicable range | Thin plates $L/h > 20$ | Moderately thick $10 < L/h < 20$ |
| Naval arch example | Deck plating, superstructure panels | Bottom shell, bulkhead plates |

**Naval Architecture Applications:**

- **CPT is used for:** Deck plating of large container ships, superstructure side shells, and tank tops where the plate is thin and shear deformation is negligible.
- **FSDT is used for:** Double bottom plates of tankers, side shell frames in way of heavy concentrated loads, and bulkhead plates where the thickness is significant.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B(b):**

**Derivation of Mindlin Element Stiffness Matrix:**

The strain vector for the Mindlin element has two parts: bending and shear.

$$
\{\varepsilon\} = \begin{Bmatrix} \kappa \\ \gamma \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} B_b \\ B_s \end{bmatrix} \{q\}^e
$$

Where:
- $\kappa = \{\partial \theta_y/\partial x, -\partial \theta_x/\partial y, \partial \theta_y/\partial y - \partial \theta_x/\partial x\}^T$ (curvatures)
- $\gamma = \{\partial w/\partial x + \theta_y, \partial w/\partial y - \theta_x\}^T$ (shear strains)

The element stiffness matrix is:

$$
[K]^e = \int\!\!\int_A \left( [B_b]^T [D_b] [B_b] + [B_s]^T [D_s] [B_s] \right) dA
$$

Where:

$$
[D_b] = \frac{E h^3}{12(1-\nu^2)} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \frac{1-\nu}{2} \end{bmatrix}, \quad [D_s] = \frac{E h \kappa_s}{2(1+\nu)} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Shear Locking Explained:**

Shear locking is a **numerical pathology** that occurs in low-order Mindlin plate elements when the plate becomes thin ($h \to 0$). As $h$ decreases, the shear stiffness $G h \to 0$ physically, but in the displacement formulation, the shear strain energy term **artificially dominates** because the elements cannot represent the kinematic constraint $\gamma_{xz} = \gamma_{yz} = 0$ that must hold in the thin-plate limit.

**Mathematical origin:** The shear energy term is $O(h)$, while the bending term is $O(h^3)$. For thin plates, the constraint enforces $\theta_x \to \partial w/\partial y$ and $\theta_y \to -\partial w/\partial x$, but the element cannot capture this exactly, leading to parasitic shear strains that over-stiffen the element. The element stiffness becomes unbounded as $h \to 0$, and the deflections go to zero — locking the structure.

**Remedies for Shear Locking:**

1. **Selective Integration (Reduced Integration):** Use lower-order quadrature (1-point or 2-point) for the shear term to relax the constraint. For a 4-node Mindlin element, use $2 \times 2$ for bending and $1 \times 1$ for shear.
2. **Assumed Natural Strain (ANS) Method:** Interpolate the shear strains in a way that satisfies the thin-plate constraint.
3. **Mixed Interpolation of Tensorial Components (MITC):** Use separate interpolation schemes for different strain components, ensuring that the shear strains collapse correctly as $h \to 0$.
4. **Discrete Kirchhoff (DKT) Method:** Enforce the Kirchhoff constraint $\gamma = 0$ at discrete points within the element.
5. **Higher-order Elements:** Use 8-noded or 9-noded serendipity elements, which are less prone to locking.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **not** confuse shear locking with **membrane locking** (which occurs in shell elements).
> - Selective/reduced integration can introduce **hourglass modes** (zero-energy modes); these must be checked or stabilized.
> - Always state the **shear correction factor** $\kappa_s = 5/6$ explicitly; omitting it loses 1 mark.
> - Many students write $[K]^e$ without distinguishing the bending and shear contributions; **always show them as a sum** to earn full marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Revision Checklist — Plate Finite Element Formulation**

- [ ] **CPT (Kirchhoff)** is for thin plates ($L/h > 20$); uses **Hermitian shape functions**; requires $C^1$ continuity; stiffness matrix is $12 \times 12$ per element.
- [ ] **FSDT (Mindlin)** is for moderately thick plates ($10 < L/h < 20$); uses **Lagrange shape functions**; requires only $C^0$ continuity; stiffness matrix is also $12 \times 12$ but includes a separate **shear stiffness term**.
- [ ] **Flexural rigidity:** $D = E h^3 / [12(1-\nu^2)]$ — the $(1-\nu^2)$ factor is mandatory.
- [ ] **Constitutive matrix** $[D_b]$ is symmetric, $3 \times 3$ for bending; $[D_s]$ is diagonal, $2 \times 2$ for shear.
- [ ] **Element stiffness:** $[K]^e = \int [B]^T [D] [B] \, dA$ — derived from the principle of **minimum potential energy** (Galerkin method gives the same result for self-adjoint problems).
- [ ] **Equivalent nodal loads:** $\lbrace F \rbrace^e = \int [N]^T q \, dA$; for uniform load, corners get $q_0 ab / 4$ and edges get $q_0 ab / 2$ each (for some schemes).
- [ ] **Gauss Quadrature:** Minimum $2 \times 2$ for 4-noded rectangular Kirchhoff; $2 \times 2$ for bending and $1 \times 1$ for shear in Mindlin (to prevent shear locking).
- [ ] **Shear locking** occurs in low-order Mindlin elements as $h \to 0$; remedies include **selective/reduced integration**, **MITC**, and **DKT** methods.
- [ ] **Boundary conditions for plates:** Simply supported (SS): $w = 0$, $M_n = 0$. Clamped (CC): $w = 0$, $\theta_n = 0$. Free (FF): $M_n = 0$, $V_n = 0$.
- [ ] **Isoparametric mapping:** Use natural coordinates $(\xi, \eta) \in [-1, 1]^2$ with Jacobian $[J]$; $dA = \lvert J \rvert d\xi d\eta$.
- [ ] **Naval application:** Hull bottom and side shell plates are predominantly analyzed using FSDT; deck plating and superstructure panels can use CPT.
- [ ] **Convergence:** Refining the mesh (more elements) generally improves accuracy; check convergence by **h-refinement** (smaller elements) or **p-refinement** (higher-order shape functions).
- [ ] **Always assemble** the global $[K]$ and $\{F\}$ before applying boundary conditions; **never apply BCs to individual elements**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Issues Associated with Plate Formulation of Finite Elements
Numerical Examples on Simple Plate Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST753 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS IN NAVAL ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Numerical Examples on Simple Plate Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Plate Analysis in FEA** refers to the finite element discretization and solution of two-dimensional solid elements whose thickness is small compared to their in-plane dimensions (typically $h/a \leq 1/10$), and which resist transverse loads primarily through **bending action** governed by the governing differential equation of the **Classical Plate Theory (CPT) — Kirchhoff–Love Hypothesis**:

$$D \nabla^4 w = q(x,y)$$

where $D = \dfrac{E h^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ is the **flexural rigidity of the plate**, $w$ is the transverse deflection, and $q$ is the distributed transverse load.

In **Naval Architecture**, plate analysis is the cornerstone of ship structural design because every hull shell, deck plating, bulkhead, and bottom panel is, in its simplest form, a plate subjected to hydrostatic pressure, sea loads, cargo loads, or combinations of in-plane and bending stresses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A *Simple Plate* in this module refers to a flat, isotropic, rectangular Kirchhoff plate of constant thickness, simply supported or clamped on its boundaries, loaded transversely. The numerical examples assume small deflection theory (linear elastic bending).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sail Under Storm"

Imagine a **square ship's deck plate** (say, 2 m × 2 m × 10 mm) during a storm. The hydrostatic pressure from a cargo hold pushes **upward**, and the plate — being thin and flat — behaves like a **taut sail caught by the wind**. It does *not* stretch (membrane action is neglected in CPT), it **bends**. The steel fibres on the top surface are compressed, those on the bottom are stretched.

Mathematically, we slice this plate into smaller "mini-plates" called **finite elements** (usually rectangles). At each corner of every element, we place **nodes** carrying three Degrees of Freedom (DOF):

- $w$ — vertical deflection
- $\theta_x = \partial w / \partial y$ — rotation about the x-axis
- $\theta_y = -\partial w / \partial x$ — rotation about the y-axis

Just as a sailor measuring only the *heights of sail corners* and *slopes at those corners* can predict the sail's full shape, FEA predicts the entire plate deflection field from these nodal quantities alone.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Young's Modulus of shipbuilding steel:** $E = 2.0 \times 10^{11} \text{ N/m}^2$ (**210 GPa**)
- **Poisson's ratio for steel:** $\nu = 0.3$ (dimensionless)
- **Flexural Rigidity (D):** expressed in **N·m**
- **Deflection (w):** expressed in **mm** or **m**
- **Bending Moment (M):** expressed in **N·m/m** (per unit width)
- **Uniform pressure (q):** expressed in **kN/m²** or **N/mm²**

> [!NOTE]
> In ship structural practice, plates are usually designated by their *thickness in mm × breadth in m × length in m* (e.g., 10 × 2 × 2 plate). Stiffeners are added perpendicular to the plate (forming a *grillage* or *stiffened panel*). This module focuses on the **unstiffened plate** as the base unit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Displacement Field and Strain-Displacement Relations

For the **Kirchhoff–Love Plate (CPT)**, the displacement field is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u(x,y,z) &= -z \, \frac{\partial w}{\partial x} \\
v(x,y,z) &= -z \, \frac{\partial w}{\partial y} \\
w(x,y,z) &= w(x,y)
\end{aligned}
$$

The **curvatures** (analogous to strain) are:

$$
\boldsymbol{\kappa} = 
\begin{Bmatrix} -\partial^2 w/\partial x^2 \\ -\partial^2 w/\partial y^2 \\ -2\partial^2 w/\partial x \partial y \end{Bmatrix}
$$

The **bending moments per unit length** are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_x &= -D\left(\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2}\right) \\
M_y &= -D\left(\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2}\right) \\
M_{xy} &= -D(1-\nu)\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x \partial y}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.2 Why and How of the ACM Rectangular Element

The simplest plate-bending element used in KTU-style numerical problems is the **Adini–Clough–Melosh (ACM) element** (1961). It is a **4-noded rectangle** with **12 DOF total** (3 DOF per node). Its non-conforming nature (it does not satisfy the Kirchhoff condition $\partial w/\partial n$ continuity between elements) is tolerated because:

- The element is **simple to formulate** — perfect for KTU derivations.
- It **passes the patch test** for rectangular meshes.
