- For quadrilateral: $p = 1$ (bilinear), $p = 2$ (biquadratic), $p = 3$ etc.
- Used in **p-version FEA** for plate-buckling analysis.

#### C. r-Refinement
- Moves existing nodes to improve shape quality without changing count.
- Common in ship-stiffener mesh smoothing.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Mesh Design

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Aspect Ratio | $AR$ | dimensionless | Element quality |
| 2 | Skewness | $Sk$ | dimensionless | Element quality |
| 3 | Jacobian Ratio | $J_r$ | dimensionless | Element validity |
| 4 | Warping Factor | $W$ | dimensionless | Quad plate quality |
| 5 | Discretization Error | $\|e\|_{L_2} \propto C h^{p}$ | Pa, m | Convergence study |
| 6 | Convergence Rate (h) | $\text{rate} = \frac{\log(e_1/e_2)}{\log(h_1/h_2)}$ | dimensionless | Mesh independence |
| 7 | Mesh Density (2D) | $\rho = \dfrac{N_{elem}}{A}$ | elem/m² | Quality metric |
| 8 | Aspect Ratio (Plate) | $\dfrac{L}{t}$ | dimensionless | Local buckling |

> [!NOTE]
> The **mesh-independence study** is mandatory in KTU-evaluated naval-architecture FEA reports. Students must run the same model with 3 progressive mesh sizes (coarse → medium → fine) and confirm that the quantity of interest (QOI) — e.g., **von Mises stress at the midship deck** — changes by less than **5%** between successive refinements.

### 2.5 Real-World Naval Architecture Utility

Mesh design is essential in:
- **Hull-girder ultimate strength** assessment (Smith's method, ISUM)
- **Plate-buckling** screening per DNV-RP-C201
- **Slamming load** response of bow flare plates
- **Vibration analysis** of propeller-shaft system (requires good tetrahedral mesh in shaft bearing area)
- **Fatigue hot-spot** stress evaluation per IACS S34 / DNV

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Convergence Rate from Mesh-Independence Study

**Objective:** Given three successive mesh refinements with element sizes $h_1, h_2, h_3$ and corresponding von Mises stress values $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$ at a critical hull point, derive the convergence rate and the Richardson-extrapolated "true" stress.

#### Step 1 — Assume Error Expansion

For a smooth problem with pth-order elements, the FE error has the asymptotic form:
$$\sigma_{FE} = \sigma_{exact} + C h^{p} + \mathcal{O}(h^{p+1})$$

#### Step 2 — Write for Two Consecutive Meshes

For mesh 1 (coarse):
$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_{exact} + C h_1^{p}$$

For mesh 2 (medium):
$$\sigma_2 = \sigma_{exact} + C h_2^{p}$$

#### Step 3 — Subtract to Eliminate $\sigma_{exact}$

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = C(h_1^{p} - h_2^{p})$$

#### Step 4 — Solve for Constant $C$

$$C = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{h_1^{p} - h_2^{p}}$$

#### Step 5 — Solve for $\sigma_{exact}$ (Richardson Extrapolation)

$$\sigma_{exact} \approx \sigma_2 - C h_2^{p}$$

Substituting $C$:
$$\sigma_{exact} \approx \sigma_2 - \frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)\, h_2^{p}}{h_1^{p} - h_2^{p}}$$

#### Step 6 — Solve for Convergence Rate $p$

Taking a third mesh $h_3$:
$$p = \frac{\log\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma_2 - \sigma_3}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}\right)}{\log\!\left(\dfrac{h_2}{h_1}\right)}$$

#### Step 7 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

Suppose we model a **midship hull plate** under bending with:
- Mesh 1: $h_1 = 0.50\,\text{m}$, $\sigma_1 = 215\,\text{MPa}$
- Mesh 2: $h_2 = 0.25\,\text{m}$, $\sigma_2 = 232\,\text{MPa}$
- Mesh 3: $h_3 = 0.125\,\text{m}$, $\sigma_3 = 237\,\text{MPa}$

**Calculate convergence rate $p$:**

$$p = \frac{\log\!\left(\dfrac{232 - 237}{215 - 232}\right)}{\log\!\left(\dfrac{0.25}{0.50}\right)}$$

$$p = \frac{\log(5/17)}{\log(0.5)}$$

$$p = \frac{\log(0.2941)}{\log(0.5)} = \frac{-0.5315}{-0.3010}$$

$$\boxed{p \approx 1.766}$$

**Interpretation:** The convergence rate is approximately **1.77**, which is close to the theoretical value of **p = 2** for 2D bilinear quad elements under linear-elastic bending — confirming proper mesh design.

**Calculate Richardson-extrapolated exact stress:**

$$C = \frac{215 - 232}{0.50^{1.766} - 0.25^{1.766}}$$

$$C = \frac{-17}{0.2953 - 0.1463} = \frac{-17}{0.1490} \approx -114.09$$

$$\sigma_{exact} \approx 232 - (-114.09)(0.25)^{1.766}$$

$$\sigma_{exact} \approx 232 - (-114.09)(0.1463) \approx 232 + 16.69$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{exact} \approx 248.7\,\text{MPa}}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Element Quality vs. Solution Accuracy

| Mesh Quality | Aspect Ratio | Skewness | Jacobian | Expected Stress Error | Recommended Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Excellent | $1.0$–$1.5$ | $0.0$–$0.25$ | $\ge 0.95$ | $<1\%$ | Hot-spot, fatigue |
| Good | $1.5$–$3.0$ | $0.25$–$0.50$ | $0.85$–$0.95$ | $1\%$–$3\%$ | Hull-girder bending |
| Acceptable | $3.0$–$5.0$ | $0.50$–$0.75$ | $0.70$–$0.85$ | $3\%$–$7\%$ | Bulk plating |
| Poor (reject) | $>5.0$ | $>0.85$ | $<0.70$ | $>10\%$ or divergence | **Invalid** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Mesh Design Workflow in Naval FEA

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Ship Geometry from CAD] --> B[Identify Critical Regions]
    B --> B1[Bulk Hull Plating]
    B --> B2[Hatch Corners]
    B --> B3[Bracket Toes]
    B --> B4[Propeller Boss]
    C[Select Element Type] --> C1[Shell QUAD4 / TRI3]
    C --> C2[Beam for Stiffeners]
    C --> C3[Solid TET10 for Boss]
    D[Generate Initial Mesh Coarse] --> E[Run Mesh Independence Study]
    E --> F{Convergence within 5 percent?}
    F -- No --> G[Refine Mesh h refinement]
    G --> E
    F -- Yes --> H[Apply Symmetry and Boundary Conditions]
    H --> I[Solver Run]
    I --> J{Quality Metrics Pass?}
    J -- No --> K[Remesh Local Region]
    K --> D
    J -- Yes --> L[Extract von Mises Stress]
    L --> M[Apply DNV or IACS Criteria]
    M --> N[Report: Mesh Validated]
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture — Element Quality Checker Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input[Mesh Node Coordinates] --> A[Compute Aspect Ratio AR]
    Input --> B[Compute Skewness Sk]
    Input --> C[Compute Jacobian JR]
    Input --> D[Compute Warping Factor W]
    A --> Q{Quality Pass?}
    B --> Q
    C --> Q
    D --> Q
    Q -- Yes --> OK[Element Flagged GREEN]
    Q -- No --> Flag[Element Flagged RED and Reported]
    OK --> Solver[FE Solver Assembly]
    Flag --> Remesh[Trigger Local Remeshing]
    Remesh --> Input
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Refinement Strategy Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Mesh Refinement Decision] --> R1[h Refinement: Reduce Element Size]
    Root --> R2[p Refinement: Increase Polynomial Order]
    Root --> R3[r Refinement: Move Nodes]
    R1 --> R1a[Global Refinement]
    R1 --> R1b[Local Refinement at Hot Spots]
    R2 --> R2a[Use in p version FEA]
    R3 --> R3a[Mesh Smoothing Algorithms]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Hull-Girder Cross Section Mesh Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Deck_Top[Top Deck Plating]
        D1[Quad Plate Element 1]
        D2[Quad Plate Element 2]
        D3[Quad Plate Element 3]
    end
    subgraph Side_Shell[Side Shell Plates]
        S1[Quad Element]
        S2[Quad Element]
        S3[Quad Element]
    end
    subgraph Bottom_Plate[Bottom Shell]
        B1[Quad Element]
        B2[Quad Element]
    end
    Deck_Top --- Side_Shell
    Side_Shell --- Bottom_Plate
    S2 -. stiffener beam element .-> S2
```

> [!NOTE]
> The schematic above represents the typical **mesh topology of a hull-girder transverse section** modeled in **2D plane-stress FEA**. Stiffeners are represented as **beam elements** rigidly coupled to the shell mid-surface nodes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the term **mesh independence** as applied to a finite element analysis of a ship hull.

**Model Answer:**
> Mesh independence is the condition reached during successive mesh refinements in which the quantity of interest (QOI) — such as **maximum von Mises stress at the midship deck** or **hull-girder bending moment capacity** — changes by less than a specified tolerance (typically 5%) between two consecutive mesh densities. At this point, the FE solution is considered numerically converged and is no longer dominated by discretization error due to mesh coarseness.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for QOI criterion, 1 for tolerance value)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
List any **four element shape-quality metrics** used to judge FE mesh quality in naval architecture applications.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Aspect Ratio** (AR) — ratio of longest to shortest edge of element
2. **Skewness** — angular deviation from ideal geometry
3. **Jacobian Ratio** — minimum-to-maximum Jacobian determinant ratio
4. **Warping Factor** — out-of-plane distortion of quadrilateral plate elements

*(3 marks: 0.5 each + 1 for correct naval-application framing)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **three main types of meshes** (structured, unstructured, hybrid) used in ship hull FEA. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A double-bottom tank of a bulk carrier is meshed with three progressively refined meshes:

| Mesh | Element Size $h$ (m) | Max von Mises Stress $\sigma$ (MPa) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 (Coarse) | $0.40$ | $186.5$ |
| 2 (Medium) | $0.20$ | $201.2$ |
| 3 (Fine) | $0.10$ | $206.8$ |

Determine the **convergence rate** $p$ using the first two meshes, and comment on whether mesh independence is achieved (use 5% tolerance). **(7 marks)**

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Define **Aspect Ratio**, **Skewness**, and **Jacobian Ratio** for a 2D quadrilateral FE element. State the acceptable ranges for each in a ship-hull plating analysis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **h-refinement**, **p-refinement**, and **r-refinement** strategies. For a midship hull-girder bending analysis, justify which strategy is most appropriate and why. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Complete Step-by-Step Solutions

#### Solution to Q3(a) — Mesh Types

**1. Structured Mesh:**
- *Definition:* Mesh with regular grid-like connectivity; every interior node has the same number of adjacent elements.
- *Sketch description:* Looks like a rectangular grid mapped onto the hull surface.
- *Advantage:* Faster solution, lower memory, simpler matrix bandwidth.
- *Disadvantage:* Cannot conform to complex ship-geometry transitions (e.g., bulbous bow, knuckles).
- *(2 marks)*

**2. Unstructured Mesh:**
- *Definition:* Mesh with arbitrary connectivity; nodes may belong to varying numbers of elements; typically built from triangles/tets.
- *Sketch description:* Irregular pattern filling complex surfaces.
- *Advantage:* Excellent adaptability to complex ship geometry.
- *Disadvantage:* Slower solver convergence, higher memory demand.
- *(2 marks)*

**3. Hybrid Mesh:**
- *Definition:* Combination of structured mesh in regular regions and unstructured mesh in irregular regions.
- *Sketch description:* Structured in side shell, unstructured near bow/stern.
- *Advantage:* Balances accuracy and computational cost.
- *Disadvantage:* More complex to generate; requires careful transition zones.
- *(2 marks)*

*General introduction and conclusion: (1 mark)*

---

#### Solution to Q3(b) — Convergence Rate Calculation

**Given:**
- $h_1 = 0.40$ m, $\sigma_1 = 186.5$ MPa
- $h_2 = 0.20$ m, $\sigma_2 = 201.2$ MPa
- $h_3 = 0.10$ m, $\sigma_3 = 206.8$ MPa

**Step 1: Compute error changes**

$$\Delta \sigma_{12} = \sigma_2 - \sigma_1 = 201.2 - 186.5 = 14.7\,\text{MPa}$$

$$\Delta \sigma_{23} = \sigma_3 - \sigma_2 = 206.8 - 201.2 = 5.6\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 2: Apply convergence-rate formula**

$$p = \frac{\log(\Delta \sigma_{23}/\Delta \sigma_{12})}{\log(h_2/h_1)}$$

$$p = \frac{\log(5.6/14.7)}{\log(0.20/0.40)}$$

$$p = \frac{\log(0.3810)}{\log(0.5)} = \frac{-0.4192}{-0.3010}$$

$$\boxed{p \approx 1.392}$$

*(3 marks for correct setup and calculation)*

**Step 3: Check mesh independence (5% tolerance)**

$$\%\text{Change}_{12} = \frac{|186.5 - 201.2|}{201.2}\times 100\% = 7.31\%$$

$$\%\text{Change}_{23} = \frac{|201.2 - 206.8|}{206.8}\times 100\% = 2.71\%$$

Since the change from Mesh 1→2 (7.31%) **exceeds 5%** but Mesh 2→3 (2.71%) is **within 5%**, **mesh independence is NOT yet achieved at Mesh 2 but is achieved at Mesh 3**.

*(2 marks for correct tolerance comparison)*

**Step 4: Engineering comment**

The theoretical rate for bilinear quad elements in plane stress is $p = 2$. The calculated rate of 1.392 suggests presence of **stress singularities** (e.g., re-entrant corners at bracket toes) where convergence is theoretically non-uniform. **Recommendation:** Apply local mesh refinement at the hot-spot or use **quarter-point singular elements** to capture the singularity.

*(2 marks for engineering insight)*

---

#### Solution to Q4(a) — Quality Metric Definitions

**Aspect Ratio (AR):**
$$AR = \frac{L_{max}}{L_{min}}$$
where $L_{max}$ and $L_{min}$ are the longest and shortest edges of the element.
*Acceptable range for ship plating: $AR \le 5$.*
*(2 marks)*

**Skewness:**
$$Sk = 1 - \frac{\theta_{min}}{90°}$$
where $\theta_{min}$ is the smallest interior angle.
*Acceptable range for ship plating: $Sk \le 0.85$.*
*(2 marks)*

**Jacobian Ratio:**
$$J_r = \frac{\min(\det \mathbf{J})}{\max(\det \mathbf{J})}$$
*Acceptable range for ship plating: $J_r \ge 0.70$. If $J_r < 0$, the element is inverted and invalid.*
*(2 marks)*

*Introduction and correct formatting: (1 mark)*

---

#### Solution to Q4(b) — Refinement Strategies

**1. h-Refinement:**
- *Definition:* Reduces the element size $h$ by increasing the number of elements.
- *Mechanism:* Subdivides existing elements into smaller ones.
- *Advantage:* Universally supported; straightforward to implement.
- *Disadvantage:* Increases DOF count → higher compute cost.
- *(2 marks)*

**2. p-Refinement:**
- *Definition:* Increases the polynomial order $p$ of element shape functions.
- *Mechanism:* Uses higher-order Legendre/Hierarchical polynomials.
- *Advantage:* Achieves exponential convergence for smooth problems.
- *Disadvantage:* Requires p-version FEA solver; not supported by all packages.
- *(2 marks)*

**3. r-Refinement:**
- *Definition:* Repositions existing nodes to improve shape quality without changing element count.
- *Mechanism:* Moves nodes toward regions of high solution gradient.
- *Advantage:* No increase in DOF.
- *Disadvantage:* Risk of mesh tangling; limited improvement.
- *(2 marks)*

**Justification for midship hull-girder bending analysis:**

For midship hull-girder bending, the stress field is **smooth and globally distributed**. **h-refinement is most appropriate** because:
- Standard FE packages (Nastran, ANSYS) natively support it.
- Ship classification societies (DNV, LR) provide guidance on element size (e.g., mesh size $\le$ 2× stiffener spacing).
- A mesh-independence study with h-refinement is straightforward to document for regulatory submission.
- p-refinement is overkill for this problem class; r-refinement alone is insufficient for hull-girder analysis.

*(1 mark)*

---

### 5.4 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Mesh Design Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in convergence-rate calculations — $h$ must be in consistent units (m or mm).
> 2. **Using logarithmic base 10 instead of natural log** in convergence-rate formula — result still valid but students must show working clearly.
> 3. **Conflating mesh-independence with mesh-validity** — independence means QOI converged; validity means elements pass quality checks (AR, Sk, J).
> 4. **Assuming all stress singularities behave like $r^{-1/2}$** — bulk-head intersections can have order $r^{-0.5}$, but bracket toes can be order $r^{-0.7}$.
> 5. **Not justifying mesh choices** with reference to classification society rules (DNV-RP-C201, IACS S34) — always cite rule source in design justification.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Mesh Design in Naval Architecture FEA**

- ✅ **Mesh Design** = strategic discretization balancing accuracy and computational cost.
- ✅ Three primary mesh types: **structured, unstructured, hybrid** (hybrid is industry default).
- ✅ Four key quality metrics: **AR, Skewness, Jacobian Ratio, Warping Factor**.
- ✅ Standard thresholds: **AR ≤ 5**, **Sk ≤ 0.85**, **J_r ≥ 0.7**, **W ≤ 0.1**.
- ✅ Three refinement strategies: **h-refinement** (size), **p-refinement** (order), **r-refinement** (node movement).
- ✅ **Convergence rate formula:**
$$p = \frac{\log(\Delta \sigma_{23}/\Delta \sigma_{12})}{\log(h_2/h_1)}$$
- ✅ **Richardson extrapolation** for true stress estimate — essential for **mesh-independence reporting**.
- ✅ 5% tolerance on QOI between consecutive refinements is the **KTU-accepted convergence criterion**.
- ✅ Always cite **DNV-RP-C201 / IACS S34** for element size guidelines.
- ✅ Use **shell elements (QUAD4, TRI3)** for plating; **beam elements** for stiffeners; **solid TET10** only for thick brackets/propeller boss.
- ✅ Apply **symmetry boundary conditions** to halve the model where possible (saggital plane of hull-girder).
- ✅ For stress singularities, use **quarter-point (singular) elements** at re-entrant corners.
- ✅ **Mesh-independence study = mandatory** in any reportable FEA analysis for class approval.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Substructures and Sub-Modelling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST753 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS IN NAVAL ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Substructures and Sub-Modelling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — SUBSTRUCTURES AND SUB-MODELLING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Substructuring?

**Substructuring** is a finite element analysis (FEA) technique in which a large, computationally expensive global structure is partitioned into smaller, independent components called **substructures** (or *super-elements*). Each substructure is condensed down to a much smaller set of degrees of freedom (DOFs) located only at the *interface* (or *boundary*) nodes where it connects to neighbouring substructures. The reduced substructures are then re-assembled into a much smaller global system, which is solved efficiently. The internal DOFs of every substructure can later be recovered by back-substitution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Substructuring* is a divide-and-conquer strategy that exploits the sparsity and block nature of the global stiffness matrix **[K]** to drastically reduce the size of the problem that must be solved in a single shot, while preserving exact (or near-exact) static and dynamic behaviour.

### 1.2 What is Sub-Modelling?

**Sub-modelling** (also called the *cut-boundary displacement method* or *global–local analysis*) is a sequential, hierarchical FEA technique. A coarse *global model* of the entire structure is solved first. The displacement field at a chosen cut-boundary (the boundary of a smaller region of interest) is then extracted from the global solution. This boundary displacement is subsequently imposed as a prescribed boundary condition on a finer *local sub-model* (a refined mesh of the region of interest), and the sub-model is solved independently.

> [!NOTE]
> Sub-modelling is sanctioned by the KTU board as a **legitimate two-stage modelling strategy** when stress gradients in one localised region (e.g., a hatch corner of a ship, a propeller boss fillet) are too severe to be captured by a single uniform mesh.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 1 — Substructuring as a "Modular Jigsaw"**
> Imagine building a giant Lego model of a frigate (say, **10,00,000** bricks). You would never assemble all bricks at once on the floor. Instead, you build the bow, the midship, and the stern as **three independent sub-assemblies** on the table, each using only the connecting studs as "contact points". Once ready, you snap the three modules together. The total problem becomes three small problems plus one tiny connection problem. **That is substructuring.**

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 2 — Sub-modelling as a "Google Maps Zoom"**
> When you look at a map of India, every state is approximated by smooth boundaries. But when you zoom into Kerala, you suddenly see every backwater curve, every hill contour. **Sub-modelling** does exactly this in FEA: a coarse "India-scale" mesh is solved first, and then a finer "Kerala-scale" mesh is solved only over the region where you need high-fidelity stress detail, using the coarse displacement map at its boundary as a *boundary condition*. This avoids meshing the entire structure finely.

### 1.4 Why are These Techniques Critical in Naval Architecture?

Naval architecture problems are almost always **multi-scale**:

| Phenomena of Interest | Characteristic Length Scale | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Hull-girder bending | $\sim L_{BP} \approx \mathbf{100\text{ m}}$ | Whole-ship vibration |
| Plate panel buckling | $\sim b \approx \mathbf{1\text{ m}}$ | Stiffened panel |
| Stress concentration at a hatch corner | $\sim \sqrt{Rt} \approx \mathbf{10\text{ mm}}$ | Crack initiation |

A *single mesh* cannot resolve all three length scales economically. Substructuring and sub-modelling are the standard KTU-approved answers to this multi-scale dilemma.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constant Reminder:** The standard acceleration due to gravity used in naval architecture gravity-load studies is **g = 9.81 m/s²**, and Young's modulus for ship-building mild steel is approximately **E = 2.05 × 10⁵ MPa**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Substructuring Philosophy — Block Partitioning

Consider the global equilibrium equation of an FE model:

$$ [K]_{G} \, \{u\}_{G} = \{F\}_{G} $$

Substructuring partitions $\{u\}_{G}$ into two physical categories:

* **Boundary (master / interface / super-element) DOFs** — denoted $\{u_b\}$, shared with neighbouring substructures.
* **Internal (slave / kept) DOFs** — denoted $\{u_i\}$, unique to the substructure.

After partitioning, the equilibrium of a single substructure $s$ becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} K_{ii}^{(s)} & K_{ib}^{(s)} \\ K_{bi}^{(s)} & K_{bb}^{(s)} \end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix} u_i^{(s)} \\ u_b^{(s)} \end{Bmatrix}
=
\begin{Bmatrix} F_i^{(s)} \\ F_b^{(s)} \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

The strategic idea is to **eliminate** $\{u_i^{(s)}\}$ algebraically so that only $\{u_b^{(s)}\}$ remains as the unknowns of the substructure.

### 2.2 Method 1 — Static Condensation (Guyan Reduction)

Static condensation (also called **Guyan reduction**) assumes that the internal DOFs respond *instantaneously* and *quasi-statically* to the boundary DOFs (no inertial coupling). From the first row of the partitioned equation:

$$ K_{ii} \, u_i + K_{ib} \, u_b = F_i $$

Solving for $\{u_i\}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\{u_i\} = K_{ii}^{-1} \, (\{F_i\} - K_{ib} \, \{u_b\})
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting into the second row yields the **condensed stiffness matrix** and **condensed load vector** that act only on the boundary DOFs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
[K_b^{*}]\, \{u_b\} &= \{F_b^{*}\} \\
\text{where } \quad [K_b^{*}] &= [K_{bb}] - [K_{bi}]\,[K_{ii}]^{-1}\,[K_{ib}] \\
\text{and } \quad \{F_b^{*}\} &= \{F_b\} - [K_{bi}]\,[K_{ii}]^{-1}\,\{F_i\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> $[K_b^{*}]$ is a much smaller, dense matrix that lives only on the master DOFs. After assembly across all substructures, the global system becomes tiny and cheap to solve.

### 2.3 Method 2 — Component Mode Synthesis (Craig–Bampton Method)

For **dynamic** problems (free vibration, transient response, wave-impact), pure static condensation is inaccurate because inertial effects at the internal DOFs are ignored. The **Craig–Bampton** method augments the boundary DOFs with a set of *fixed-interface normal modes* (eigenvectors of the substructure when its boundary is clamped).

The Craig–Bampton transformation reads:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{Bmatrix} u_i \\ u_b \end{Bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} \Phi_N & -K_{ii}^{-1} K_{ib} \\ 0 & I \end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix} \eta \\ u_b \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:

* $\Phi_N$ = matrix of **fixed-interface normal modes** (eigenvectors of $K_{ii}\,\phi = \omega^2 M_{ii}\,\phi$ with $u_b = 0$),
* $\eta$ = modal coordinates of the normal modes,
* the off-diagonal block $-K_{ii}^{-1}K_{ib}$ = the *static constraint modes* representing the displacement of internal nodes due to a unit displacement of each boundary DOF.

### 2.4 Sub-Modelling — Procedure in 5 Steps

The KTU board recognises the following canonical 5-step procedure for sub-modelling:

1. **Build and solve** a coarse global model of the full structure.
2. **Identify** the region of interest (ROI) and define a *cut boundary* enclosing it.
3. **Interpolate** the global nodal displacements onto the cut-boundary nodes of the sub-model using element shape functions.
4. **Apply** these interpolated displacements as **Dirichlet boundary conditions** on the sub-model.
5. **Solve** the sub-model independently and post-process the refined stress field.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for Module 4. Memorise every entry. Pipes $\vert$ have been replaced by `\mid` to keep the markdown table parser happy.

| $\#$ | Concept | Master Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Static condensation (Guyan) | $[K_b^{*}] = [K_{bb}] - [K_{bi}]\,[K_{ii}]^{-1}\,[K_{ib}]$ | $[K_b^{*}]$ is *dense* and *symmetric PD* |
| 2 | Condensed load vector | $\{F_b^{*}\} = \{F_b\} - [K_{bi}]\,[K_{ii}]^{-1}\,\{F_i\}$ | Vector |
| 3 | Internal DOF recovery | $\{u_i\} = [K_{ii}]^{-1}\,(\{F_i\} - [K_{ib}]\{u_b\})$ | Back-substitution |
| 4 | Craig–Bampton transformation | $\{u\} = [T]\,\{q\}$ with $[T] = \begin{bmatrix} \Phi_N & \Psi \\ 0 & I \end{bmatrix}$ | $\Psi = -K_{ii}^{-1}K_{ib}$ |
| 5 | Reduced mass matrix | $[M^{*}] = [T]^{T}\,[M]\,[T]$ | Same size as reduced $[K^{*}]$ |
| 6 | Eigenproblem on substructure | $([K_{ii}] - \omega^2 [M_{ii}])\,\Phi_N = 0$ | With $u_b = 0$ |
| 7 | Sub-modelling BC | $\{u\}_{cut}^{local} = [N(\xi,\eta)]\,\{u\}_{global}^{cut}$ | Shape-function interpolation |
| 8 | Energy error bound | $\dfrac{\mid \mid u - u_h \mid \mid_E}{\mid \mid u \mid \mid_E} \le C\,h$ | Linear FE convergence |
| 9 | Standard acceleration | $g = 9.81$ | m/s² |
| 10 | Mild steel modulus | $E = 2.05 \times 10^{5}$ | MPa |

### 2.6 Real-World Naval Architecture Utilities

* **Whole-ship vibration analysis** of a frigate — bow, midship, stern analysed as separate Craig–Bampton super-elements, re-assembled at hull-transverse bulkheads.
* **Crashworthiness** simulation of a double-hull tanker — substructure of the struck side refined to capture local buckling and denting.
* **Propeller-shaft alignment** — sub-modelling used to study the stress concentration at the propeller boss / shaft taper fillet.
* **Slamming load studies** — sub-modelling of the bow flare region with fine shell elements while the rest of the hull uses coarse beam elements.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Static Condensation from First Principles

We start from the partitioned equilibrium of a single substructure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} K_{ii} & K_{ib} \\ K_{bi} & K_{bb} \end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix} u_i \\ u_b \end{Bmatrix}
=
\begin{Bmatrix} F_i \\ F_b \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Write the two scalar block equations explicitly:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row 1:} \quad & K_{ii}\,u_i + K_{ib}\,u_b = F_i \\
\text{Row 2:} \quad & K_{bi}\,u_i + K_{bb}\,u_b = F_b
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Assume $K_{ii}$ is non-singular** (it is, since it is the stiffness of a constrained internal node group). Isolate $u_i$ from Row 1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{ii}\,u_i &= F_i - K_{ib}\,u_b \\
u_i &= K_{ii}^{-1}(F_i - K_{ib}\,u_b)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute this expression for $u_i$ into Row 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{bi}\,[K_{ii}^{-1}(F_i - K_{ib}\,u_b)] + K_{bb}\,u_b = F_b
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Expand the left-hand side and regroup terms in $u_b$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{bi}\,K_{ii}^{-1}\,F_i \;-\; K_{bi}\,K_{ii}^{-1}\,K_{ib}\,u_b \;+\; K_{bb}\,u_b = F_b
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Collect the $u_b$ terms on the left:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(K_{bb} - K_{bi}\,K_{ii}^{-1}\,K_{ib})\,u_b \;=\; F_b - K_{bi}\,K_{ii}^{-1}\,F_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Identify the condensed quantities** and write the reduced equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K^{*}\,u_b &= F^{*} \\
\text{where } \quad K^{*} &= K_{bb} - K_{bi}\,K_{ii}^{-1}\,K_{ib} \\
\text{and } \quad F^{*} &= F_b - K_{bi}\,K_{ii}^{-1}\,F_i
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Schur complement** of $K_{ii}$ in the full substructure matrix, and it is the cornerstone of every KTU substructuring question.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Craig–Bampton Transformation

We want to choose a transformation matrix $T$ such that the reduced substructure equations are *block-diagonal* in mass and stiffness, enabling efficient modal analysis.

**Step 1 — Consider a fixed-interface eigenproblem** of the substructure with all boundary DOFs clamped ($u_b = 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
(K_{ii} - \omega_r^2 M_{ii})\,\phi_r = 0, \qquad r = 1, 2, \dots, n_{kept}
\end{aligned}
$$

Collect the lowest $n_{kept}$ eigenvectors as columns of $\Phi_N \in \mathbb{R}^{n_i \times n_{kept}}$.

**Step 2 — Define the *static constraint modes***. For each boundary DOF $j$, apply a unit displacement at boundary DOF $j$ while keeping all other boundary DOFs fixed and the internal DOFs in static equilibrium:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{ii}\,\psi_j + K_{ib}\,e_j &= 0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \psi_j &= -K_{ii}^{-1}\,K_{ib}\,e_j
\end{aligned}
$$

Stacking all $\psi_j$ as columns gives the constraint-mode matrix $\Psi = -K_{ii}^{-1}K_{ib}$.

**Step 3 — Assemble the Craig–Bampton transformation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\{u\} = \begin{Bmatrix} u_i \\ u_b \end{Bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} \Phi_N & \Psi \\ 0 & I \end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix} \eta \\ u_b \end{Bmatrix} \;\equiv\; [T]\,\{q\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the transformation to mass and stiffness:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
[K^{*}] &= [T]^{T}[K][T] \\
[M^{*}] &= [T]^{T}[M][T]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Show the resulting block structure** (this is the key KTU result):

$$
\begin{aligned}
[K^{*}] &= \begin{bmatrix} \Phi_N^{T}K_{ii}\Phi_N & 0 \\ 0 & K_{bb}^{*} \end{bmatrix} \\
[M^{*}] &= \begin{bmatrix} I + \Phi_N^{T}M_{ii}\Phi_N & \Phi_N^{T}M_{ib} \\ M_{bi}\Phi_N & M_{bb}^{*} \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

The stiffness matrix becomes **block-diagonal**, which makes modal extraction trivial and is the reason Craig–Bampton is the *gold standard* for ship-vibration substructuring.

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation — Sub-Modelling Boundary Interpolation

Consider a coarse 4-node quad element in the global model whose cut-boundary edge runs between global nodes $G_1, G_2$. In the sub-model, the same physical edge is shared by finer 4-node quad elements, with local cut-boundary nodes $L_1, L_2, L_3$.

**Step 1 — Parametric coordinate of a local node** on the cut edge:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\xi = \dfrac{x_{L_k} - x_{G_1}}{x_{G_2} - x_{G_1}} \in [-1, +1]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the 1-D shape functions of the global quad at $\xi$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_1(\xi) &= \tfrac{1}{2}(1 - \xi), \quad N_2(\xi) = \tfrac{1}{2}(1 + \xi)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpolated boundary displacement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_x^{L_k} &= N_1(\xi)\,u_x^{G_1} + N_2(\xi)\,u_x^{G_2} \\
u_y^{L_k} &= N_1(\xi)\,u_y^{G_1} + N_2(\xi)\,u_y^{G_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Stack for all cut-boundary local nodes** to form a displacement vector $\{u\}_{cut}^{local}$, then apply it as a Dirichlet BC on the sub-model and solve.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Mini-Example for Exam Practice)

> A 3-DOF spring-mass chain has stiffness matrix (in N/m): $K = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & -2 & 0 \\ -2 & 5 & -3 \\ 0 & -3 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$. Treat node 1 as the *internal* DOF and nodes 2, 3 as *boundary* DOFs. A load $F = \begin{Bmatrix} 10 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}$ N acts only on the internal node.

**Step 1 — Identify blocks:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{ii} = [3], \quad K_{ib} = K_{bi}^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad K_{bb} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & -3 \\ -3 & 4 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $K_{ii}^{-1} = \tfrac{1}{3}$.**

**Step 3 — Condense the stiffness:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K^{*} &= K_{bb} - K_{bi}K_{ii}^{-1}K_{ib} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 5 & -3 \\ -3 & 4 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \tfrac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 5 & -3 \\ -3 & 4 \end{bmatrix} - \tfrac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 5 - 4/3 & -3 \\ -3 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 11/3 & -3 \\ -3 & 4 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Condense the load:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F^{*} &= F_b - K_{bi}K_{ii}^{-1}F_i = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \tfrac{1}{3}(10) = \begin{bmatrix} 20/3 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve reduced system $K^{*}u_b = F^{*}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} 11/3 & -3 \\ -3 & 4 \end{bmatrix}\begin{Bmatrix} u_2 \\ u_3 \end{Bmatrix} &= \begin{Bmatrix} 20/3 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix} \\
\Rightarrow \quad u_2 &= \dfrac{80}{17} \text{ m}, \qquad u_3 = \dfrac{60}{17} \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Recover the internal DOF:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_1 &= K_{ii}^{-1}(F_i - K_{ib}u_b) = \tfrac{1}{3}\bigl(10 - (-2)u_2 - (0)u_3\bigr) \\
&= \tfrac{1}{3}\bigl(10 + 2\cdot\tfrac{80}{17}\bigr) = \tfrac{330}{51} = \tfrac{110}{17} \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to *"verify by solving the full system"*, you will recover $u = \tfrac{1}{17}\begin{Bmatrix} 110 \\ 80 \\ 60 \end{Bmatrix}$ m — the same answer with only two unknowns solved instead of three. That is the *computational saving* the examiner is testing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Workflow — Static Condensation (Guyan) Substructuring

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Full Substructure K and F partitioned] --> B[Extract blocks Kii Kib Kbi Kbb]
    B --> C[Invert Kii]
    C --> D[Compute K star = Kbb minus Kbi Kii inv Kib]
    D --> E[Compute F star = Fb minus Kbi Kii inv Fi]
    E --> F[Solve reduced system K star ub = F star]
    F --> G[Recover ui using back substitution]
    G --> H[Assemble across all substructures at master DOFs]
    H --> I[Final global displacements]
```

### 4.2 Workflow — Craig-Bampton Component Mode Synthesis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    J[Substructure geometry and mesh] --> K[Solve fixed interface eigenproblem]
    K --> L[Collect lowest normal modes in PhiN]
    J --> M[Compute static constraint modes Psi]
    M --> N[Form Craig Bampton T matrix]
    L --> N
    N --> O[Compute reduced K star and M star]
    O --> P[Solve reduced modal problem]
    P --> Q[Recover physical displacements]
```

### 4.3 Workflow — Sub-Modelling Global-to-Local Two-Stage Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Build coarse global model] --> S[Apply loads and solve global model]
    S --> T[Identify region of interest ROI]
    T --> U[Define cut boundary around ROI]
    U --> V[Interpolate global displacements to local cut boundary nodes]
    V --> W[Build refined local sub model]
    W --> X[Impose interpolated displacements as Dirichlet BCs]
    X --> Y[Solve sub model independently]
    Y --> Z[Extract refined stress field at ROI]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Substructure Database

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph GlobalAssembly
        nodeG[Global Reduced System]
    end
    subgraph SubOne
        nodeA1[Substructure 1 Mesh]
        nodeA2[Condensation Engine for S1]
        nodeA3[Master DOFs of S1]
    end
    subgraph SubTwo
        nodeB1[Substructure 2 Mesh]
        nodeB2[Condensation Engine for S2]
        nodeB3[Master DOFs of S2]
    end
    subgraph SubThree
        nodeC1[Substructure 3 Mesh]
        nodeC2[Condensation Engine for S3]
        nodeC3[Master DOFs of S3]
    end
    nodeA1 --> nodeA2 --> nodeA3
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2 --> nodeB3
    nodeC1 --> nodeC2 --> nodeC3
    nodeA3 --> nodeG
    nodeB3 --> nodeG
    nodeC3 --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeX[Final Global Solution Vector]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each *Condensation Engine* block is itself a black-box that performs the Guyan or Craig–Bampton transformation internally; the *Global Assembly* only ever sees the condensed master-DOF matrices.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1.** Define *substructuring* in finite element analysis. Mention **two** situations in naval architecture where substructuring is preferred over a single global model.

**Model Answer:**
Substructuring is a divide-and-conquer FEA technique in which a large structure is divided into smaller, manageable substructures. Each substructure is condensed to its boundary (master) degrees of freedom, and the reduced substructures are assembled into a much smaller global system. In naval architecture it is preferred for: *(i)* whole-ship vibration analysis where the hull is decomposed at transverse bulkheads into bow, midship, and stern super-elements, and *(ii)* crashworthiness / slamming studies where locally detailed meshes are coupled to a coarse global hull-girder model.

**Q2.** What is *sub-modelling*? State the role of the *cut boundary* in this technique.

**Model Answer:**
Sub-modelling is a hierarchical two-stage FEA technique in which a coarse global model is first solved to obtain displacements at a chosen cut boundary, and these displacements are then imposed as prescribed boundary conditions on a finer local sub-model containing the region of interest. The cut boundary is the artificial interface that separates the refined sub-model from the rest of the structure; it transmits the global deformation field into the local model via interpolated nodal displacements, ensuring kinematic continuity between the two analyses.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Substructuring via Static Condensation**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* For a single substructure, the partitioned stiffness equation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} K_{ii} & K_{ib} \\ K_{bi} & K_{bb} \end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix} u_i \\ u_b \end{Bmatrix}
=
\begin{Bmatrix} F_i \\ F_b \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Starting from the above matrix equation, **derive** the condensed equilibrium $K^{*} u_b = F^{*}$ that governs only the boundary DOFs. Clearly state the expressions for $K^{*}$ and $F^{*}$.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A two-DOF internal group is connected to a three-DOF boundary group. The block matrices are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{ii} &= \begin{bmatrix} 6 & -2 \\ -2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad
K_{ib} = K_{bi}^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \\
K_{bb} &= \begin{bmatrix} 5 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 5 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 4 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad
F_i = \begin{Bmatrix} 10 \\ 20 \end{Bmatrix}, \qquad
F_b = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $K^{*}$, $F^{*}$, and solve for $u_b$. Hence recover $u_i$ by back-substitution.

**Model Solution for (a):**

Step 1 — Write Row 1: $K_{ii} u_i + K_{ib} u_b = F_i$.
Step 2 — Solve for $u_i$: $u_i = K_{ii}^{-1}(F_i - K_{ib} u_b)$.
Step 3 — Substitute into Row 2: $K_{bi} u_i + K_{bb} u_b = F_b$.
Step 4 — Expand and rearrange to obtain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\underbrace{(K_{bb} - K_{bi} K_{ii}^{-1} K_{ib})}_{K^{*}} u_b \;=\; \underbrace{F_b - K_{bi} K_{ii}^{-1} F_i}_{F^{*}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

Step 1 — Invert $K_{ii}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{ii}^{-1} = \tfrac{1}{20}\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 6 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Compute the product $K_{bi} K_{ii}^{-1} K_{ib}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{bi} K_{ii}^{-1} &= \tfrac{1}{20}\begin{bmatrix} -4 & -2 \\ 0 & -4 \end{bmatrix} \\
K_{bi} K_{ii}^{-1} K_{ib} &= \tfrac{1}{20}\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 4 \\ 0 & 8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0.2 \\ 0 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Compute the condensed stiffness:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K^{*} = K_{bb} - K_{bi} K_{ii}^{-1} K_{ib} = \begin{bmatrix} 4.8 & -1.2 & 0 \\ -1.2 & 5.0 & -1.0 \\ 0 & -1.0 & 3.6 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Compute the condensed load:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{bi} K_{ii}^{-1} F_i &= \tfrac{1}{20}\begin{bmatrix} -4 & -2 \\ 0 & -4 \end{bmatrix}\begin{Bmatrix} 10 \\ 20 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} -4 \\ -4 \end{Bmatrix} \\
F^{*} &= F_b - K_{bi} K_{ii}^{-1} F_i = \begin{Bmatrix} 4 \\ 4 \\ 4 \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 5 — Solve $K^{*} u_b = F^{*}$ by Gaussian elimination. Solving yields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_b = \begin{Bmatrix} u_3 \\ u_4 \\ u_5 \end{Bmatrix} \approx \begin{Bmatrix} 1.4815 \\ 1.7284 \\ 1.5910 \end{Bmatrix} \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 6 — Back-substitute to recover $u_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_i &= K_{ii}^{-1}(F_i - K_{ib} u_b) = \tfrac{1}{20}\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 6 \end{bmatrix}\left(\begin{Bmatrix} 10 \\ 20 \end{Bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}u_b\right) \\
\Rightarrow u_i &\approx \begin{Bmatrix} 2.2963 \\ 3.0682 \end{Bmatrix} \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Sub-Modelling**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* With the aid of a labelled flow chart, **describe** the five-step procedure for sub-modelling. Explain why the cut-boundary must be located *sufficiently far* from the region of highest stress gradient.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A coarse global 4-node quad element with global nodal coordinates $(0,0)$, $(2,0)$, $(2,2)$, $(0,2)$ m has solved nodal displacements $u_x = (0, 0.002, 0.004, 0.002)$ m on its bottom edge between $(0,0)$ and $(2,0)$. A sub-model introduces an extra local node at the midpoint $(\xi = 0)$. Using 1-D shape-function interpolation, compute the interpolated displacement $u_x$ at the local midpoint node.

**Model Solution for (a):**

The five steps are:
1. Build and solve the coarse global model.
2. Identify the region of interest and define a cut boundary enclosing it.
3. Interpolate the global displacements onto the cut-boundary nodes of the sub-model using shape functions.
4. Apply these interpolated displacements as Dirichlet boundary conditions on the sub-model.
5. Solve the sub-model independently and post-process the refined stress field.

The cut boundary must be located *far enough* from regions of severe stress gradients because the coarse global solution provides a *smoothed* displacement field. Saint-Venant's principle states that the perturbation caused by imposing displacements on an artificial boundary decays exponentially with distance; therefore, the further the cut boundary is from the singularity, the smaller the error introduced by the interpolation. KTU typically expects the cut boundary to lie at least one characteristic element length away from any stress raiser.

**Model Solution for (b):**

At $\xi = 0$, the 1-D linear shape functions are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_1(0) = \tfrac{1}{2}(1 - 0) = 0.5, \qquad N_2(0) = \tfrac{1}{2}(1 + 0) = 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

Interpolated displacement at the local midpoint:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_x^{L} = N_1 u_x^{G_1} + N_2 u_x^{G_2} = 0.5(0) + 0.5(0.002) = 0.001 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign error in $K_{ii}^{-1}$:** Students often forget that the inverse of a non-diagonal $K_{ii}$ must use the cofactor/determinant formula, *not* a simple reciprocal of the diagonal entry. Partial marks are awarded only if the determinant and cofactor are shown.
> 2. **Forgetting the symmetry of $K^{*}$:** Examiners deduct marks if the student does not explicitly verify that $K^{*} = (K^{*})^{T}$ after condensation.
> 3. **Mis-defining cut-boundary location:** A common 2-mark loss in sub-modelling answers is failing to justify *why* the cut boundary must be located in a low-stress-gradient region (Saint-Venant's principle).
> 4. **Confusing substructuring with sub-modelling:** Substructuring is *parallel* (all substructures solve simultaneously and assemble); sub-modelling is *sequential* (global solved first, then local). Mixing them up is a guaranteed 2–3 mark deduction.
> 5. **Units of displacement recovery:** Always state the unit (m, mm). Examiners are strict about dimensional consistency.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4**

- [x] **Substructuring** = divide-and-conquer; partition into *internal* and *boundary* DOFs and eliminate the internal ones.
- [x] **Static (Guyan) condensation** is valid for static and low-frequency dynamic problems where inertial coupling across the cut is negligible.
- [x] The condensed stiffness is a **Schur complement**: $K^{*} = K_{bb} - K_{bi} K_{ii}^{-1} K_{ib}$.
- [x] The condensed load is $F^{*} = F_b - K_{bi} K_{ii}^{-1} F_i$.
- [x] Internal DOFs are recovered by **back-substitution**: $u_i = K_{ii}^{-1}(F_i - K_{ib} u_b)$.
- [x] **Craig–Bampton** method = static condensation + fixed-interface normal modes; used for dynamic problems.
- [x] Craig–Bampton transformation $T = \begin{bmatrix} \Phi_N & -K_{ii}^{-1} K_{ib} \\ 0 & I \end{bmatrix}$ produces a block-diagonal reduced stiffness.
- [x] **Sub-modelling** = sequential global-then-local analysis; uses *shape-function interpolation* of global displacements onto a finer local cut boundary.
- [x] Cut boundary must lie in a **low-stress-gradient** region (Saint-Venant's principle) to minimise interpolation error.
- [x] Sub-modelling is **not** an iterative method — only one global solve and one local solve are required.
- [x] Computational saving of substructuring scales with the **ratio** of internal to boundary DOFs in each substructure.
- [x] Always report $K^{*}$ as **symmetric** and **positive-definite** when $K_{ii}$ is PD (it is, for well-posed problems).
- [x] Naval architecture applications: hull-girder vibration, slamming, propeller boss, hatch corners, double-hull crashworthiness.
- [x] Standard constants to memorise: $g = 9.81$ m/s², $E_{steel} = 2.05 \times 10^{5}$ MPa.
- [x] Examiner's hot-button phrases: *"derive the Schur complement"*, *"justify the cut-boundary location"*, *"recover the internal DOFs"*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FE Model Loads and Boundary Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST753 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS IN NAVAL ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** FE Model Loads and Boundary Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🏗️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Finite Element Analysis (FEA) applied to **Naval Architecture**, **FE Model Loads** refer to the complete set of forces, pressures, accelerations, and moments that a discretized ship structure is subjected to during a numerical simulation. These loads mathematically represent the **operational environment** acting on the hull girder, plating, stiffeners, and superstructure.

**Boundary Conditions (BCs)** are the mathematical constraints applied to the Finite Element model that define the **support, restraint, and symmetry** of the structure. They prescribe known displacements, rotations, or symmetric relationships at specific Degrees of Freedom (DOFs) and are essential to obtain a **non-singular, physically meaningful stiffness matrix solution** (i.e., avoiding rigid body motion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> A properly defined FE model of a ship requires accurate representation of **(i) the structural geometry, (ii) the applied loading, and (iii) the boundary/symmetry conditions**. The combined load + BC system simulates the vessel's behavior in a real seagoing or harbor environment such as **still water (SW), harbor (H), or extreme wave (E)** scenarios.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ship as a Floating Bridge"

Imagine a ship as a **very long floating bridge**:
- The **loads** are everything pushing, pulling, or pressing on the bridge — the weight of vehicles, wind on the deck, water pressure from a river current.
- The **boundary conditions** are the bridge's end supports (abutments) and the road surface (ground) — they hold the bridge in place and define where it can/cannot move.

In FE terms:
- **Loads** = The "questions" we ask the structure: *What is happening to the ship?*
- **Boundary conditions** = The "rules of the game": *How is the ship held in place?*

If you remove the bridge supports, even a small load will cause the entire structure to "float away" mathematically — this is called **rigid body motion** and the FE solver will crash or give garbage results. That is why BCs are not optional — they are **non-negotiable**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics (KTU Board Values)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values for Ship FE Analysis:**
> - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$
> - **Density of sea water:** $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \; \text{t/m}^3$ (1025 kg/m³)
> - **Density of fresh water:** $\rho_{fw} = 1.000 \; \text{t/m}^3$
> - **Density of structural steel:** $\rho_{st} = 7.85 \; \text{t/m}^3$
> - **Young's Modulus (shipbuilding steel):** $E = 2.06 \times 10^{11} \; \text{N/m}^2$ (210 GPa)
> - **Poisson's ratio:** $\nu = 0.30$
> - **Yield stress (mild ship steel grade A):** $\sigma_y = 235 \; \text{MPa}$

## 1.4 Classification Overview

| Category | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Hull Girder Loads** | Global loads (bending, shear, torsion) on entire ship | Strength assessment |
| **Local Loads** | Pressures on plates, stiffener loads | Local scantling checks |
| **Static Loads** | Time-invariant (weight, hydrostatic) | Still water analysis |
| **Dynamic Loads** | Time-variant (waves, slamming) | Dynamic strength analysis |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchy of Loads in Ship FE Analysis

Ship FE loads follow a **multi-level hierarchy** as recommended by **IACS (International Association of Classification Societies) Common Structural Rules (CSR)**:

### Level 1 — Hull Girder Loads (Global)
1. **Still Water Bending Moment (SWBM):** $M_{sw}$
2. **Wave Bending Moment:** $M_{w}$
3. **Total Vertical Bending Moment:** $M_{T} = M_{sw} + M_{w}$
4. **Shear Force:** $V_{T}$
5. **Hull Girder Torsional Moment** (especially for container ships)

### Level 2 — Local Loads
1. **External hydrostatic pressure** on shell plating
2. **External hydrodynamic pressure** (wave + slamming)
3. **Internal pressure** (tanks, holds, cargo)
4. **Inertial loads** (self-weight with accelerations)
5. **Local patch loads** (e.g., tugboat push, berthing)

### Level 3 — Operational Loads
1. **Cargo loads** (containers, bulk, tanks)
2. **Ballast loads**
3. **Machinery and equipment reactions**
4. **Sea pressure** on rudder, propeller, shaft brackets

## 2.2 Types of Boundary Conditions in Ship FEA

Boundary conditions in ship FE analysis differ from typical civil/mechanical FEA because the ship is a **free-free body floating in fluid** rather than a structure on a rigid foundation.

| BC Type | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Clamped** | $u = v = w = 0$ | Fixed support (rarely used in ships) |
| **Pinned / Hinged** | $w = 0$ | Roller / pin support |
| **Symmetry Plane** | $u = 0$ (longitudinal sym.) | Model only half of symmetric ship |
| **Anti-symmetry Plane** | $v = w = 0$ | Sagging vs. hogging alternatives |
| **Rigid Beam / MPC** | $u_A = u_B$ (multi-point constraint) | Tie plate to stiffener web |
| **Elastic Spring** | $F = k \cdot \delta$ | Fender, mooring, soil |
| **Master-Slave / Cyclic Symmetry** | Coupled DOF | Stern/Aft symmetry exploitation |
| **Inertial Release** | Net force = 0, net moment = 0 | Free-free floating body (no BC needed) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The two most common BC strategies in ship FEA are:
> 1. **Symmetry exploitation** (model ½ or ¼ of the ship) — preferred for **bending analysis**.
> 2. **Inertial release** — preferred for **complete ship model** with rigid body modes.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic for Defining FE Loads

**Step 1 — Identify the design scenario:**
- Still Water (SW), Harbor (H), Sailing in regular/irregular waves.

**Step 2 — Compute external pressure distribution:**
- Use **hydrostatic** + **hydrodynamic** (Froude–Krylov + diffraction) pressures.

**Step 3 — Apply internal loads:**
- Tank filling levels, bulk cargo distribution, container stack weights.

**Step 4 — Account for inertia:**
- Self-weight = $\rho \cdot g \cdot V$ applied as body force or as a global acceleration.

**Step 5 — Define boundary/symmetry conditions:**
- Choose between **symmetry**, **elastic support**, or **inertial release**.

**Step 6 — Validate the model:**
- Check **reaction forces** balance the **applied loads** (global equilibrium).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p_{hydro} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | $M_{sw} = \int Q(x) \cdot x \, dx$ | Still water bending moment from weight curve | kN·m |
| 3 | $M_{w} = f(L, B, T, C_b, \lambda)$ | Wave bending moment (IACS / CSR formula) | kN·m |
| 4 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Bending stress in hull girder (Navier) | Pa |
| 5 | $F_{gravity} = m \cdot g$ | Self-weight body force | N |
| 6 | $F_{buoyancy} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | Buoyancy force (Archimedes) | N |
| 7 | $p_{total} = p_{static} + p_{dyn}$ | Total pressure (static + dynamic) | Pa |
| 8 | $F_{slam} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_s A v^2$ | Slamming force (impact) | N |
| 9 | $k_{spring} = \dfrac{E A}{L}$ | Axial stiffness of a tie/strut | N/m |
| 10 | $\sum F_x = 0, \sum F_y = 0, \sum F_z = 0$ | Global equilibrium (3 translation) | — |
| 11 | $\sum M_x = 0, \sum M_y = 0, \sum M_z = 0$ | Global equilibrium (3 rotation) | — |
| 12 | $I = \int_A y^2 \, dA$ | Second moment of area for hull section | m⁴ |
| 13 | $A_{eff} = A_{shell} + \alpha A_{stiff}$ | Effective shear area (with stiffeners) | m² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Every classification society (DNV, LR, ABS, BV, KR) prescribes a specific set of load cases and boundary conditions that must be satisfied. The KTU examiner expects familiarity with **IACS CSR** and the **3-equation of equilibrium** as a closure check.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hydrostatic Pressure Distribution on a Ship Hull

The hydrostatic pressure at any point on the hull plating depends on the **vertical distance below the waterline**. We derive the pressure at the keel and at the waterline.

**Step 1 — Define depth coordinate:**
Let $h$ = vertical depth of the point below the waterline (m). The water surface is at $h = 0$.

**Step 2 — Pressure at depth $h$ from Pascal's principle:**

$$
p(h) = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h
$$

**Step 3 — At the waterline (deck side):**
$h = 0 \Rightarrow p(0) = 0$.

**Step 4 — At the keel (deepest point, draft $T$):**

$$
p_{keel} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot T
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (KTU-style):**
Given: Draft $T = 12 \; \text{m}$, $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$.

$$
p_{keel} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 12
$$

$$
p_{keel} = 120{,}651 \; \text{Pa} \approx 120.7 \; \text{kPa}
$$

> That is approximately **1.2 atmospheres** of pressure at the keel of a Panamax bulker!

## 3.2 Derivation: Global Force Equilibrium Check (Static Verification)

After solving the FE problem, the **reaction forces** at supports and **applied loads** must balance. This is the first thing a KTU examiner checks.

**Step 1 — Sum of forces in $x$-direction:**

$$
\sum F_x = 0 \implies F_{x,applied} + F_{x,reaction} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Sum of forces in $y$-direction:**

$$
\sum F_y = 0 \implies F_{y,applied} + F_{y,reaction} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Sum of forces in $z$-direction (vertical, the most important for ships):**

$$
\sum F_z = 0 \implies W_{ship} - F_{buoyancy} + F_{z,reaction} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Sum of moments about $x$, $y$, $z$ axes:**

$$
\sum M_x = 0, \quad \sum M_y = 0, \quad \sum M_z = 0
$$

**Step 5 — For a free-floating ship (no BCs applied):**
With **inertial release**, the global reaction forces and moments are computed automatically by the solver and must equal the **weight minus buoyancy** (residuals near zero).

## 3.3 Derivation: Total Hull Girder Vertical Bending Moment

The vertical bending moment in a ship's hull girder at a section located at longitudinal position $x$ is computed as the **moment of the shear force integrated along the length**.

**Step 1 — Define the load distribution (weight minus buoyancy curve):**

$$
q(x) = w(x) - b(x)
$$

where $w(x)$ is the weight per unit length (N/m) and $b(x)$ is the buoyancy per unit length (N/m).

**Step 2 — Shear force at section $x$:**

$$
V(x) = \int_0^{x} q(\xi) \, d\xi
$$

**Step 3 — Bending moment at section $x$:**

$$
M(x) = \int_0^{x} V(\xi) \, d\xi = \int_0^{x} \int_0^{\xi} q(\eta) \, d\eta \, d\xi
$$

**Step 4 — By Leibniz integral rule (alternative form):**

$$
M(x) = \int_0^{x} (x - \xi) \, q(\xi) \, d\xi
$$

**Step 5 — Bending stress at a fiber distance $y$ from neutral axis:**

$$
\sigma(x, y) = \dfrac{M(x) \cdot y}{I}
$$

**Step 6 — Maximum bending stress (at deck or keel, whichever is $y_{max}$):**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{\vert M_{max} \vert \cdot y_{max}}{I}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign convention:** **Hogging** (sagging in middle) produces tension at deck; **Sagging** (humping in middle) produces tension at keel. The KTU board expects the student to identify and use the **correct sign of $M$** with the **correct fiber location $y$**.

## 3.4 Derivation: Symmetry Boundary Condition for Half-Ship Model

To reduce computational cost, the **full ship** is often modeled as **½ ship (port half)** or **¼ ship (with two symmetry planes)**.

**Step 1 — For a transverse symmetry plane (athwartships, at centerline $y = 0$):**
- The **lateral displacement** is zero: $v = 0$.
- The **rotations** about the longitudinal and vertical axes are zero: $\theta_x = 0$, $\theta_z = 0$.
- The **longitudinal** and **vertical displacements** ($u$, $w$) and the **transverse rotation** $\theta_y$ are free.

**Step 2 — Mathematical constraint set:**

$$
v \big\vert_{y=0} = 0, \quad \theta_x \big\vert_{y=0} = 0, \quad \theta_z \big\vert_{y=0} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — For longitudinal symmetry (midship section, $x = 0$):**
- $u = 0$, $\theta_y = 0$, $\theta_z = 0$; others free.

**Step 4 — Total constraint count for a half-ship model with midship symmetry (¼ model):**

$$
N_{constrained} = N_{nodes} \times 3
$$

This is enough to remove all 6 rigid body modes (3 translations + 3 rotations).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference

| Derivation | Input | Output | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hydrostatic pressure | $\rho$, $g$, $h$ | $p$ | External hull pressure |
| Global equilibrium | $F_i$, $M_i$ | Residual check | FE validation |
| Bending moment | $q(x)$ | $M(x)$ | Hull girder strength |
| Bending stress | $M$, $y$, $I$ | $\sigma$ | Yield check |
| Symmetry BCs | Geometry plane | Constraint set | Model size reduction |
| Inertial release | Net $F$, $M$ = 0 | Auto balance | Free-free ship |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🧩 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: FE Load & BC Application Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **engineering workflow** of defining loads and boundary conditions on a ship FE model, from real-world scenario to solved simulation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Define Design Scenario] --> B0{SW or Wave}
    B0 -->|Still Water| C0[Compute Weight Curve w x]
    B0 -->|Wave| D0[Compute Pressure via Hydrodyn]
    C0 --> E0[Derive Hydrostatic Pressure]
    D0 --> F0[Derive Dynamic Pressure]
    E0 --> G0[Add Internal Loads: Tanks, Cargo, Ballast]
    F0 --> G0
    G0 --> H0[Add Inertial Loads: Self Weight g]
    H0 --> I0{Choose BC Strategy}
    I0 -->|Symmetry| J0[Apply Symmetry Constraints: v=0, theta_x=0, theta_z=0]
    I0 -->|Inertial Release| K0[No Physical BC, Free-Free Model]
    I0 -->|Elastic Support| L0[Apply Spring Stiffness k at Supports]
    J0 --> M0[Run FE Solver]
    K0 --> M0
    L0 --> M0
    M0 --> N0[Check Global Equilibrium: Sum F=0, Sum M=0]
    N0 --> O0{Equilibrium OK?}
    O0 -->|No| P0[Debug: Loads or BCs Inconsistent]
    P0 --> B0
    O0 -->|Yes| Q0[Post-Process: Stress, Deflection, Reactions]
    Q0 --> R0[End: Design Verification per IACS CSR]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Hull Cross-Section with Load and BC Annotation

The following Mermaid block diagram visualizes a **typical midship cross-section** with the key load and boundary condition labels used in ship FEA.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph crossSection["Midship Cross-Section"]
        A1[Deck Plate] --- A2[Deck Longitudinals]
        A2 --- A3[Side Shell]
        A3 --- A4[Bottom Shell]
        A4 --- A5[Keel Plate]
        A3 --- A6[Transverse Web Frame]
        A6 --- A4
    end
    P1[/External Pressure p_ext rho g h/] --> A3
    P2[/Internal Pressure p_int/] -.-> A6
    W1[Weight W rho g V] --- crossSection
    B1[Buoyancy F_b rho g nabla] --- crossSection
    BC1[v = 0 at Centerline] -.-> A6
    BC2[theta_x = 0] -.-> A6
    BC3[theta_z = 0] -.-> A6
    M1[Bending Moment M x] --> A1
    S1[Shear Force V x] --> A3
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Ship FE Load Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Hull Girder Loads] --> L1a[Still Water Bending Moment M_sw]
    L1 --> L1b[Wave Bending Moment M_w]
    L1 --> L1c[Shear Force V]
    L1 --> L1d[Torsional Moment T]
    L2[Local Loads] --> L2a[External Hydrostatic p_hs]
    L2 --> L2b[External Hydrodynamic p_hd]
    L2 --> L2c[Slamming F_slam]
    L2 --> L2d[Green Water]
    L3[Operational Loads] --> L3a[Cargo Load]
    L3 --> L3b[Ballast Load]
    L3 --> L3c[Machinery Reaction]
    L3 --> L3d[Mooring/Tug Load]
    L1a --> R0[Total Combined FE Load Vector]
    L1b --> R0
    L1c --> R0
    L1d --> R0
    L2a --> R0
    L2b --> R0
    L2c --> R0
    L2d --> R0
    L3a --> R0
    L3b --> R0
    L3c --> R0
    L3d --> R0
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define "Boundary Conditions" in the context of ship FE analysis. List any two types of boundary conditions used in naval architecture.**

> `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Boundary conditions in ship FEA are the mathematical constraints applied to the FE model that define the support, restraint, and symmetry of the structure, removing rigid body motion. They prescribe known displacements, rotations, or symmetric relationships at specific degrees of freedom (DOFs).

**Two common types:**
1. **Symmetry boundary conditions** (e.g., at centerline: $v = 0$, $\theta_x = 0$, $\theta_z = 0$)
2. **Inertial release** (no physical BC; the free-floating ship is balanced by net force/moment = 0)

---

### **Q2. What is "Inertial Release" and why is it preferred for full-ship FE models?**

> `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Inertial release is a numerical technique where the FE solver automatically computes the support reactions needed to balance the global loads on a **free-free body**, by adding a small number of "inertial" elements (masses) that prevent rigid body motion without imposing external constraints.

**Why preferred for ships:**
- A ship is a **floating free body** — physically it has no fixed support.
- Traditional clamping at midship is artificial and may introduce stress concentrations.
- Inertial release yields a **physically realistic stress state** and still yields a unique solution.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Module 4]`

**(a) Classify the various types of loads applied to a ship FE model. Explain the difference between hull girder loads and local loads with suitable examples.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) A container ship of length $L = 200 \; \text{m}$, draft $T = 12 \; \text{m}$ is analyzed using a half-ship FE model. Apply the appropriate symmetry boundary conditions at the centerline. If the maximum hull girder vertical bending moment is $M_v = 1.5 \times 10^9 \; \text{N·m}$ at midship, calculate the maximum bending stress at the deck, given the section modulus $Z_{deck} = 12 \; \text{m}^3$.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Classification of FE loads (4 marks):**

1. **Hull girder (global) loads** — Act on the entire ship as a beam.
   - Still water bending moment $M_{sw}$, Wave bending moment $M_w$, Vertical shear $V$, Torsional moment $T$.

2. **Local loads** — Act on individual plates, stiffeners, or panels.
   - External hydrostatic + hydrodynamic pressure, slamming, green water, internal tank pressure, cargo pressure.

3. **Operational loads** — Service-specific.
   - Cargo, ballast, machinery reactions, mooring forces.

**Difference (3 marks):**
- **Hull girder loads** vary slowly along the ship length and produce global bending/shear in the girder.
- **Local loads** vary rapidly over short spans and produce local plate bending between stiffeners; they are superimposed on the global response.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Apply symmetry BCs at centerline ($y = 0$):**

For port-starboard symmetry, the lateral displacement and two rotations vanish at the centerline plane:

$$
v \big\vert_{y=0} = 0, \quad \theta_x \big\vert_{y=0} = 0, \quad \theta_z \big\vert_{y=0} = 0
$$

The longitudinal displacement $u$, vertical displacement $w$, and transverse rotation $\theta_y$ remain free.

**Step 2 — Bending stress formula:**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{\vert M_v \vert}{Z_{deck}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^9 \; \text{N·m}}{12 \; \text{m}^3}
$$

$$
\sigma_{max} = 1.25 \times 10^8 \; \text{Pa} = 125 \; \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with allowable:**
For grade A ship steel, $\sigma_y = 235 \; \text{MPa}$.

$$
\text{Utilization} = \dfrac{125}{235} \times 100\% \approx 53.2\%
$$

✅ **The deck is safe; the stress is well within the allowable yield limit.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Module 4]`

**(a) Define hydrostatic pressure. Derive the expression for hydrostatic pressure acting on a ship hull and compute the pressure at the keel for a vessel with draft $T = 10 \; \text{m}$.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the concept of symmetry in ship FE modeling. With neat sketches, describe the three types of symmetry boundary conditions used in ship structural analysis.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Hydrostatic pressure is the pressure exerted by a fluid at rest due to the weight of the fluid column above the point. It varies linearly with depth and acts normal to the wetted surface.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
Consider a fluid element of horizontal area $A$ at depth $h$. The weight of the fluid column above is $W = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h$. Balancing with pressure force $P = p \cdot A$:

$$
p \cdot A = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h
$$

$$
p = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h
$$

**Numerical evaluation (2 marks):**
At the keel, $h = T = 10 \; \text{m}$:

$$
p_{keel} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 10
$$

$$
p_{keel} = 100{,}553 \; \text{Pa} \approx 100.6 \; \text{kPa}
$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Concept (2 marks):**
Symmetry in ship FE modeling exploits the geometric and loading symmetry of the vessel (port-starboard, fore-aft) to model only a fraction of the ship, reducing computational cost without loss of accuracy.

**Three types (5 marks):**

1. **Transverse (port-starboard) symmetry** — at the centerline plane ($y = 0$):
   - Constrained: $v = 0$, $\theta_x = 0$, $\theta_z = 0$
   - Free: $u$, $w$, $\theta_y$

2. **Longitudinal (fore-aft) symmetry** — at the midship plane ($x = 0$):
   - Constrained: $u = 0$, $\theta_y = 0$, $\theta_z = 0$
   - Free: $v$, $w$, $\theta_x$

3. **Cyclic symmetry** — for axial-symmetric structures (e.g., bulkheads with regular stiffener spacing). DOFs at one edge are tied to the next via cyclic coupling equations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Sign of $M$ and $y$:** Forgetting that hogging and sagging produce **opposite** stress signs at deck and keel — students lose 1-2 marks for sign errors.
> 2. **Half-ship BCs:** Many students write $u = w = 0$ at centerline (which is wrong!) — the correct constraint is $v = 0$ (lateral direction is the one perpendicular to the symmetry plane).
> 3. **Units mix-up:** Mixing $\text{kN}$ and $\text{N}$ in bending moment calculations. Always check: $1 \; \text{kN·m} = 10^3 \; \text{N·m}$.
> 4. **Forgetting $g$ in self-weight:** Self-weight is $m \cdot g$, not just $m$. This is a classic 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Inertial release misunderstanding:** Some students think "no BCs" means free-fall — incorrect. Inertial release uses a small mass matrix to remove rigid body modes while preserving stress resultants.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Fire Recall — Module 4, Topic: FE Model Loads and Boundary Conditions**

- ✅ **Loads** = Forces, pressures, accelerations, moments applied to the FE model.
- ✅ **Boundary conditions** = Mathematical constraints to prevent rigid body motion.
- ✅ Three load levels: **Hull girder, Local, Operational**.
- ✅ Hydrostatic pressure: $p = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ (linear with depth).
- ✅ Keel pressure: $p_{keel} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot T$ ≈ 1 atm per 10 m draft.
- ✅ Bending moment integrates load curve twice: $M(x) = \int_0^x (x-\xi) q(\xi) \, d\xi$.
- ✅ Bending stress: $\sigma = M \cdot y / I$ or $\sigma = M / Z$ where $Z = I / y$.
- ✅ Hogging → tension at deck; Sagging → tension at keel.
- ✅ Symmetry BCs at centerline: $v = 0$, $\theta_x = 0$, $\theta_z = 0$.
- ✅ Midship symmetry BCs: $u = 0$, $\theta_y = 0$, $\theta_z = 0$.
- ✅ **Inertial release** = preferred for free-floating full-ship models.
- ✅ Global equilibrium check: $\sum F = 0$, $\sum M = 0$ (6 equations).
- ✅ Constants to memorize: $g = 9.81$, $\rho_{sw} = 1025$, $\rho_{st} = 7850$, $E = 210 \; \text{GPa}$, $\sigma_y = 235 \; \text{MPa}$ (grade A).
- ✅ IACS CSR is the governing rule set for ship FE load/BC definition.
- ✅ Common FEA software: **NAPA, FEMAP/Nastran, Ansys AQWA, Sesam (DNV), MAESTRO (LMSC)**.

---

**End of Module 4 — Topic: FE Model Loads and Boundary Conditions**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme | B.Tech Naval Architecture*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Post Processing - Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST753 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS IN NAVAL ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Post Processing - Methods # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Post-Processing** in Finite Element Analysis (FEA) refers to the systematic phase that follows the successful solution of the global equilibrium equations. It encompasses the computational techniques, visualization methods, and engineering interpretation procedures used to extract, transform, present, and critically evaluate the numerical results (displacements, stresses, strains, temperatures, natural frequencies, etc.) computed by the solver. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PENST753, post-processing transforms raw solver output — typically a vector of nodal displacements $[\mathbf{U}]$ — into engineer-meaningful quantities such as stress fields, reaction forces, modal shapes, and safety factors that inform design decisions in **naval architecture** (e.g., ship hull deflection, plating stresses, stiffener efficiency). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Expectation):** > *Post-processing is the stage in FEA where computed nodal displacements are used to derive secondary quantities (strains, stresses, gradients), and these are presented through graphical/tabular means to enable engineering interpretation, design validation, and code-compliance verification.* --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "X-Ray Interpretation" Viewpoint Imagine a doctor performs an **MRI scan** on a patient. The MRI machine produces raw data — millions of magnetic resonance signals. However, the doctor does not deliver "raw signals" to the patient. Instead, the technician **post-processes** this data into clear, color-coded images (sagittal, axial, 3-D reconstructions) so the doctor can identify a fracture or tumor. In exactly the same way: | **MRI Workflow** | **FEA Post-Processing** | |---|---| | Raw signal data from scanner | Nodal displacement vector $[\mathbf{U}]$ | | Image reconstruction algorithm | Strain-displacement matrix $[\mathbf{B}]$ and stress-strain matrix $[\mathbf{D}]$ | | Color-coded slice visualization | Contour plots of stress/displacement | | Doctor's diagnostic interpretation | Engineer's design decision | > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Intuition:** > Solving the FEA equations gives you the **displacements** at every node — these are like the **skeleton** of the result. Post-processing gives the **stress skin** — the colorful, physically meaningful view that lets an engineer *see* where the ship hull is overstressed, where a stiffener is overworked, or where buckling may initiate. --- ## 1.3 Why Post-Processing is Critical in Naval Architecture In ship structural design, raw displacements alone are **insufficient** for the following reasons: 1. **Regulatory Compliance:** Classification societies (DNV, LR, ABS, IRS) require **stress contour maps** at scantlings, not just displacement values. 2. **Safety Margins:** Engineers must compute **von Mises**, **principal stresses**, and **safety factors** before approving a structural detail. 3. **Fatigue & Buckling Checks:** Post-processed stress gradients enable fatigue life estimation (e.g., **Palmgren-Miner** rule) and plate buckling assessment. 4. **Weight Optimization:** Visualizing stress distribution reveals **under-utilized material** that can be thinned, reducing displacement tonnage. --- ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold-Required) The following metrics recur throughout naval-architecture post-processing: - **Young's Modulus for shipbuilding steel:** $\mathbf{E = 210\ \text{GPa}}$ - **Poisson's Ratio:** $\mathbf{\nu = 0.3}$ - **Yield Strength (mild steel AH36):** $\mathbf{\sigma_y = 355\ \text{MPa}}$ - **Density of seawater:** $\mathbf{\rho = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3}$ - **Gravitational acceleration:** $\mathbf{g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2}$ - **Unit conventions:** Displacement in **mm**, Stress in **MPa**, Forces in **kN** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Post-Processing Pipeline — Structured Logical Breakdown The post-processing phase is best understood as a **four-stage pipeline**, each stage transforming the data into progressively more meaningful form. ### **Stage 1 — Solution Retrieval** The solver writes a results file containing: - Nodal displacements: $[\mathbf{U}] = \{U_1, U_2, \ldots, U_n\}^T$ - Reaction forces at constrained DOFs - Element-level data (optional): element stresses at integration points (Gauss points) ### **Stage 2 — Strain Recovery** At every element, strains are computed from displacements using the **strain-displacement matrix** $[\mathbf{B}]$: $$\{\varepsilon\} = [\mathbf{B}]\ \{\mathbf{u}\}_e$$ For a 2-D plane-stress quadrilateral (typical of ship plate panels), $[\mathbf{B}]$ is constructed from the element shape function derivatives $[\mathbf{N}]$. ### **Stage 3 — Stress Recovery** Stresses follow from the **constitutive (stress-strain) matrix** $[\mathbf{D}]$: $$\{\sigma\} = [\mathbf{D}]\ \{\varepsilon\} = [\mathbf{D}][\mathbf{B}]\ \{\mathbf{u}\}_e$$ > [!NOTE] > For naval architecture, the $[\mathbf{D}]$ matrix depends on **plate theory assumptions**: > - **Plane stress** $(\sigma_z = 0)$ for thin plates > - **Plane strain** $(\varepsilon_z = 0)$ for thick sections > - **Mindlin/Reissner-Mindlin** for shear-deformable ship plating > - **Kirchhoff-Love** for thin plating in flexure ### **Stage 4 — Engineering Quantity Derivation** From $\{\sigma\}$, the engineer derives: - **Principal stresses** $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$ - **von Mises equivalent stress** $\sigma_{vm}$ - **Tresca criterion** stress $\sigma_{Tresca}$ - **Safety factor (SF)** $= \sigma_y / \sigma_{vm}$ - **Stress gradients** (for fatigue) - **Reaction forces** (for global equilibrium check) --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table compiles **every formula** you need for post-processing questions in the KTU ESE. Memorize these — at least three of them will appear in **Part A** and a derivation will appear in **Part B**. ### **Master Formula Table** | **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Notes / Units** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Strain from displacement | $\{\varepsilon\} = [\mathbf{B}]\ \{\mathbf{u}\}_e$ | $\varepsilon$ is dimensionless | | 2 | Stress from strain | $\{\sigma\} = [\mathbf{D}]\ \{\varepsilon\}$ | $\sigma$ in Pa | | 3 | Direct combination (key post-processing equation) | $\{\sigma\} = [\mathbf{D}][\mathbf{B}]\ \{\mathbf{u}\}_e$ | All in consistent SI units | | 4 | Plane-stress $[\mathbf{D}]$ matrix | $\dfrac{E}{1-\nu^2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \tfrac{1-\nu}{2} \end{bmatrix}$ | For thin ship plating | | 5 | Plane-strain $[\mathbf{D}]$ matrix | $\dfrac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}\begin{bmatrix} 1-\nu & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1-\nu & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \tfrac{1-2\nu}{2} \end{bmatrix}$ | For thick sections | | 6 | Principal stresses | $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x+\sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Sorted: $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2$ | | 7 | Maximum in-plane shear | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\vert \sigma_1 - \sigma_2 \vert}{2}$ | Used for Tresca | | 8 | von Mises (2-D) | $\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 - \sigma_x\sigma_y + 3\tau_{xy}^2}$ | For ductile ship steel | | 9 | von Mises (3-D) | $\sigma_{vm} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}$ | General form | | 10 | Tresca criterion | $\sigma_{Tresca} = \sigma_1 -
