# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** 
**Explain why stresses in FEA are computed at Gauss points rather than at the element nodes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Stresses are derived from strains using σ = Dε, where ε = Bu. The [B] matrix involves derivatives of shape functions, which are polynomial functions of (ξ, η). For an *n*-node isoparametric element, these derivatives are polynomials of degree (n−2), and the resulting strains are polynomials of degree (2n−2). Gauss quadrature integrates such polynomials **exactly** when 2n−2 ≤ 2k−1 (where k is the number of Gauss points per direction). Hence, the most accurate and mathematically consistent location to evaluate stresses is at the Gauss points. Additionally, stresses at nodes are less accurate because the strains at nodes are obtained by extrapolation, which introduces error. *(3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** 
**What is the Von Mises stress? Write its expression for a 2D plane-stress state and state its significance in ship structural design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Von Mises stress (σ_vm)** is a scalar measure of the multi-axial stress state that represents the onset of yielding in ductile materials. For a 2D plane-stress state with σₓ, σᵧ, τₓᵧ:

$$\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\sigma_{x}^{2}-\sigma_{x}\sigma_{y}+\sigma_{y}^{2}+3\tau_{xy}^{2}}$$

**Significance in ship design:** As per classification rules (DNV, LR, ABS), a ship's hull plate passes the yield check if σ_vm ≤ σ_y/safety_factor (typically σ_y/1.1 for AH36 steel, σ_y = 355 N/mm²). It accounts for combined membrane + bending + shear, which is the realistic loading scenario on ship panels. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Question (14 Marks, Module 4 Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4 Set 1]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for stress recovery in a 2D plane-stress finite element from the displacement solution. Show clearly the role of the [B] and [D] matrices. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a hull plate element, the recovered stresses at a Gauss point are: σₓ = 180 N/mm², σᵧ = 60 N/mm², τₓᵧ = 45 N/mm². Determine:
   - (i) The principal stresses σ₁ and σ₂.
   - (ii) The maximum in-plane shear stress.
   - (iii) The Von Mises stress.
   - (iv) Check yielding against AH36 steel (σ_y = 355 N/mm²) using safety factor 1.1. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting with the equilibrium equation solved at the global level:
$$[K]\{U\} = \{F\} \Rightarrow \{U\} \text{ is known.}$$

For an element *e*, we extract nodal displacements {uᵉ} from {U} using the connectivity array. The displacement field inside the element is interpolated using shape functions:
$$u(x,y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(x,y)\, u_i, \quad v(x,y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(x,y)\, v_i$$

The **strain-displacement relation** for 2D plane stress is:

$$\{\varepsilon\} = \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_x \\ \varepsilon_y \\ \gamma_{xy} \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \partial/\partial x & 0 \\ 0 & \partial/\partial y \\ \partial/\partial y & \partial/\partial x \end{bmatrix}\begin{Bmatrix} u \\ v \end{Bmatrix}$$

Substituting the shape function expansion:

$$\{\varepsilon\} = [B]\{u^{e}\}$$

where the [B] matrix contains the partial derivatives of *Nᵢ* with respect to *x* and *y*. For isoparametric elements, these are obtained via the inverse Jacobian:

$$\begin{Bmatrix} \partial N_i/\partial x \\ \partial N_i/\partial y \end{Bmatrix} = [J]^{-1}\begin{Bmatrix} \partial N_i/\partial \xi \\ \partial N_i/\partial \eta \end{Bmatrix}$$

Applying the **constitutive law (Hooke's Law)** for plane stress:

$$\{\sigma\} = [D]\{\varepsilon\} = \frac{E}{1-\nu^{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & (1-\nu)/2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_x \\ \varepsilon_y \\ \gamma_{xy} \end{Bmatrix}$$

Combining the two steps:

$$\boxed{\{\sigma\} = [D][B]\{u^{e}\} = [S]\{u^{e}\}}$$

where [S] = [D][B] is the stress-displacement matrix. This is evaluated at each Gauss point (ξ_GP, η_GP) of the element. *(7 marks — full derivation with all intermediate steps)*

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: σₓ = 180 N/mm², σᵧ = 60 N/mm², τₓᵧ = 45 N/mm².

**(i) Principal stresses (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}\sigma_{1,2} &= \frac{\sigma_x+\sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} \\[4pt]&= \frac{180+60}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{180-60}{2}\right)^2 + 45^2} \\[4pt]&= 120 \pm \sqrt{(60)^2 + (45)^2} \\[4pt]&= 120 \pm \sqrt{3600+2025} \\[4pt]&= 120 \pm \sqrt{5625} \\[4pt]&= 120 \pm 75\end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \sigma_1 = 195 \text{ N/mm}^2, \quad \sigma_2 = 45 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**(ii) Maximum in-plane shear stress (1 mark):**

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \sqrt{(60)^2 + (45)^2} = 75 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**(iii) Von Mises stress (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}\sigma_{vm} &= \sqrt{\sigma_x^{2}-\sigma_x\sigma_y+\sigma_y^{2}+3\tau_{xy}^{2}} \\[4pt]&= \sqrt{(180)^2-(180)(60)+(60)^2+3(45)^2} \\[4pt]&= \sqrt{32400-10800+3600+6075} \\[4pt]&= \sqrt{31275} \\[4pt]&\approx 176.85 \text{ N/mm}^2\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Yield check (2 marks):**

$$\sigma_{allowable} = \frac{\sigma_y}{1.1} = \frac{355}{1.1} = 322.73 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Since σ_vm = 176.85 N/mm² < 322.73 N/mm² → **The hull plate PASSES the yield check (utilization = 54.8%).**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 Set 2]**

**(a)** Explain with mathematical justification the **stress extrapolation** procedure from Gauss points to element nodes for a 4-noded bilinear quadrilateral element. Mention the role of nodal averaging in obtaining continuous stress contours. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship bottom plate panel is subjected to a 2D plane-stress state. The following stresses were recovered at three separate points:

| Point | σₓ (N/mm²) | σᵧ (N/mm²) | τₓᵧ (N/mm²) |
|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 220 | 80 | 30 |
| P2 | 180 | 90 | 50 |
| P3 | 250 | 50 | 40 |

For each point, determine whether the plate satisfies the yield criterion using **Tresca's criterion** with σ_y = 235 N/mm² and safety factor 1.1. Identify the **critical point** requiring redesign. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Stress Extrapolation (7 marks):**

In a 4-noded isoparametric quadrilateral (Q4) element, the standard Gauss quadrature uses 2×2 sampling points at (ξ, η) = (±1/√3, ±1/√3). The stress is recovered at these four interior points, but for post-processing (contour plots, interpretation), stresses are needed at the four corner nodes.

The **bilinear shape functions** of the Q4 element are:

$$N_i(\xi,\eta) = \frac{1}{4}(1+\xi\xi_i)(1+\eta\eta_i), \quad i=1,2,3,4$$

Since stresses inside the element are assumed to vary bilinearly (same order as displacement field), we can express each nodal stress as a linear combination of the four Gauss-point stresses:

$$\sigma_{node,i} = \sum_{j=1}^{4} E_{ij}\,\sigma_{GP,j}$$

where [E] is the **extrapolation matrix** of size 4×4. For the 2×2 Gauss scheme, [E] takes the closed form:

$$[E] = \begin{bmatrix} a & b & c & d \\ b & a & d & c \\ c & d & a & b \\ d & c & b & a \end{bmatrix}, \quad a=2.3660,\ b=0.3660,\ c=0.5000,\ d=-1.3660$$

The columns/rows of [E] sum to 4 — ensuring **stress conservation** under averaging.

**Nodal Averaging:** At a node shared by *m* surrounding elements, each element contributes its own extrapolated nodal stress. The **averaged nodal stress** is:

$$\bar{\sigma}_k = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{e=1}^{m} \sigma_{k}^{(e)}$$

This averaging is essential to obtain a **continuous, single-valued stress field** across element boundaries, which is a prerequisite for smooth contour plots. Without averaging, the contour would show **discontinuities** at every element edge — a hallmark of poor post-processing. *(7 marks)*

**Part (b) — Tresca Yield Check (7 marks):**

For plane stress (σ_z = 0), the three principal stresses at each point are ordered as:
$$\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq 0 \geq \sigma_3 = 0 \quad \text{(if both in-plane stresses positive)}$$

or
$$\sigma_1 \geq 0 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3 \quad \text{(if in-plane stresses have opposite signs)}$$

In all cases, the **Tresca stress** is:

$$\sigma_{Tresca} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3$$

**Allowable Tresca stress:**

$$\sigma_{Tresca,allow} = \frac{\sigma_y}{1.1} = \frac{235}{1.1} = 213.64 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Point P1: σₓ = 220, σᵧ = 80, τₓᵧ = 30**

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{220+80}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{220-80}{2}\right)^2 + 30^2} = 150 \pm \sqrt{(70)^2 + 900} = 150 \pm \sqrt{5800} = 150 \pm 76.16$$

$$\sigma_1 = 226.16, \quad \sigma_2 = 73.84, \quad \sigma_3 = 0$$

$$\sigma_{Tresca} = 226.16 - 0 = 226.16 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Utilization = 226.16/213.64 = **105.86% → FAILS** ❌

**Point P2: σₓ = 180, σᵧ = 90, τₓᵧ = 50**

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{180+90}{2} \pm \sqrt{(45)^2 + (50)^2} = 135 \pm \sqrt{2025+2500} = 135 \pm \sqrt{4525} = 135 \pm 67.27$$

$$\sigma_1 = 202.27, \quad \sigma_2 = 67.73, \quad \sigma_3 = 0$$

$$\sigma_{Tresca} = 202.27 - 0 = 202.27 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Utilization = 202.27/213.64 = **94.67% → PASSES** ✓

**Point P3: σₓ = 250, σᵧ = 50, τₓᵧ = 40**

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{250+50}{2} \pm \sqrt{(100)^2 + (40)^2} = 150 \pm \sqrt{11600} = 150 \pm 107.70$$

$$\sigma_1 = 257.70, \quad \sigma_2 = 42.30, \quad \sigma_3 = 0$$

$$\sigma_{Tresca} = 257.70 - 0 = 257.70 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Utilization = 257.70/213.64 = **120.62% → FAILS** ❌

**Critical Point:** **Point P3** (highest utilization = 120.62%) requires **redesign** — typically by increasing plate thickness or stiffener spacing. Point P1 also fails marginally. *(7 marks — distributed as 2 marks per point + 1 mark identification)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 3 in the Von Mises formula** — Many students write σ_vm = √(σₓ² + σᵧ² + τₓᵧ²), which is **WRONG**. The correct plane-stress expression is **σ_vm = √(σₓ² − σₓσᵧ + σᵧ² + 3τₓᵧ²)**. Loss: 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using σ_y directly without safety factor** — Classification rules always include 1.1 (or higher) partial safety factor. Loss: 1 mark.
> 3. **Not ordering principal stresses correctly** — Always label σ₁ ≥ σ₂ ≥ σ₃ before applying Tresca. Loss: 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the σ₃ = 0 step in plane stress** — Forgetting that out-of-plane principal stress is zero. Loss: 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing plane stress vs plane strain [D] matrix** — For ship plates (t ≤ span/10), use **plane stress**; for thick members, plane strain. Loss: 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Stress Results (Module 4)**

- ⭐ **Master Formula:** {σ} = [D][B]{uᵉ} — the **trinity** of FEA stress recovery.
- ⭐ **Compute at Gauss points** — not at nodes. Extrapolate outward using shape functions.
- ⭐ **Nodal averaging** at shared nodes gives **smooth, continuous contours** — essential for design interpretation.
- ⭐ **Plane stress [D]** for ship plates (σ_z = 0); plane strain [D] for thick sections.
- ⭐ **Principal stress formula:** σ₁,₂ = (σₓ+σᵧ)/2 ± √[((σₓ−σᵧ)/2)² + τₓᵧ²].
- ⭐ **Von Mises (plane stress):** σ_vm = √[σₓ² − σₓσᵧ + σᵧ² + 3τₓᵧ²].
- ⭐ **Tresca:** σ_T = σ₁ − σ₃ (always σ_T ≤ 1.155 × σ_vm for plane stress).
- ⭐ **AH36 shipbuilding steel:** σ_y = 355 N/mm², E = 2.05×10⁵ N/mm², ν = 0.30.
- ⭐ **Class approval check:** σ_vm ≤ σ_y / 1.1 (DNV/LR/ABS standard partial safety factor).
- ⭐ **Jacobian [J]** is required to convert shape-function derivatives from (ξ, η) to (x, y).
- ⭐ **Stress extrapolation matrix [E]** for Q4 with 2×2 GPs has closed form with a=2.366, b=0.366, c=0.5, d=−1.366.
- ⭐ **Multi-axial Von Mises (3D):** σ_vm = √[½{(σ₁−σ₂)² + (σ₂−σ₃)² + (σ₃−σ₁)²}].
- ⭐ **In Naval Architecture applications:** stress recovery is the bridge between mathematical FEA solution and **classification society approval** — the practical deliverable.
- ⭐ **Common pitfall:** von Mises is NOT a stress that physically exists — it is a *scalar failure index*; the real physical stresses are σₓ, σᵧ, τₓᵧ.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fatigue and Fracture Results
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST753 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS IN NAVAL ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fatigue and Fracture Results

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fatigue and Fracture Results** in Finite Element Analysis (FEA) for Naval Architecture refer to the post-processed outputs obtained by evaluating the time-dependent and stress-intensity-dependent failure criteria of marine structures (hulls, offshore platforms, propellers, brackets, stiffened panels) subjected to cyclic wave loading, slamming loads, vibration, and combined operational stresses. These results quantify **fatigue life (N)**, **damage accumulation (D)**, **stress intensity factor (K)**, **crack growth rate (da/dN)**, and **critical fracture parameters (K_IC, J-integral)** at critical hotspots identified through mesh convergence studies.

The discipline sits at the intersection of three pillars:
1. **Computational Solid Mechanics (CSM)** – solved nodal stress field $\{\sigma\}$.
2. **Fatigue Life Prediction Theory** – Miner's rule, S-N curves, and spectral methods.
3. **Fracture Mechanics** – Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics (LEFM) and Elastic-Plastic Fracture Mechanics (EPFM).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Paper Clip:**
> Take a standard metal paper clip and bend it back-and-forth 5–10 times. It breaks — not because you exceeded its single-load strength (which is huge for a paper clip), but because each tiny bend causes microscopic damage that **accumulates** with every cycle. This is **fatigue**. Now imagine a tiny invisible crack at the bend point growing larger with each cycle until catastrophic failure. This combined damage-and-crack-propagation process is the essence of **fatigue and fracture** analysis.
>
> In a ship, every wave-induced hull bending cycle is a "bend" of the paper clip. Over a vessel's 25-year service life, the hull experiences **$10^7$ to $10^8$ stress cycles**. FEA-based fatigue tools predict *where* the "paper clip will break first" and *when*.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

The following engineering constants govern fatigue and fracture assessment in marine FEA:

| Symbol | Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\sigma_a$ | Alternating stress amplitude | **MPa** |
| $\sigma_m$ | Mean stress | **MPa** |
| $N_f$ | Cycles to failure | dimensionless |
| $K$ | Stress intensity factor | **MPa·m$^{1/2}$** |
| $K_{IC}$ | Fracture toughness | **MPa·m$^{1/2}$** (steel ≈ 50–200) |
| $C, m$ | Paris law constants | $C \approx 10^{-11}$ to $10^{-9}$ |
| $Y(a)$ | Geometry factor | dimensionless |
| $\Delta K_{th}$ | Threshold SIF range | **MPa·m$^{1/2}$** ≈ 3–7 |

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Logic of Fatigue-Fracture-FEA Pipeline

The analytical pipeline for generating fatigue and fracture results in naval FEA follows a strict sequence:

- **Step 1 — Load Extraction (Time Domain):** Long-term sea-keeping simulations (using potential theory, RAOs, or CFD) produce stress time histories $\sigma(t)$ at integration points of the FE mesh.
- **Step 2 — Cycle Counting (Rainflow Algorithm):** The continuous stress signal is decomposed into discrete cycles $\left(\sigma_{a,i}, \sigma_{m,i}\right)$ using the standardized **ASTM E1049-85 rainflow counting method**.
- **Step 3 — S-N Curve Application:** Each cycle is evaluated against the material's Wöhler (S-N) curve to extract $N_i$ (cycles-to-failure for that stress range).
- **Step 4 — Damage Accumulation (Miner's Rule):** Damage from all cycles is linearly summed:

$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$

- **Step 5 — Crack Propagation (Paris-Erdogan Law):** For pre-existing defects (e.g., weld toe cracks), the crack length $a$ grows as:

$$\frac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K)^m$$

- **Step 6 — Criticality Check:** Compare $D$ to 1.0 (failure) or $a$ to $a_{cr}$ (critical crack length from $K = K_{IC}$).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The following table is the single most important reference for Module 4 derivations and numerical problems. Memorize the form of every equation.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Use Case in Naval FEA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Goodman Mean Stress Correction | $\sigma_{ar} = \dfrac{\sigma_a}{1 - \sigma_m / \sigma_u}$ | Convert variable-amplitude to equivalent fully-reversed amplitude |
| 2 | S-N Curve (Power Law) | $\sigma_a \cdot N^b = K' \; \text{or} \; \log N = \log A - m \log \Delta \sigma$ | Basic fatigue life in base metal zones |
| 3 | Miner's Cumulative Damage | $D = \sum \dfrac{n_i}{N_i}$ | Spectral / long-term fatigue assessment |
| 4 | Palmgren-Miner Failure Criterion | Failure when $D \geq 1.0$ | Design check |
| 5 | Paris-Erdogan Crack Growth | $\dfrac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K)^m$ | Fracture analysis in stiffeners, welds |
| 6 | Stress Intensity Factor | $K = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Crack-tip driving force |
| 7 | Critical Crack Length | $a_{cr} = \dfrac{1}{\pi} \left(\dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y \sigma}\right)^2$ | Fitness-for-service (FFS) |
| 8 | Range of SIF | $\Delta K = K_{max} - K_{min} = Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Paris law input |
| 9 | Hot Spot Stress (HSS) | $\sigma_{HS} = 1.67 \cdot \sigma_{0.5t} - 0.67 \cdot \sigma_{1.5t}$ | IACS / DNV weld fatigue rules |
| 10 | Narrow-Band Spectral Density | $E[N] = \dfrac{T \cdot \nu_0 \cdot \sqrt{2 \pi \left(\ln N_{tot} - \ln \nu_0\right)}}{\Gamma(1 + m/2)} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\sigma_0 \sqrt{2}}{K'}\right)^m$ | Fatigue from sea state spectra |

> **Units Reminder:** All lengths (crack size $a$, thickness $t$) in **meters [m]**, stresses in **MPa**, stress intensity in **MPa·m$^{1/2}$**, and cycles $N$ in dimensionless count.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In ship classification (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, IACS), fatigue and fracture results from FEA determine:
- **Hull girder endurance limit** under 25-year North Atlantic wave spectrum.
- **Weld toe cracking** in critical details (bilge strake, hatch corners, side shells).
- **FPSO turret / mooring bracket** fatigue life.
- **Propeller blade root** crack initiation under combined bending + centrifugal force.
- **Submarine pressure hull** flaw tolerance under Rule 90-day diving cycles.

The classification societies demand **damage $D \leq 1.0$** with safety factors of 2–3 on life for primary structural members.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Miner's Rule from Linear Damage Mechanics

**Premise:** Assume that at any stress amplitude $\sigma_a$, a material can endure exactly $N_f(\sigma_a)$ cycles before failure. Each individual cycle consumes $1/N_f$ of the available "damage capacity." Damage from $n$ cycles at that amplitude is $n/N_f$. Summing across all encountered stress blocks gives total damage $D$. Failure occurs when $D = 1$.

**Step-by-step mathematical formulation:**

Let the loading spectrum be divided into $k$ discrete stress blocks. For block $i$:
- Stress range: $\Delta \sigma_i$
- Number of applied cycles: $n_i$
- Cycles to failure at this stress: $N_i$

The fractional damage in block $i$ is:

$$d_i = \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$

Summing over all $k$ blocks using the principle of linear superposition of independent damage increments:

$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} d_i = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$

The failure criterion is therefore:

$$D_{failure} = 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i} = 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Miner's rule assumes **no load-interaction effects** (no overload retardation or underload acceleration). It is therefore *non-conservative* for variable-amplitude spectra with large occasional peaks. Classification societies often use the **Dirlik** or **Tovo-Benasciutti** closed-form spectral methods instead.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Paris-Erdogan Crack Growth Law

**Premise:** Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics (LEFM) defines the crack-tip stress field intensity through the stress intensity factor $K$. For a through-thickness crack of length $2a$ in an infinite plate under remote uniform tension $\sigma$, the Mode-I stress intensity factor is:

$$K_I = \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$$

In finite geometries and welded joints, a **geometry correction factor** $Y(a)$ (or $F(a/W)$ where $W$ is plate width) modifies this:

$$K = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$$

The **stress intensity factor range** under cyclic loading is:

$$\Delta K = K_{max} - K_{min} = Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$$

Empirically, in the **stable crack growth region (Region II of the da/dN vs. $\Delta K$ curve)**, the crack extension per cycle is observed to obey a power law. Paris and Erdogan (1963) proposed:

$$\frac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^m$$

where $C$ and $m$ are material constants (typically $m = 2$–$4$ for steels, $m = 3$ for aluminum).

**Integration to obtain cycles-to-failure:**

Separate variables and integrate from the initial crack size $a_0$ to the critical size $a_{cr}$:

$$N_f = \int_{a_0}^{a_{cr}} \frac{da}{C (\Delta K)^m} = \int_{a_0}^{a_{cr}} \frac{da}{C \left( Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi a} \right)^m}$$

Assuming $Y$ is approximately constant over the integration range (valid for small cracks or semi-empirical treatment):

$$N_f = \frac{1}{C (Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi})^m} \int_{a_0}^{a_{cr}} a^{-m/2} \, da$$

For $m \neq 2$:

$$N_f = \frac{1}{C (Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi})^m} \cdot \left[ \frac{a^{1 - m/2}}{1 - m/2} \right]_{a_0}^{a_{cr}}$$

$$\boxed{N_f = \frac{a_0^{1 - m/2} - a_{cr}^{1 - m/2}}{C (Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi})^m (m/2 - 1)}}$$

The critical crack size is found from the fracture toughness condition $K_{max} = K_{IC}$:

$$a_{cr} = \frac{1}{\pi} \left( \frac{K_{IC}}{Y \sigma_{max}} \right)^2$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Hot-Spot Stress Extrapolation (DNV-RP-C203 / IACS S34)

For welded plate joints, the nominal stress in the weld throat is masked by the local weld notch singularity. The **hot-spot stress** $\sigma_{HS}$ is defined as the **structural stress** at the weld toe, extrapolated to the surface from points at distances $0.5t$ and $1.5t$ (where $t$ is the plate thickness) from the weld toe.

**Linear extrapolation formula:**

$$\sigma_{HS} = 1.67 \cdot \sigma_{0.5t} - 0.67 \cdot \sigma_{1.5t}$$

**Derivation of coefficients:** A linear fit through two points $(x_1, \sigma_1) = (0.5t, \sigma_{0.5t})$ and $(x_2, \sigma_2) = (1.5t, \sigma_{1.5t})$ gives:

$$\sigma(x) = \sigma_{0.5t} + \frac{\sigma_{1.5t} - \sigma_{0.5t}}{1.5t - 0.5t} (x - 0.5t)$$

Setting $x = 0$ (extrapolation to the weld toe surface):

$$\sigma_{HS} = \sigma_{0.5t} - \frac{\sigma_{1.5t} - \sigma_{0.5t}}{t} \cdot 0.5t$$

$$\sigma_{HS} = \sigma_{0.5t} - 0.5 \sigma_{1.5t} + 0.5 \sigma_{0.5t} = 1.5 \sigma_{0.5t} - 0.5 \sigma_{1.5t}$$

The factor **1.67 / 0.67** (instead of 1.5 / 0.5) is the **modified coefficient** that accounts for the non-linear stress gradient in the actual plate — empirically calibrated by IIW (International Institute of Welding) and adopted by IACS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The hot-spot stress is then inserted into the relevant S-N curve** (typically FAT class 90, 71, or 63 in DNV-RP-C203) to compute fatigue life at the weld detail.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Numerical Example (Full Worked Solution)

**Problem:** A bilge strake weld in an A36 steel ship hull has a detected surface crack of length $a_0 = 1.5$ mm. Material data: $C = 6.89 \times 10^{-12}$ (SI, $\Delta K$ in MPa·m$^{1/2}$), $m = 3.0$, $K_{IC} = 55$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$, $Y = 1.0$, applied cyclic stress range $\Delta \sigma = 180$ MPa. Compute the fatigue life $N_f$ in cycles.

**Step 1:** Critical crack size:

$$a_{cr} = \frac{1}{\pi} \left( \frac{K_{IC}}{Y \sigma_{max}} \right)^2$$

Assuming $R = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max} = 0$ (fully reversed), so $\sigma_{max} = \Delta \sigma = 180$ MPa:

$$a_{cr} = \frac{1}{\pi} \left( \frac{55}{1.0 \times 180} \right)^2$$

$$a_{cr} = \frac{1}{\pi} (0.3056)^2 = \frac{0.0934}{\pi} = 0.0297 \text{ m} = 29.7 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Paris law integration formula with $m = 3$:

$$N_f = \frac{a_0^{1 - m/2} - a_{cr}^{1 - m/2}}{C (Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi})^m (m/2 - 1)}$$

Compute $1 - m/2 = 1 - 1.5 = -0.5$:

$$N_f = \frac{a_0^{-0.5} - a_{cr}^{-0.5}}{C (Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi})^3 \cdot 0.5}$$

**Step 3:** Numerical substitution:

$$a_0^{-0.5} = (0.0015)^{-0.5} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.0015}} = 25.82 \text{ m}^{-0.5}$$

$$a_{cr}^{-0.5} = (0.0297)^{-0.5} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.0297}} = 5.804 \text{ m}^{-0.5}$$

$$a_0^{-0.5} - a_{cr}^{-0.5} = 25.82 - 5.804 = 20.02 \text{ m}^{-0.5}$$

$$(Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi})^3 = (1.0 \times 180 \times 1.7725)^3 = (319.04)^3 = 3.247 \times 10^{7} \text{ (MPa)}^3 \text{ m}^{3/2}$$

$$C \times 0.5 \times 3.247 \times 10^{7} = 6.89 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.5 \times 3.247 \times 10^{7}$$

$$= 6.89 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.624 \times 10^{7} = 1.119 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$N_f = \frac{20.02}{1.119 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.789 \times 10^{5} \text{ cycles}$$

**Result:** The bilge strake can sustain approximately **$1.79 \times 10^5$ stress cycles** before the crack becomes critical — corresponding to a design life check of $\sim 1$ to 2 years under a typical $10^5$–$10^6$ cycles/year wave-induced stress spectrum. This implies that a repair or reinforcement plan is required.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Define the term "Hot-Spot Stress" as used in fatigue analysis of welded ship structures. Mention the standard extrapolation formula.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) Hot-spot stress is the **structural stress at the weld toe**, obtained by linear extrapolation of the stress field to the toe surface, excluding the local notch stress concentration of the weld profile itself.
- (1 mark) Standard formula (DNV / IACS):

$$\sigma_{HS} = 1.67 \cdot \sigma_{0.5t} - 0.67 \cdot \sigma_{1.5t}$$

- (1 mark) It is used with FAT-class S-N curves to evaluate the fatigue endurance of welded plate joints independent of the local weld geometry.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*State the Paris-Erdogan crack growth law. What are the units of C and m for a steel in SI units?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (2 marks) The Paris-Erdogan law:

$$\frac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^m$$

where $da/dN$ is the crack growth per cycle [m/cycle], $\Delta K$ is the stress intensity factor range [MPa·m$^{1/2}$], and $C$, $m$ are material constants.

- (1 mark) For a typical mild structural steel: $C \approx 10^{-11}$ to $10^{-12}$ (SI units, with $\Delta K$ in MPa·m$^{1/2}$ and $a$ in meters) and $m \approx 3.0$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set A]**

**(a)** Derive the Paris-Erdogan law for fatigue crack propagation starting from the definition of the stress intensity factor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A structural detail in a ship's side shell has an initial through-thickness crack of length $2a_0 = 2$ mm. The applied stress range is $\Delta \sigma = 200$ MPa, $R = 0$, $Y = 1.1$, $C = 5.0 \times 10^{-12}$, $m = 3.0$, and $K_{IC} = 80$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$. Calculate the number of cycles to reach critical crack size. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Stress Intensity Factor Definition (LEFM):**

For a central through-thickness crack of length $2a$ in an infinite plate:

$$K_I = \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$$

In a finite geometry with a geometry factor $Y$:

$$K = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$$

The cyclic range is:

$$\Delta K = Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$$

**Step 2 — Empirical Paris Law:**

In the stable growth region of the da/dN vs. $\Delta K$ curve, plotting both axes logarithmically reveals a straight line. Hence:

$$\log \left( \frac{da}{dN} \right) = \log C + m \log (\Delta K)$$

$$\frac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^m$$

**Step 3 — Integration:**

$$\int_{a_0}^{a_{cr}} \frac{da}{(\Delta K)^m} = C \int_{0}^{N_f} dN$$

$$N_f = \frac{1}{C} \int_{a_0}^{a_{cr}} \frac{da}{(Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi a})^m} = \frac{1}{C (Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi})^m} \int_{a_0}^{a_{cr}} a^{-m/2} \, da$$

For $m = 3$, $m/2 = 1.5$:

$$N_f = \frac{1}{C (Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi})^3} \left[ \frac{a^{-0.5}}{-0.5} \right]_{a_0}^{a_{cr}} = \frac{2}{C (Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi})^3} \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{a_0}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{a_{cr}}} \right)$$

where $a_{cr} = \dfrac{1}{\pi} \left( \dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y \sigma_{max}} \right)^2$.

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: $a_0 = 1$ mm $= 0.001$ m, $\Delta \sigma = 200$ MPa, $Y = 1.1$, $C = 5.0 \times 10^{-12}$, $m = 3$, $K_{IC} = 80$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$.

**Step 1 — Critical Crack Size:**

With $R = 0$, $\sigma_{max} = 200$ MPa:

$$a_{cr} = \frac{1}{\pi} \left( \frac{80}{1.1 \times 200} \right)^2 = \frac{1}{\pi} (0.3636)^2 = \frac{0.1322}{\pi} = 0.04208 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Integration Formula:**

$$N_f = \frac{2}{C (Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi})^3} \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{a_0}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{a_{cr}}} \right)$$

Compute each term:

$$\sqrt{a_0} = \sqrt{0.001} = 0.03162 \text{ m}^{0.5} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1/\sqrt{a_0} = 31.62 \text{ m}^{-0.5}$$

$$\sqrt{a_{cr}} = \sqrt{0.04208} = 0.2051 \text{ m}^{0.5} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1/\sqrt{a_{cr}} = 4.875 \text{ m}^{-0.5}$$

$$(Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi})^3 = (1.1 \times 200 \times 1.7725)^3 = (389.94)^3 = 5.93 \times 10^{7}$$

$$C \times (Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi})^3 = 5.0 \times 10^{-12} \times 5.93 \times 10^{7} = 2.965 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $N_f$:**

$$N_f = \frac{2 \times (31.62 - 4.875)}{2.965 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{2 \times 26.745}{2.965 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{53.49}{2.965 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{N_f \approx 1.804 \times 10^5 \text{ cycles}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Miner's rule for cumulative fatigue damage. State its assumptions and limitations in the context of ship structural analysis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship hull experiences the following stress spectrum over one voyage:

| Block | $\Delta \sigma$ (MPa) | $n_i$ (cycles) | $N_i$ (cycles to failure) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 120 | 50,000 | 800,000 |
| 2 | 90 | 200,000 | 5,000,000 |
| 3 | 60 | 1,000,000 | 50,000,000 |

Calculate the cumulative damage and comment on the design adequacy. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Concept (3 marks):** Miner's rule (Palmgren-Miner hypothesis) assumes that fatigue damage accumulates linearly with each applied stress cycle. If at stress range $\Delta \sigma_i$ the material can sustain $N_i$ cycles to failure, then $n_i$ applied cycles cause fractional damage $d_i = n_i / N_i$. Summing over all encountered stress blocks gives cumulative damage:

$$D = \sum_{i} \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$

Failure is predicted when $D = 1$.

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
- Damage is linear in cycles, i.e., no load interaction.
- The order of loading does not affect total damage.
- Material retains no "memory" of prior load history.
- Each cycle at a given stress range consumes a fixed fraction of life.

**Limitations in Ship Structural Analysis (2 marks):**
- **Non-conservative** under variable-amplitude spectra with occasional high peaks (overload retardation is ignored).
- **No sequence effect** — but real welded joints show crack-closure retardation after overloads.
- Cannot model **mean stress relaxation** under high cyclic plasticity.
- Classification societies (DNV, LR) therefore apply **design fatigue factors (DFF)** of 2–3 to life when Miner's rule is used as the basis.

**Model Solution (b):**

Compute fractional damage for each block:

$$D_1 = \frac{50{,}000}{800{,}000} = 0.0625$$

$$D_2 = \frac{200{,}000}{5{,}000{,}000} = 0.0400$$

$$D_3 = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{50{,}000{,}000} = 0.0200$$

Cumulative damage:

$$D = D_1 + D_2 + D_3 = 0.0625 + 0.0400 + 0.0200 = 0.1225$$

**Comment (1 mark):** Since $D = 0.1225 \ll 1.0$, the structure is well within safe limits for this voyage. Considering that the ship will perform $\sim 200$–300 such voyages over its 25-year design life, the projected long-term damage is:

$$D_{25yr} = 0.1225 \times 300 \approx 36.75 \gg 1.0$$

This indicates the **high-stress block (120 MPa)** dominates fatigue consumption, and the design must be revised to either reduce the stress range or apply a **design fatigue factor of 3** ($D \leq 1.0$ after multiplication by 3 implies allowable $D \leq 0.33$ per voyage). The current detail would require post-weld treatment (grinding, TIG dressing) or thickening.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in Module 4 Fatigue-Fracture Problems:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Using $a_0$ in mm and $K$ units with m. Convert everything to meters (or inches consistently) before substituting into the Paris formula.
> 2. **Wrong formula selection for $m$:** Students often write $N_f$ for the *general* case $m \neq 2$ and forget the special case at $m = 2$ gives a *logarithmic* integral, not power-law. Always check.
> 3. **Hot-spot formula coefficient:** Memorize **1.67 and 0.67** (NOT 1.5 and 0.5 — the latter is the basic linear extrapolation without the IIW correction).
> 4. **Miner's rule sign convention:** Damage is **sum of $n_i / N_i$**, NOT $N_i / n_i$. Reversing this gives life estimates 3–4 orders of magnitude wrong.
> 5. **Forgetting the safety factor:** KTU 2024 scheme valuation often expects the **Design Fatigue Factor (DFF)** of 2–3 explicitly mentioned in the conclusion.
> 6. **Crack location assumption:** The Paris law assumes a **central through-thickness crack** unless $Y(a)$ is explicitly given. Edge cracks have different $Y$ values.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Miner's Rule:** $D = \sum (n_i / N_i)$; failure at $D = 1$. Linear, non-conservative under variable amplitude.
- [ ] **Paris Law:** $da/dN = C (\Delta K)^m$ in stable Region II.
- [ ] **SIF Definition:** $K = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ — units MPa·m$^{1/2}$.
- [ ] **Critical Crack:** $a_{cr} = (1/\pi)(K_{IC}/Y\sigma_{max})^2$ from $K = K_{IC}$.
- [ ] **Integrated Life (m ≠ 2):** $N_f = [a_0^{1-m/2} - a_{cr}^{1-m/2}] / [C(Y\Delta\sigma\sqrt{\pi})^m (m/2 - 1)]$.
- [ ] **Hot-Spot Stress:** $\sigma_{HS} = 1.67\sigma_{0.5t} - 0.67\sigma_{1.5t}$ (IACS / DNV).
- [ ] **Mean Stress:** Goodman / Gerber / Soderberg corrections convert to equivalent fully-reversed amplitude.
- [ ] **S-N form:** $\sigma_a N^b = K'$ — slope $b \approx -0.1$ for steels, $-0.13$ for aluminum.
- [ ] **Design Fatigue Factor (DFF):** Apply 2–3 to life or divide stress by 1.25–1.5.
- [ ] **Mesh refinement:** Sub-model with element size $\leq 0.25 t$ at hot-spot; linear elements preferred over solid elements for shell weld modeling to avoid singular stress fields contaminating the result.
- [ ] **Spectrum methods:** Use Dirlik or Narrow-band for spectral fatigue when time history is unavailable.
- [ ] **Crack closure:** Real welded joints show Paris Region II growth rates $C$ that may be 3–10× lower than base metal.
- [ ] **Class society rules:** DNV-RP-C203, IACS S34, LR SSC, ABS Guide for Fatigue — all use S-N + Miner's rule as the baseline.
- [ ] **Units Checklist:** MPa–m–cycle–MPa·m$^{1/2}$ — never mix mm with MPa·m$^{1/2}$.
